author: curtislarson

title: Feature request: Web Locks API
body:
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Locks_API

> The Web Locks API allows scripts running in one tab or worker to asynchronously acquire a lock, hold it while work is performed, then release it. While held, no other script executing in the same origin can acquire the same lock, which allows a web app running in multiple tabs or workers to coordinate work and the use of resources.

I've been looking for some ways to manage access to a single resource, and this seems like it could fill quite a lot of the gap left by shared workers.

description = [[

}

local POST_FORM = { query = INTRO_QUERY }

Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10.15; rv:104.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/104.0"
]]

portrule = shortport.port_or_service({ 80, 443, 9000 }, { "http", "https" }, "tcp", "open")

local function merge_table(...)
	local all = {}
	for _, s in ipairs(...) do
		for k, v in pairs(s) do
			all[k] = v
		end
	end
	return all
end

action = function(host, port)
	local domain = host.targetname or host.name or host.ip
	local result = { count = 0, success_list = {} }
	local headers = { ["user-agent"] = USER_AGENT }
	local pipes, auth

	auth = stdnse.get_script_args("auth-token")
	if auth ~= nil then
		stdnse.debug("auth-token = %q", auth)
		headers["authorization"] = "Bearer " .. auth
	end

	auth = stdnse.get_script_args("auth-cookie")
	if auth ~= nil then
		stdnse.debug("auth-cookie = %q", auth)
		headers["cookie"] = auth
	end

	for _, path in ipairs(GENERAL_PATHS) do
		-- local uri = base .. path
		local query = url.build_query({ query = INTRO_QUERY })

		-- General GET request
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path .. "?" .. query, {
			header = headers
		}, pipes, "GET")

		-- GET request with base64 encoded
		query = url.build_query({ query = base64.enc(INTRO_QUERY) })
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path .. "?" .. query, {
			header = headers
		}, pipes, "GET")

		-- General POST form-data
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path, {
			header = merge_table(headers, {
				["content-type"] = "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
			}),
			content = url.build_query({ query = INTRO_QUERY }),
		}, pipes, "POST")

		-- General POST json
		pipes = http.pipeline_add(path, {
			header = merge_table(headers, {
				["content-type"] = "application/json",
			}),
			content = json.generate({ query = INTRO_QUERY }),
		}, pipes, "POST")
	end

	local responses = http.pipeline_go(domain, port.number, pipes)

	stdnse.debug("count = %d", #responses)

	for i, res in ipairs(responses) do
		stdnse.debug("status = %d", res.status)
		result.count = result.count + 1

		if res.status == 200 then
			table.insert(result.success_list, pipes[i].path)
		end
	end

	return { result, header = headers }
end

author: xbol0

title: serde_v8: StructAccess implements SeqAccess
body:
Is there a reason that `serde_v8` deserializes structs as a sequence of it's values?

That is to say `StructAccess` implements `serde::de::SeqAccess` instead of `serde::de::MapAccess`.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2fc88aba1ac69dd72e0d516de22157f72778b2ef/serde_v8/de.rs#L559

And as a consequence, later interpreting the struct as a sequence:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/2fc88aba1ac69dd72e0d516de22157f72778b2ef/serde_v8/de.rs#L373-L381

The implementation discards information about the key for each value, and I'm not sure whether this is an intentional design decision.

I would be happy to implement `serde::de::MapAccess` for `StructAccess` instead if this is the case.
author: Suficio

title: deno compile ignores custom ca-certificates on linux when downloading runtimes
body:
I am using the official `denoland/deno:alpine` docker image to build some cross-platform binaries.

The build must run behind a proxy that performs SSL re-encryption, and requires a custom cert to be trusted. I've added this cert to the docker image like so:

```Dockerfile
FROM denoland/deno:alpine-1.25.2

ADD .certs/dummy .certs/*.crt /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/
RUN chmod 644 /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/*.crt \
    && cat /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/*.crt \
    >> /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
    
...
```

I'm trying to run something like:

```sh
deno --unstable compile --cert="/etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt" -o build/mod-x86_64-apple-darwin \
        --target x86_64-apple-darwin \
        mod.ts
```

Without the `--cert` arg, the build fails much earlier when downloading imports. With the `--cert` arg, it successfully downloads and caches the imports, but fails to download the runtime binary.

```
Checking https://dl.deno.land/release/v1.25.2/deno-x86_64-apple-darwin.zip
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
error: error sending request for url (https://dl.deno.land/release/v1.25.2/deno-x86_64-apple-darwin.zip): error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer

Caused by:
    0: error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
    1: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
```

I've also tried using the following in my dockerfile, but got the same result as I did with `--cert`
```Dockerfile
ENV DENO_TLS_CA_STORE="system"
```

My suspicion is that the `--cert` arg as well as the `DENO_TLS_CA_STORE` env var both take effect for import resolution, but not for the download of the actual binary runtime when everything is bundled.

title: [Flash] Error thrown when "Deno.upgradeHttpRaw" used in the handler
body:
Using `Deno.upgradeHttpRaw()` API in a handler for `Deno.serve()` API causes an error and a panic:

```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'then')
    at Object.serve (deno:ext/flash/01_http.js:546:58)
    at async Promise.all (index 0)
    at async Object.serve (deno:ext/flash/01_http.js:621:5)

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.25.3
Args: ["../deno/target/debug/deno", "run", "-A", "--unstable", "npm:vite"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', ext/flash/lib.rs:302:37
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

The culprit is this line:
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/d7b27ed63bf74c19b0bc961a52665960c199c53a/ext/flash/01_http.js#L544

This is because when using `Deno.upgradeHttpRaw()` we're expecting that there will be no response returned (ie. `resp` is undefined). I believe the server should just go to handle next request and not do anything for given request.
author: bartlomieju

title: Deno can't handle .JS source map
body:
The code:

```ts
const a = (
  it: [
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string,
    string
  ]
) => it;

throw new Error("abc");
```

Is compiled with `tsc --inlineSourceMap main2.ts` to:

```js
var a = function (it) { return it; };
throw new Error("abc");
//# sourceMappingURL=data:application/json;base64,eyJ2ZXJzaW9uIjozLCJmaWxlIjoibWFpbjIuanMiLCJzb3VyY2VSb290IjoiIiwic291cmNlcyI6WyJtYWluMi50cyJdLCJuYW1lcyI6W10sIm1hcHBpbmdzIjoiQUFBQSxJQUFNLENBQUMsR0FBRyxVQUNSLEVBbUJDLElBQ0UsT0FBQSxFQUFFLEVBQUYsQ0FBRSxDQUFDO0FBRVIsTUFBTSxJQUFJLEtBQUssQ0FBQyxLQUFLLENBQUMsQ0FBQyJ9
```

It works on node:

```
$ node --enable-source-maps main2.js
/.../main2.ts:24
throw new Error("abc");
      ^

Error: abc
    at Object.<anonymous> (/.../main2.ts:24:7)
    at Module._compile (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1085:14)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:1114:10)
    at Module.load (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:950:32)
    at Function.Module._load (internal/modules/cjs/loader.js:790:12)
    at Function.executeUserEntryPoint [as runMain] (internal/modules/run_main.js:75:12)
    at internal/main/run_main_module.js:17:47
``` 

But not on Deno:
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.25.3 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.6.194.5
typescript 4.7.4

$deno run main2.js
error: Uncaught Error: abc
Warning Couldn't format source line: Line 24 is out of bounds (source may have changed at runtime)
    at file:///.../main2.js:24:7
```

I also tested `tsc --inlineSourceMap --inlineSources main2.ts`, to the same effect.

Would it possible for Deno to handle arbitrary JS source maps? For example, from CoffeeScript?

author: steida

title: perf: ideas to improve startup time
body:
### Results (after applying below optimizations)

1.5x improvement

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34997667/190909956-9eb5e2a1-ad22-4e05-af74-63178bc1a4ae.png)

`runjs` is a barebones rusty_v8 CLI for baseline.
`target/release/deno` is Deno from https://github.com/littledivy/deno/tree/startup_time
`deno` is Deno 1.25.3
`node` is Node.js 18.8.0

### Potential optimizations

Deno `main`:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34997667/190911427-63cb49c7-11ce-43c8-b501-2a9abc1b477d.png)

- [x] Don't `clap::App::clone`. (minor) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15951
- [x] Compile with `opt-level = 3`. (major) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15952
- [ ] Do not run the `Drop` handler for CliMainWorker. `drop_in_place::<CliMainWorker>`. (major) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15953
- [ ] Move internal JS off of V8 heap. Replace `v8::String::new` with `v8::String::new_external_onebyte_static`. (minor)
- [ ] Lazy creation of `deno_fetch::create_http_client`. (major)
- [ ] OnceCell::initialize + `LZ4_decompress_safe` (major)
- [ ] ParsedSourceCache initialization (sqlite connection to cache store) is expensive. (major)
- [x] Avoid canoncalization of file paths in ConfigFile::discover_from if file does not exist. (major) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15957
- [x] Use single ObjectTemplate for ops in `initialize_ops` (minor) https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15959

macOS:

- [ ] `dyld` (major)

Dependency on CoreGraphics & Metal slows down dyld. These come from webgpu, ideally we should lazy load them.

Without webgpu:
```
../deno/target/release/deno:
	/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreFoundation.framework/Versions/A/CoreFoundation (compatibility version 150.0.0, current version 1858.112.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/CoreServices (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1141.1.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/Security.framework/Versions/A/Security (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 60158.100.133)
	/usr/lib/libiconv.2.dylib (compatibility version 7.0.0, current version 7.0.0)
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1311.100.3)
	/usr/lib/libresolv.9.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1.0.0)
```

With webgpu:
```
/Users/divy/.deno/bin/deno:
	/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreFoundation.framework/Versions/A/CoreFoundation (compatibility version 150.0.0, current version 1853.0.0)
	/usr/lib/libobjc.A.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 228.0.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/CoreServices (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1141.1.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/QuartzCore.framework/Versions/A/QuartzCore (compatibility version 1.2.0, current version 1.11.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/Metal.framework/Versions/A/Metal (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 257.24.0)
	/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1311.0.0)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreGraphics.framework/Versions/A/CoreGraphics (compatibility version 64.0.0, current version 1557.1.1)
	/System/Library/Frameworks/Security.framework/Versions/A/Security (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 60157.30.13)
	/usr/lib/libiconv.2.dylib (compatibility version 7.0.0, current version 7.0.0)
	/usr/lib/libresolv.9.dylib (compatibility version 1.0.0, current version 1.0.0)
```

Additionally we depend on `Security.framework` for hyper-tls integration. This could also be lazy loaded in some way.

After applying all of that:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34997667/190911704-6f6febe8-5995-4af8-9d96-d2342f58890b.png)

There are maybe some optimizations that can be made in `deno_graph::crate_graph` for the main module.

Rest of the time is spent in:
- `MainWorker::execute_script`
- `v8::Context::FromSnapshot`
- `v8::Isolate::InitWithSnapshot`

See https://github.com/littledivy/deno/tree/startup_time

author: bjesuiter

title: npm: Import map is not supported for "require()"
body:
We have support for import map in case of ESM imports of npm: specifiers, but it's not supported if a module is `require`d. 

This might be a bit exotic, but I believe there are certain valid use cases for it.

Eg. I'm trying to run a Vite project, I did set up following import map:
```json
{
    "imports": {
        "@vitejs/plugin-vue": "npm:@vitejs/plugin-vue@3.0.3",
        "vite-plugin-deno": "./plugin.js",
        "vite": "npm:vite@3.0.7",
        "vue": "npm:vue@3.2.37"
    }
}
```

This works nicely, as long as there's a `package.json` file in the same directory like so:
```json
{
  "name": "my-vue-app",
  "private": true,
  "version": "0.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "vite",
    "build": "vite build",
    "preview": "vite preview"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "vue": "^3.2.37"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@vitejs/plugin-vue": "^3.0.3",
    "vite": "^3.0.7"
  }
}
```

However as soon as I remove that `package.json` file, I'm no longer able to run `deno run -A --unstable npm:vite`, because I get following error:
```
failed to load config from /Users/ib/dev/my-vue-app/vite.config.js
error when starting dev server:
Error: Cannot find module 'vite'
Require stack:
- /Users/ib/dev/my-vue-app/vite.config.js
- /Users/ib/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vite/3.1.2/dist/node/chunks/dep-cff57044.js
    at Function.Module._resolveFilename (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:615:17)
    at Function.Module._load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:447:29)
    at Module.require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:658:21)
    at require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:789:18)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///Users/ib/dev/my-vue-app/vite.config.js:31:19)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///Users/ib/dev/my-vue-app/vite.config.js:56:4)
    at Module._compile (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:719:36)
    at Object._require.extensions.<computed> [as .js] (file:///Users/ib/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vite/3.1.2/dist/node/chunks/dep-cff57044.js:63489:24)
    at Module.load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:636:34)
    at Function.Module._load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:493:14)
```

If `require()` took into account import map file, just like ESM imports do, then we could remove `package.json` from the project altogether and instead replace it with `deno.json` + `import_map.json`.
author: bartlomieju

title: lsp: allow "lint-only" mode
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
It's no secret that deno_lint is fast. Like, much, [much faster than eslint](https://github.com/denoland/deno_lint#performance). As such (and as the [deno_lint roadmap](https://github.com/denoland/deno_lint/issues/176) heads toward eslint plugin compatibility and autofix), deno will see an increasing number of users and companies adopt deno lint for their linting solution, whether they use node, deno, or any other runtime. 

Obviously, running lint in CI is nice, but most users want LSP integration, so that they can see lint issues as they write code.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
If this extension/lsp were to enable users to run the deno lsp _purely_ for linting, **without** disabling normal typescript language features, and without enabling _any_ other deno lsp features, we could have a hybrid world where users who use node, bun, etc, could use deno lint to great success.

This would increase adoption and awareness of deno tooling, and make other engineers happy.

Essentially, I wish the deno vscode extension would let us do `"deno.enable": false, "deno.lint": true`. In this sense, we get the linting features of the deno lsp without turning off visual studio code typescript language features.

Please consider this request! Let me know if I can provide any further information.

title: Ignored "experimentalDecorators" compiler option
body:
The [Configuration File](https://deno.land/manual@v1.25.3/getting_started/configuration_file#json-schema) docs link to the following JSON schema file: https://deno.land/x/deno/cli/schemas/config-file.v1.json

Disabling the `experimentalDecorators` compiler option seems to have no effect? Is there no way to disable this behavior?
```js
// deno.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "experimentalDecorators": false // default: true
  }
}
```

It actually warns about the `experimentalDecorators` option being ignored:
```
$ deno run --config deno.json main.ts
Unsupported compiler options in "file:///path/to/deno.json".
  The following options were ignored:
    experimentalDecorators
```

title: Cached warning messages cause confusion in editors
body:
When importing the standard library without specifying a version, there are two warnings:
1. Implicitly using latest version (\<version\>) for \<url\>;
2. The import of "\<url\>" was redirected to "\<new-url\>".

But it shows wrong redirect and wrong latest version (older ones).
You could see on screenshots, that version offered by Deno is `0.151.0`; but the real redirect refers to version `0.156.0`:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/32432324/191333419-6483c111-cb91-42a1-9f75-62d78e479296.png)

Quick fix "Update specifier to its redirected specifier" updates the link to the outdated version.

Expected behavior:
When no version is defined, show real redirects in hints; follow real redirects on "quick fix".

author: jonasfrey

title: feature request: add `--allow-sys-info` permission flag
body:
The following APIs currently require `--allow-env` permission without argument.

- loadavg
- osRelease
- hostname
- networkInterfaces
- systemMemoryInfo
- getGid
- getUid

When a user wants to use any of these, they need to pass `env` permission, but that also allows access to the all env variables. This situation doesn't make much sense because system info and env vars are independent aspects of the system.

If we introduce a new permission flag `--allow-sys-info` to control the access to these system info, then user can control them independently to the access to env vars.

```sh
deno run --allow-sys-info script.ts
# => allows access to all sys info
```

```sh
deno run --allow-sys-info=hostname script.ts
# => allows access to only hostname
```

---
This idea is from this comment https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15932#discussion_r973602140

title: Runtime API to check whether in self-contained exe
body:
I couldn't find a runtime API to check whether the current code is running inside a self-contained exe.

This would make it easy to distinguish between the "production" (compiled) version and testing via `deno run`.

I was thinking about something like `Deno.standalone` or maybe something more descriptive like `Deno.selfContainedExe`.

title: lsp: "Add all missing imports" VSCode quick fix produces invalid import specifiers
body:
Running "Add all missing imports" causes the `.ts` extension to be appended before the end of the actual filename in some conditions (possibly due to multiple `-` in filename, or the fact that the filenames share a common substring).

E.g it inserts `./duck-config.ts-options` instead of `./duck-config-options.ts`

Repro repo with full explanation: https://github.com/curtislarson/deno-add-all-missing-imports-repro

title: [feat] permission file
body:
### Scenario
```sh
$ deno test ${pem_hello} ./hello.ts
$ deno test ${pem_world} ./world.ts
# WILD PERM
$ deno test -A
$ deno run ${perm_hello} ./hello.ts
$ deno run ${perm_hello} ./world.ts
$ deno compile ${perm_hello} ./hello.ts
$ deno compile ${perm_world} ./world.ts
```

### Feat
deno.jsonc
```jsonc
{
  "perm": Perm | Path | WhatwgImportId
}
```

```typescript
type Perm = Record<PermId, DenoPerm>
type PermId = ImportMapId | PlaceHolderImportId | WhatwgImportId;
```

```sh
$ deno test
$ deno run ./hello.ts
$ deno run ./world.ts
$ deno compile ./hello.ts
$ deno compile ./world.ts
```

title: Add example for excluding vendored dependency from `deno coverage`
body:
The manual page for testing coverage mentioned that there are default inclusions/exclusions and that if you add either of those flags it would override those defaults. However it doesn't show examples of how you can extend it to exclude directories or files.

https://deno.land/manual@v1.25.4/testing/coverage

I tried updating my coverage command to keep the default exclusion but also exclude my vendor and public directories but it doesn't appear to work.

```sh
deno coverage --exclude="(test\.(ts|tsx|mts|js|mjs|jsx|cjs|cts)|^vendor/.*)" --lcov cov > cov.lcov
```

When I use the codecov action to try uploading the cov.lcov, it ends up still having coverage information for my vendor directory. My dependencies are a lot larger than my actual project, so it completely throws off the overall code coverage percentage for my project.

I tried updating my codecov.yml to exclude it but that didn't work. I based it on oak's codecov.yml file then updated the ignore based on the codecov ignoring paths documentation.

https://docs.codecov.com/docs/ignoring-paths

```yml
comment: false
codecov:
  require_ci_to_pass: true
coverage:
  status:
    project:
      default:
        informational: true
ignore:
  - "vendor/**/*"
```

author: Daiki48

title: deno vendor import map should keep the entries of the existing import map
body:
The `import_map.json` file generated by `deno vendor` doesn't contain the imports of the existing `import_map.json`. This makes script to fail because these import entries are missing.

As an example, Lume uses the following import map:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "lume/": "https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.11.4/"
  }
}
```

To vendor a Lume project, the script take 2 lume files and the _config.ts file:

```bash
deno vendor \
https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.11.4/task.ts \
https://deno.land/x/lume@v1.11.4/cli.ts \
./_config.ts
```

The `vendor/import_map.json` file doesn't include the `lume/` import, so it fails.

Lume has a `lume vendor` command to run `deno vendor` and fix the new `import_map.json` file by adding automatically the missing imports (https://github.com/lumeland/lume/blob/f9866692391ed4d7500494b9d2b29de0921f9f26/cli/vendor.ts#L55-L63) but I think this is something that Deno should do.

author: dsherret

title: Fast streams
body:
_This issue acts as a design doc for a set of performance optimizations and clean-ups that can be performed in Deno to make piping data between consumers and producers of data significantly faster than we have previously been able to, while preserving the pristine APIs we expose to users for streaming data._

### Introducing streams

_Streams_ represent a combination of a single data _source_ and a data _sink_. Streams are abstract - they do not exist as objects in JS. Streams are a logical "pipe" that is able to transfer data between between a _source_ and a _sink_. On these streams the action of _streaming_ is performed. _Streaming_ is the operation of transferring data from the _source_ to the _sink_.

Sources are the producing end of a stream. They are most often represented as `ReadableStream` objects in JS. There are two types of sources: JS backed, and resource backed. JavaScript backed sources are streams that are constructed manually in JS, where the chunks sent on the stream stem from user code. Resource backed sources are producers backed by a Rust side resource. A `ReadableStream` representing file handle contents (`FsFile#readable`) is such a 

- `fsFile.readable` (a source representing data read from a file handle)
- `req.body` (a source representing the data received in an HTTP server request)
- `resp.body` (a source representing the data received as the response to an outbound `fetch` request)

Sinks are consuming end of a stream. In JS they are sometimes represented as `WritableStream`, but more often than not as an abstract resource that consumes data. There are once again two types of sinks: JS backed, and resource backed. JS backed sinks read chunks from the stream in JS. Resource backed sinks consume streams in Rust - they usually send this data to some IO sink, like a network socket or open file.

To make streams optimally fast, we need to optimize them specifically for the pair of source and sink they consist of. For example, a stream with a Rust backed sink & source, can perform the entire stream in Rust - no copies to JavaScript are necessary. If the reverse is true (both sides of the stream are JS backed), no copies to Rust are necessary.

Some sinks are also special: they want to read _all_ data from the stream into a single combined chunk for example. In this case if the source can provide all data in a single operation more efficiently than streaming into chunks, cpu cycles and thus time can be saved resulting faster streams.

### Streaming operation

To optimize streams what we really need to do is optimize the streaming operation. This means that every place we "perform a streaming operation" we need to decide if we can perform an optimized operation.

More concretely the baseline operation we are trying to optimize is the following:

1. get a reader from the `ReadableStream` (source)
2. while the reader has more chunks:
  1. read a chunk from the reader
  2. emit the chunk into the underlying sink
3. unlock the stream by closing the reader

This operation exists in various places. Some examples:

- `ReadableStream#pipeTo(writable)`
- `ReadableStream#pipeThrough(transformer)`
- `req.body` reading in `fetch`
- `resp.body` reading in HTTP server

Currently all of the above implementations of the operation (except for the HTTP server case) are completely un-optimized, and are implemented in pure JS. This means that if a stream consists of a rust backed sink and source (e.g file upload via fetch), all data needs to be copied once from Rust into JS, and then from JS back into Rust.

### Optimizations

This is exactly where the core of the optimization lies. We can significantly improve performance by skipping the step where we copy data from / to JS.

We have some practical experience with the performance improvements this unlocks in the HTTP server. We have implemented an optimization there that allows some Rust backed sources to be directly used as a HTTP response, without having to get copied through JS. This significantly improves throughput.

### The implementation

Deno uses WHATWG streams as the primitive for sources and sinks. Our implementation work will focus exclusively on these (`ReadableStream` and `WritableStream`). These stream primitives have the benefit that they are able to be locked. This allows us to take full ownership of the underlying source / sink, allowing us to move the ownership from JS to Rust. This enables all following optimizations.

To make the optimization work, we need to be able to effectively identify which sources and sinks are resource backed, and can have their streaming operation offloaded to Rust. To do this we need to brand all JS objects that represent a source or sink that is backed by a resource. Luckily this is easy, as the API for all source and sink resources is the same: it is the `deno_core::Resource` read & write API. To brand a source or sink object thus, we only need to attach a hidden `[rid]` property to it. This property can then hold the resource ID backing the source or sink.

For sources, this branding is already implemented for many streams through the `readableStreamForRid` helper function in `__bootstrap.streams.readableStreamForRid`. We need to implement a similar helper function for `WritableStream` sinks, probably called `writableStreamForRid`. A non branding version of this helper already exists in `ext/net`.

We then need to update all code paths that perform a streaming operation to go into a special case path if both source and sink are resource backed. The most generic case of this is `ReadableStream#pipeTo` and `ReadableStream#pipeThrough`. The latter is similar to the former, except that it has to make two connections rather than just one (src -> transformer sink & transfomer src -> sink). For these generic cases a new op would be introduced (`op_pipe`) that takes a source and sink resource and performs a simple streaming operation in Rust. More specialized logic and op integration is required when piping the `req.body` into `fetch`, or `resp.body` into a HTTP response.

Finally, more specialized streaming-like code paths can be optimized. A good example of this is the "read all" operation that is performed on `req.body` and `resp.body` when `req.arrayBuffer()` and `resp.arrayBuffer()` are called. Here we can add an op that performs the entire read operation in Rust (`op_read_all`), only passes a single aggregated chunk to JavaScript, rather than aggregating chunks manually in JS. @marcosc90 came up with this optimization in #16038. There are likely other specialized streaming-like operations that could use similar optimizations. 

- Branding sources
  - [x] `FsFile#readable`
  - [x] `Deno.stdin#readable`
  - [x] `Deno.Process#stdout#readable`
  - [x] `Deno.Process#stderr#readable`
  - [ ] `Deno.Child#stdout` (difficult, because unrefable)
  - [ ] `Deno.Child#stderr` (difficult, because unrefable)
  - [x] `Conn#readable`
  - [x] `resp.body` returned from `Cache#match`
  - [ ] `req.body` in `Deno.serveHttp`
  - [x] `req.body` in `Deno.serve`
  - [x] `resp.body` returned from `fetch`
  - [ ] `CompressionStream#readable`
  - [ ] `WebSocketStream#readable`
  - [ ] `TextEncoderStream#readable`
- Branding sinks
  - [ ] `FsFile#writable`
  - [ ] `Deno.stdout`
  - [ ] `Deno.stderr`
  - [ ] `Deno.Process#stdin#writable`
  - [ ] `Deno.Child#stdin`
  - [ ] `Conn#writable`
  - [ ] `CompressionStream#writable`
  - [ ] `WebSocketStream#writable`
  - [ ] `TextDecoderStream#writable`
- Optimize streaming operations
  - [x] `resp.body` consumption in `Deno.serveHttp`
  - [x] `resp.body` consumption in `Deno.serve`
  - [ ] `resp.body` consumption in `cache.put`
  - [ ] `req.body` consumption in `fetch`
  - [ ] `ReadableStream#pipeTo`
  - [ ] `ReadableStream#pipeThrough`
- Optimize streaming-like operations
  - [x] Collecting a `req.body` or `resp.body` (`req.arrayBuffer()` for example)

title: Local node_modules folder should use hard links instead of copying from global cache when on the same partition
body: As a first pass, we are copying files from the global cache to the local node_modules folder, but when the two locations are on the same partition, we could hard link them instead.

title: Deno doesn't support executable compressors such as UPX.
body:
I tried to compress a 106M hello world with UPX and then the resulting binary enters directly into the REPL.

Instructions to reproduce it on Ubuntu:

`sudo apt install upx-ucl`
`echo 'console.log("Hello");' > test.ts`
`deno compile test.ts`
`upx -9 test`

title: [feat] configuration preset
body:
### Related
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/11836
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/11889
https://www.npmjs.com/package/@tsconfig/strictest

### Feat
deno.jsonc
```jsonc
"compilerOptions": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/@tsconfig/node16-strictest-esm@1.0.2/tsconfig.json"
```

Or some builtin preset config
```jsonc
"compilerOptions": "denoland-strictest"
```

Or import map id
```jsonc
"compilerOptions": "npm:@tsconfig/node16-strictest-esm@1.0.2/tsconfig.json"
```

title: command for denofy npm
body:
I suggess to add command, that take 2 args: 
paths to nodejs project, path to the output, the out will be some transformation for nodejs code to use npm specifer for example 

convert 
```
const express = require('express');
```

to 

```
// just add npm: 
const express = require('npm:express')

```

let's name this command denofy.
this command may be added to deno cli or in std/node.
use cases:
- it will be to exprement/test den-npm support in some existing nodejs project without manualy change the code
- when deno-npm support more stable, it will be easy to migrate project from nodejs to deno
notes:
nodejs project means js project written to be runned using nodejs

console.log(Deno.hostname())

author: jcs224

title: [idea] compress dependency
body:
I think, `pnpm` partial solved and `yarn berry` solved the old joke on nodejs, about `the heaviest objects in the universe` by hard link to global cache, or compress dependency.

And `yarn berry`'s answer is incredible neat, and takes very incredible small disk space
- compress into zip
- runtime require/loader hook to enter zip content
- provide command to inject sdk to let different editor recognize zip

Inspired by that, I suggest maybe deno can do something similar adapting yarn berry existing codebase logic.
author: loynoir

title: Get working directory where `deno task` is called in
body:
[From npm v8 `npm-run-script` documentation](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/commands/npm-run-script):

> Scripts are run from the root of the package folder, regardless of what the current working directory is when `npm run` is called. If you want your script to use different behavior based on what subdirectory you're in, **you can use the `INIT_CWD` environment variable, which holds the full path you were in when you ran `npm run`.**
author: xnuk

title: Private registry / Configure npm registry for module download
body:
I would like to be able to use a custom, self-hosted npm registry.
Especially for resolving company-local private packages based on _scopes_ this would be very helpful.
As far as I can see, currently only the npm public registry (https://registry.npmjs.org/) can be used.

Deno should support something similar to npm's configuration:
```
npm config set registry ...
npm config set @mycompany:registry ...
```

/tmp/path/to/javascript $ echo 'console.log("deno compile test");' > app.js

deno compile test

%file:///tmp/path/to/javascript/app.js
{"argv":[],"unstable":false,"seed":null,"permissions":{"allow_env":null,"allow_hrtime":false,"allow_net":null,"allow_ffi":null,"allow_read":null,"allow_run":null,"allow_sys":null,"allow_write":null,"prompt":true},"location":null,"v8_flags":[],"log_level":null,"ca_stores":null,"ca_data":null,"unsafely_ignore_certificate_errors":null,"maybe_import_map":null,"entrypoint":"file:///tmp/path/to/javascript/app.js"}d3n0l4nd

Is there `deno compile` option to not include `.js` file's **absolute** path in compiled executable?

title: `deno vendor` does not honor import mapped jsxImportSource
body:
`deno vendor` does not honor jsxImportSource. It should automatically vendor code defined by `jsxImportSource`

 minimal repro at https://github.com/sylc/repro_issue_deno_vendor_jsxImportSource

Note:
The current work around to get it vendored correctly is to explicitly call, in the program code, the jsx source, for example adding
```
import { h } from "preact/jsx-runtime";
```

v8 10.7.193.3
typescript 4.8.3
```

author: loynoir

title: Failed to resolve JSON files when using npm specifiers
body:
Deno version: 1.26.0

```shellsession
$ deno run -A --unstable npm:xo --help
error: Expected ';', '}' or <eof> at file:///home/codespace/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/globals/13.17.0/globals.json:2:12
```

According to [the README](https://github.com/sindresorhus/globals#readme), the `globals` package contains a plain JSON file and is used by ESLint. The issue seems to be related to JSON resolution (it seems like the resolver treats the JSON as a ES module), but it's just my best guess.

The `xo` package has a lot of dependencies so it's definitely not a minimal reproduction. Pardon me for not having a better one.
author: kidonng

title: Integrity check with data uri dynamic imports support
body:
According to Deno's [documentation](https://deno.land/manual@v1.26.0/linking_to_external_code/integrity_checking#runtime-verification):
> Like caching above, you can also use the --lock=lock.json option during use of the deno run sub command, validating the integrity of any locked modules during the run. Remember that this only validates against dependencies previously added to the lock.json file. **New dependencies will be cached but not validated.**

However this seems to be outdated/not true as it fails when using dynamic imports:
```
error: The source code is invalid, as it does not match the expected hash in the lock file.
  Specifier: data:text/javascript,REDACTED
  Lock file: lock.json
```

I would expect to have no validation on specifier not included in the lock file.

title: lsp: cached diagnostics display, even when file is opened in editor
body:
**Describe the bug**

**To Reproduce**

No clear reproduction path. This is in a large private code base. Warning shows whenever I first open the file and goes away after I make any change near the lines affected. For example if I add a new line, it will fix it, and it will stay fixed if I remove the new line, returning it to how it was originally but without any warning.

**Expected behavior**

There should be no warning.

**Screenshots**

In this example, I show one of these false warnings. The test before it correctly recognizes the type of this. In the video I update the test with the warning to have a new line and the warning disappears. If I remove the new line, the error doesn't return. There are 2 tests in this file where this warning happens. Both warnings resolve when the first one resolves, even though I don't touch the second spot where this warning occurs in the file. There are many similar tests that have no warnings.

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3241653/193434797-1912e75e-bd44-4582-9d8e-703704a1a2ac.mp4

**Versions**

vscode: 1.71.2 deno: 1.25.4 extension: v3.13.1

body:
It'd be great if `deno fmt` formatted code within JSDoc code blocks in scripts. This'd come in handy when writing examples, etc. For example, @crowlKats is currently reorganizing and cleaning up documentation of `deno_std` in denoland/deno_std#2658. This issue continues the discussion in denoland/deno_std#2736.

title: Support for install scripts (ex. "postinstall") for "npm:" specifiers with global node modules
body:
> i'm wondering if maybe we should run post install scripts with a prompt when a user does --node-modules-dir . So the registry cache will always be pure, but the local node_modules won't necessarily be
> Edit: the reason for this only being done in local node_modules dir is that the user's global cache might get into an inconsistent state if they choose not to run or to run a post install script

^^ by @dsherret 

We should consider adding support for "postinstall" scripts, but instead of running them manually users would be prompted to confirm they indeed want postinstall script to run. In such case, the script would have permissions passed on the CLI applied with missing permissions being prompted for.

title: suggestion: import.meta extends, importer and main module meta datas
body:
Can it be useful to add importer informations on import.meta for better logs (and errors) ? For instance, a multiple times imported module can log contextualized messages (eg.: [in version checker to indicate which module require a specific version](https://github.com/JOTSR/ensure_version/blob/275d8f1213bc6b3f8b85f5a267d814a4f5014b09/ensure_version.ts#L123) ).

```ts
import.meta.importer = {
    url: 'importer/url',
    main: true
}
```

Maybe it can be recursive to get imports chain for some usages

```ts
import.meta.importer = {
   url: '...',
   main: ... ,
   importer: { ... }
}
```

For main module, importer should be set to ```null```
Maybe Deno.mainModule should be moved (or duplicate since import.meta is not accessible outside of modules, eg: REPL) to

```ts
import.meta.mainModule = {
   url: '...',
   main: true,
   importer: null //maybe undefined or refers to itself
}
```

author: bogdankol

title: npm - Cache slimmed down version of registry.json
body:
Right now we cache the entire npm request response, but these files can be fairly big (ex. 6mb for @types/node - https://registry.npmjs.org/@types/node) and there's a lot of information in there that's not useful at all. We should consider slimming it down a bit and remove certain properties that we're positive we'll never use (ex. "contributors").

Alternatively, we could save this file in our own format with a schema version, then cache bust when we the user upgrades their CLI and we need more information. This would probably be simpler.

author: jmoalves

title: Overriding `Promise.prototype.then` affects runtime
body:
~~`SafePromise` is exported by primordials, but functions that return native `Promise` such as `PromisePrototypeThen` are used in the internal code, so it is possible to interrupt any process by overriding `Promise.prototype.then` as follows:~~

```js
const originalThen = Promise.prototype.then;
Promise.prototype.then = function (...args) { console.log("bang!"); return originalThen.call(this, ...args) };
```

~~This probably happens because the `await` operator executes the `"then"` method of the received "thenable" regardless of whether it is a Promise or not. To solve this, I believe we need to make one of the following choices:~~

* ~~Do not use the `await` operator~~
* ~~Provide wrappers for all `Promise` methods by primordials so that they are always wrapped in `SafePromise`~~

----

After reading the spec in detail, it appears that the problem is not caused by the `await` operator, but by calling the `"then"` method directly. I will try to fix the problem and submit a PR.
author: petamoriken

title: Overriding `Promise.prototype.then` affects runtime
body:
~~`SafePromise` is exported by primordials, but functions that return native `Promise` such as `PromisePrototypeThen` are used in the internal code, so it is possible to interrupt any process by overriding `Promise.prototype.then` as follows:~~

```js

Promise.prototype.then = function (...args) { console.log("bang!"); return originalThen.call(this, ...args) };
```

~~This probably happens because the `await` operator executes the `"then"` method of the received "thenable" regardless of whether it is a Promise or not. To solve this, I believe we need to make one of the following choices:~~

After reading the spec in detail, it appears that the problem is not caused by the `await` operator, but by calling the `"then"` method directly. I will try to fix the problem and submit a PR.

title: lsp: Adding version to URL with VS Code suggestion removes final part of URL
body:
**Describe the bug**

Inserting a version to an imported URL with VS Code auto-completion results in everything after the version of the URL being removed.

**To Reproduce**

1. Add an `import` with implicit version e.g. `import { createClient } from 'https://deno.land/x/edgedb/mod.ts';`
2. Type an '@' after the module name, in this case `edgedb@` and wait for suggestions to pop up
3. Select a version from the suggestions, e.g. `v0.22.8`.

**Expected behavior**

It should result in 

`import { createClient } from 'https://deno.land/x/edgedb@v0.22.8/mod.ts';` 

but the result is

`import { createClient } from 'https://deno.land/x/edgedb@v0.22.8';`

with `/mod.ts` missing, and making the URL invalid.

title: Optimize `ReadableStream` backed by non-BYOB readables
body:
#16115 changed `deno_core::Resource` to have seperate read methods for BYOB optimized resources (files & sockets) and non-BYOB optimized resources (HTTP steams).

Currently the only way to read data from Rust -> JS is via the `op_read` op. This op is BYOB, and can incur an extra data copy & allocation for non-BYOB ops. I propose we rename `op_read` to `op_read_byob` and add a new `op_read_buf` that is not BYOB. We can then add a second `readableStreamForRid` function for non-BYOB optimized resources. These `ReadableStream`'s can use `op_read_buf` internally to avoid a bunch of small data copies, maybe even some data copies.
author: lucacasonato

title: error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Unsupported scheme "node" for module "node:XXX"
body:
**Single step to reproduce**

Run this command (revised command courtesy of AnInternetTroll):
```bash
deno eval --unstable 'import "npm:assemblyscript/asc"'
```

Error output:
```
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Unsupported scheme "node" for module "node:module". Supported schemes: [
    "data",
    "blob",
    "file",
    "http",
    "https",
]
    at async file:///home/codespace/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/assemblyscript/0.21.6/dist/asc.js:7:6221
```

This is because the module in question does `import 'module'` which I believe Deno translates to `import 'node:module'` ("module" being an actual Node module- confusing haha).

I've tried this in Github Codespaces (`deno 1.26.1+a622c5d (canary, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)`)
author: Kycermann

title: bug: `console.log` does not print all elements of sparse arrays
body:
It seems that as soon as a sparse array contains more than a single consecutive hole, all subsequent values and holes are omitted.

```sh
$ deno
Deno 1.26.1
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
> [1, 2, 3, , 5]
[ 1, 2, 3, <1 empty item>, 5 ]
> [1, 2, 3, , , 6]
[ 1, 2, 3, <2 empty items> ]
> console.log(_)
[ 1, 2, 3, <2 empty items> ]
undefined
> 
```

This not only happens in the repl but also when doing `console.log` from a script.
author: KnorpelSenf

title: Optimize internal JS->Rust writes using `op_write_all`
body:
Currently we use `op_write` to write data from JS to Rust. In many cases this write is wrapped in some sort of `writeAll` helper function. This function exists to ensure that the entire chunk is written to the resource, instead of just a partial. This can incur multiple Rust->JS round trips for a single chunk. I propose we add a `op_write_all` that can flush an entire chunk entirely to a resource using the new `Resource::write_all` function on resources. This can also help for resources that can avoid extraneous allocations or copies when flushing an entire chunk at once.

We can automatically use this `op_write_all` in all `WritableStream` resources, such as the ones created by `writableStreamForRid`.

title: lsp: JSON Module "has no default export" incorrectly reported when specifier does not end in `.json`
body: **Describe the bug** 1. Install deno extension for vscode, enable for project. 2. Create a `repro.ts` file with the following contents: ```ts import repro from "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/denoland/deno/adec4f575eb19a48cefe451ce3962a6a4754e3ba/cli/tests/testdata/npm/registry/jsonfile/registry.json?1" assert { "type": "json" }; console.log(repro); ``` Note a new vscode "Problem" with the text: `Module '"https://raw.githubusercontent.com/denoland/deno/adec4f575eb19a48cefe451ce3962a6a4754e3ba/cli/tests/testdata/npm/registry/jsonfile/registry.json?1"' has no default export.` **Expected behavior** It's not actually problem. It only presents a problem in vscode. It runs fine in `deno run`, `deno repl` etc It _appears_ to be a problem only when the import specifier does not end in `.json`. As far as I know, this is not a requirement in the json modules spec. **Screenshots** **Versions** vscode: 1.72.0 deno: 1.26.1 extension: v3.13.1
author: johnspurlock

title: Investigate `IO_URING` support
body:
I'm opening this issue to investigate the opportunity of implementing `IO_URING` in Deno. If you haven't heard about it `IO_URING` is a new revolutionary API for doing asynchronous operations in Linux with a much [higher throughput,](https://www.phoronix.com/news/Linux-5.6-IO-uring-Tests) much [lower tail latencies](https://ci.spdk.io/download/performance-reports/SPDK_nvme_bdev_perf_report_2205.pdf) and [CPU usage](https://twitter.com/axboe/status/1554115250588471297).

While investigating how to implement `IO_URING` for node, I came up with an approach which I think can make this very simple: Instead of implementing directly `liburing` via NAPI, I used a thin layer to expose [syscalls](https://docs.rs/linux/0.0.1/linux/syscall/index.html) to a WASM module, to then do the heavy lifting in WASM. More details [here](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/34388)

This not only greatly simplify the approach, but maximize reusability across WASM compatible runtimes. Also I think this kind of taks is a perfect fit for Rust, with its safety guarantees and speed.

Unfortunately I don't know much about Deno internals, but here maybe the Node and Deno community could come together to write a common WASM module?

author: gr2m

title: `Deno.serveHttp` always panics under certain conditions
body:
Snippet:

```ts
const port = 12345;

const listener = Deno.listen({ port });

const [serverConn, clientConn] = await Promise.all([
  listener.accept(), 
  Deno.connect({ port }),
]);

const buf = new Uint8Array(128);
const readPromise = serverConn.read(buf);
const http = Deno.serveHttp(serverConn);
```

Running the above code with `deno run --allow-net snippet.ts` always gives us panic.

```
❯ deno run --allow-net servehttp_panic.ts

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.26.1
Args: ["deno", "run", "--allow-net", "servehttp_panic.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'Only a single use of this resource should happen: FullDuplexResource { rd: AsyncRefCell<tokio::net::tcp::split_owned::OwnedReadHalf>, wr: AsyncRefCell<tokio::net::tcp::split_owned::OwnedWriteHalf>, cancel_handle: CancelHandle { node: Node { inner: UnsafeCell { .. }, _pin: PhantomPinned } } }', runtime/ops/http.rs:48:8
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```
author: magurotuna

title: `deno compile` panics when the target file name begins with `@`
body:
`deno compile` command panics if either of the following conditions is met.

- target file name begins with `@`
- stem part of the target file name is equal to one of ["main", "index", "mod", "index"] && the parent directory name starts with `@`

```shell
❯ deno compile @abc.ts
Compile file:///private/tmp/denotest/@abc.ts
Emit

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.26.1
Args: ["deno", "compile", "@abc.ts"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'called `Option::unwrap()` on a `None` value', cli/tools/standalone.rs:211:45
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

title: How to import a node module that depends on a module with a path of ".."?
body:
Hi I am new to deno, I am trying to import a node module to use snowflake, I am using deno 1.26.1.

I tried using esm.sh:
```
import { Snowflake } from 'https://esm.sh/snowflake-promise'//;
```
I get the following error after a deno run:
```
error: Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'prototype')
    at https://esm.sh/v96/readable-stream@3.6.0/deno/lib/_stream_duplex.js:2:1360
    at https://esm.sh/v96/readable-stream@3.6.0/deno/lib/_stream_duplex.js:2:810
    at https://esm.sh/v96/readable-stream@3.6.0/deno/lib/_stream_duplex.js:2:2549
```
I tried skypack:
```
import snowflakePromise from 'https://cdn.skypack.dev/snowflake-promise@4.5.0';
```
I get the following error after a deno run:
```
error: Relative import path ".." not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
    at https://cdn.skypack.dev/-/winston-transport@v4.4.0-BtLGcov7GHKlyCf57AV4/dist=es2019,mode=imports/unoptimized/legacy.js:3:29
```
`deno vendor` did not change the error.
I looked at the file skypack is complaining about and It looks like it does not like this line:
```
import TransportStream from "..";
```
Is there anything I can do about it? Can I create an import map to tell deno the correct path? Thanks in advance.

title: npm: Prisma CLI can only execute he first command you try on Windows (in cmd/PowerShell)
body:
To reproduce:
1. Install deno, make sure `deno` works in `cmd` or Powershell
2. Run a `prisma` command via Deno, .e.g. `deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma@4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1`
3. Run another Prisma CLI command via Deno, e.g. `deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma@4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1 init` 
4. Observe error message: 
   ```
   Error: Can't write to C:\Users\Jan\AppData\Local\deno\npm\registry.npmjs.org\@prisma\engines\4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1 please make sure you install "prisma" with the right permissions.
   ```

You can then reset the state by deleting the global deno npm folder (for me `C:\Users\Jan\AppData\Local\deno\npm\registry.npmjs.org`). When you then run the second command directly, it succeeds. If you run the first command afterwards though, it will also fail with the same error message.

This does not happen when using the deno CLI via WSL/Ubuntu.

---

```
PS C:\Users\Jan\Documents\throwaway\denoTest> deno --version
deno 1.26.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.7.193.3
typescript 4.8.3
```

Full output:
```
PS C:\Users\Jan\Documents\throwaway\denoTest> deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma@4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1

Prisma is a modern DB toolkit to query, migrate and model your database (https://prisma.io)

Usage

  $ prisma [command]

Commands

            init   Set up Prisma for your app
        generate   Generate artifacts (e.g. Prisma Client)
              db   Manage your database schema and lifecycle
         migrate   Migrate your database
          studio   Browse your data with Prisma Studio
          format   Format your schema

Flags

     --preview-feature   Run Preview Prisma commands

Examples

  Set up a new Prisma project
  $ prisma init

  Generate artifacts (e.g. Prisma Client)
  $ prisma generate

  Browse your data
  $ prisma studio

  Create migrations from your Prisma schema, apply them to the database, generate artifacts (e.g. Prisma Client)
  $ prisma migrate dev

  Pull the schema from an existing database, updating the Prisma schema
  $ prisma db pull

  Push the Prisma schema state to the database
  $ prisma db push

PS C:\Users\Jan\Documents\throwaway\denoTest> deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma@4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1 init
Error: Can't write to C:\Users\Jan\AppData\Local\deno\npm\registry.npmjs.org\@prisma\engines\4.4.0-integration-feat-deno-deploy.1 please make sure you install "prisma" with the right permissions.
```

author: nicolasgere

title: feature request: Deno.access API
body:
We should have `Deno.access` API for better polyfill the `fs.access` and `fs.accessSync` APIs in `std/node`.

Currently we polyfill `fs.access` API in a very hacky way using `Deno.lstat` and `Deno.getUid` (ref: https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/deef340b5557f65c5f4de52f5a66a68496db2bb0/node/_fs/_fs_access.ts#L23-L30), and the solution doesn't work on windows if the file only has the owner permissions. This causes the issue #16255 )

If we have implemented `Deno.access` in a similar way as `fs.access` of Node, we can shim it much better (and can solve the issue on windows)

related #16255

title: Deno bench is flaky due to usage of same HTTP ports
body:
CI bench run may flake out from using the same HTTP ports for different tests. At least port 4544 seems to be used for these two tests:
1. `cli/bench/http/deno_http_flash_ops_spawn.js `
2. `core/examples/http_bench_json_ops.rs`

Failed actions include at least:
* https://github.com/denoland/deno/actions/runs/3248010142/jobs/5328727229
* https://github.com/denoland/deno/actions/runs/3244266366/jobs/5320207896
* https://github.com/denoland/deno/actions/runs/3248913923/jobs/5331028242
* https://github.com/denoland/deno/actions/runs/3251439769/jobs/5336428668

Additionally: When bench fails like this it dumps out the entire `deno bundle` output to the log which is blatantly unhelpful and makes the log too large for GitHub log browser. Only viewing the raw logs makes it possible to find the actual error.

author: nestarz

title: Unable to run `rollup` as `rollup.config.js` doesn't resolve
body:
# Background
I'm working through the list of popular npm dependencies and trying to get each of their unit tests working. As part of that, I'm trying to get [mocha tests to run in deno using the steps here](https://github.com/mochajs/mocha/blob/master/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md). They're currently failing though, as I can't run rollup to build `mocha.js` for testing.

# Env
```bash
% deno --version
deno 1.26.1 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.7.193.3
typescript 4.8.3
```

# Steps to reproduce
1. Clone [mocha](https://github.com/mochajs/mocha)

```bash
git clone https://github.com/mochajs/mocha.git
cd mocha
```

2. Inspect the `package-scripts.js` to see how the build works

```js
// package-scripts.js
module.exports = {
  scripts: {
    build: {
      script: `rollup -c ./rollup.config.js`,
      description: 'Build browser bundle'
    },
```

3. Try to run `rollup`

```bash
deno run  -A --unstable npm:rollup -c ./rollup.config.js
```

4. See the error:

```bash
% deno run  -A --unstable npm:rollup -c ./rollup.config.js                           
[!] TypeError: Could not resolve 'file:///Users/mark/sandbox/mocha/rollup.config.js' from 'file:///Users/mark/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/rollup/3.1.0/dist/shared/loadConfigFile.js'.
TypeError: Could not resolve 'file:///Users/mark/sandbox/mocha/rollup.config.js' from 'file:///Users/mark/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/rollup/3.1.0/dist/shared/loadConfigFile.js'.

author: janpio

title: Measure runtime code coverage caused by running Postman/newman tests
body:
First off great job on Deno so far - kudos to the team! 🎉🎉🎉 

I would like to measure code coverage of a Deno program (an HTTP server) exercised by running Postman tests against it (via the `newman` CLI runner).

In Node.js I would achieve this by running `nyc node server.js`, and then running my Postman tests against the server that was started.

How can I do something similiar in Deno? Maybe something like this:

```bash
deno run --coverage ./main.ts &
newman ...          # Run Postman tests using newman CLI
kill $SERVER_PID    # Kill deno process to dump coverage numbers
```

Or, even better, everything in one command to start server, run external tests, and end server:

```bash
deno run --coverage-command 'newman ...' ./main.ts
```
author: anishkny

title: error: missing field headers in $DENO_DIR/deps/**/*.metadata.json
body:
`deno cache` throws error
when `.headers` field is missing in `$DENO_DIR/deps/**/*.metadata.json`

## Reproduce

remove the `.headers` field from `$DENO_DIR/deps/**/*.metadata.json`

```sh
#! /bin/sh
# repro.sh
# error: missing field headers in $DENO_DIR/deps/**/*.metadata.json
# https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16295

set -e
#set -x

gitdir=$(mktemp -d)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/hayd/deno-udd $gitdir

mainScript=$gitdir/main.ts

export DENO_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
echo "DENO_DIR: $DENO_DIR"
mkdir -p $DENO_DIR

alias deno=./target/debug/deno

# note: $DENO_DIR/lock.json is a non-standard location
deno cache --lock=$DENO_DIR/lock.json --lock-write ${mainScript}

# make deterministic DENO_DIR
find $DENO_DIR/deps -name '*.metadata.json' |
while read j
do
  # error: missing field `headers` at line 3 column 1
  cat $j | jq 'del(.headers) | del(.now)' >$j.new
  # workaround:
  #cat $j | jq '.headers={} | del(.now)' >$j.new
  mv $j.new $j
done
rm $DENO_DIR/dep_analysis_cache_v1



# rebuild cache
deno cache --lock=$DENO_DIR/lock.json ${mainScript} || true



echo "hit enter to cleanup tempdirs: rm -rf $DENO_DIR $gitdir"
read

rm -rf $DENO_DIR $gitdir
```

this will throw

```
...
Download https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/path/win32.ts
Download https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/path/_constants.ts
Download https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/path/_util.ts

error: missing field `headers` at line 3 column 1
    at file:///build/source/deps.ts:2:28
```

the stack trace is not helpful
which suggests that this is an internal error with deno

i tried to get a better stack trace, but no luck

```sh
# debug
export RUST_BACKTRACE=full
#export RUST_BACKTRACE=1
```

<details>

### deps.ts:2:28

https://github.com/hayd/deno-udd/blob/d96d0cbda7c81cb28286c41cbbe8cd05e0a81771/deps.ts#L2

```ts
export { expandGlob } from "https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/mod.ts";
                            ^ 2:28
```

https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/mod.ts
https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/main/fs/mod.ts

### deno-udd/main.ts

`expandGlob` is used here

https://github.com/hayd/deno-udd/blob/master/main.ts

```ts
  const depFiles: string[] = [];
  for (const arg of a._.map((x) => x.toString())) {
    for await (const file of expandGlob(arg)) {
      depFiles.push(file.path);
    }
  }
```

</details>

## Environment

```
deno --version

deno 1.25.4 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.7.193.3
typescript 4.7.4
```
author: milahu

title: deno cache: add option: deterministic
body:
avoid the "make deterministic DENO_DIR" workaround in #16295

let me call

```sh
deno cache --deterministic --lock=lock.json --lock-write main.ts
```

to produce `$DENO_DIR/deps/**/*.metadata.json` files
without the `.headers` and `.now` fields

### why

this is required for reproducible builds, for example in [nix](https://github.com/NixOS/nix)

### before

`$DENO_DIR/deps/https/deno.land/f217b5b64ccb9121ba749b9e41148ccdca07318a526b0539c749fa466f1b56ac.metadata.json`

```json
{
  "headers": {
    "x-cache": "Hit from cloudfront",
    "vary": "Accept-Encoding, Origin",
    "cache-control": "public, max-age=31536000, immutable",
    "x-amz-replication-status": "FAILED",
    "accept-ranges": "bytes",
    "content-type": "application/typescript; charset=utf-8",
    "x-amz-cf-id": "dXY1GkcxYcngHrSfIFlyI8NC9dy3HDNwxkdz7eA10WqjutIE7wVWUQ==",
    "server": "deno/europe-southwest1-a",
    "via": "1.1 0645caa18be2b3be5e4612d40f45a0ba.cloudfront.net (CloudFront)",
    "last-modified": "Thu, 28 Jul 2022 16:20:41 GMT",
    "content-security-policy": "default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; sandbox",
    "x-amz-version-id": "4EvD4VwJyuOgLwRa5J3QpMSoHzjhRkqP",
    "etag": "\"205cedd724537b322334156e0564c7fb\"",
    "age": "707767",
    "x-amz-cf-pop": "MAD50-C1",
    "content-length": "712",
    "access-control-allow-origin": "*",
    "date": "Fri, 07 Oct 2022 10:42:43 GMT"
  },
  "url": "https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/eol.ts",

    "secs_since_epoch": 1665847129,
    "nanos_since_epoch": 684138025

```

### after

`$DENO_DIR/deps/https/deno.land/f217b5b64ccb9121ba749b9e41148ccdca07318a526b0539c749fa466f1b56ac.metadata.json`

```json
{
  "url": "https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/eol.ts"
}
```

or

```json
"https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/eol.ts"
```

or just store the raw url in `*.metadata.json` (or in `*.url.txt`)

```
https://deno.land/std@0.150.0/fs/eol.ts
```
author: milahu

title: Feature: Support SIGHUP and SIGBREAK (port libuv support?)
body:
As deno ramps up to become compatible with node, is there an effort for porting libuv wrapper for console signals from libuv to a rust library compatible with Tokio? Specifically, SIGHUP and SIGBREAK - specifically src/win/signalc?

https://github.com/microsoft/playwright/issues/3146

Thanks for any input on effort towards this. libuv doesn't have to be the source. And this may be a small step towards getting libraries like puppeteer and playwright to work. And other libraries, like https://github.com/atroche/rust-headless-chrome, may supersede the need for these two.

Or possible compatibly on how libuv and tokio setup websockets.

There are two ways for playwright and puppeteer to communicate with chrome/edge. websockets or signals & streams.

Thank you!
-TamusJRoyce

title: A few test cases fail if the project directory is located in a deep place
body:
The following two test cases fail if they are located in a deeply nested place.

- integration::run::queue_microtask_error_handled
- integration::run::report_error_handled

Output:

```
OUTPUT
1
{
  cancelable: true,
  message: "Uncaught Error: foo",
  filename: "file:///Users/yusuktan/Repos/github.com/magurotuna/deno/cli/tests/testdata/run/report_error_handled....",
  lineno: 18,
  colno: 13,
  error: Error: foo
    at file:///Users/yusuktan/Repos/github.com/magurotuna/deno/cli/tests/testdata/run/report_error_handled.ts:18:13
}
onerror() called Error: foo
    at file:///Users/yusuktan/Repos/github.com/magurotuna/deno/cli/tests/testdata/run/report_error_handled.ts:18:13
2

OUTPUT
EXPECTED
1
{
  cancelable: true,
  message: "Uncaught Error: foo",
  filename: "[WILDCARD]/report_error_handled.ts",
  lineno: 18,
  colno: 13,
  error: Error: foo
    at [WILDCARD]/report_error_handled.ts:18:13
}
onerror() called Error: foo
    at [WILDCARD]/report_error_handled.ts:18:13
2

EXPECTED
```

Seems like the "filename" field is different between the expected and actual one. The actual output shows the "omitted" file path (notice that the string ends with the ellipsis), probably because I put the project in a deep place.
This test failure doesn't cause any problem on CI, but it would be even better if we could successfully run it regardless of each individual's setup.

title: Feature Request: Support Web Media Capture and Streams API
body:
I can see there should  have many use cases will be opened if this Web API is usable on the server side
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Media_Capture_and_Streams_API
https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/MediaDevices/getUserMedia

E.g. Use cases:
OpenCV like Real-time image recognition, face recognition on streaming video from webcam on server side.....
Training AI model using WebGPU........
Real-time QR code recognition
May be use with std/collections API to analyze the large 2D array????

Must ask for camara/mic permission before Deno can access them.
Deno can open a small window on server side along with the original CLI to displaying the video stream to user like this.

![opencv_tutorial_header](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46224228/196053157-1b38de45-2bee-4275-a4b3-a1d487942006.jpg)

What I mentioned above are just some very basic ideas, not standard descriptions, and do not need to be used as a necessary reference.

Currently, I am not aware of any other back-end JavaScript/Typescript runtime environment (Node.js or Bun) that implements this Web API, and if Deno does it first, it will be a revolutionary move.

This is very encouraging.

title: `Deno.Reader` faster than `ReadableStream`
author: iuioiua

title: Tracking issue for WebSocket performance
body:
Let's look at a simple ping/pong benchmark. 

```js
Deno.serve(req => {
  const { socket, response } = Deno.upgradeWebSocket(req);
  socket.onmessage = (e) => {
    socket.send(e.data);
  }
  return response;
}, { port: 8000 });
```

```
# Deno 1.26.1
Msg/sec: 74114.500000

# uWebSocket.js
Msg/sec: 259207.750000
```

The goal should be to have as little overhead as possible and be on par with uWebSocket.js and tokio-tungstenite. We should also integrate this test using [`uWebSockets/benchmarks/load_test.c`](https://github.com/uNetworking/uWebSockets/blob/45e9ca2372a3758ece3384ac52797da3a3c8fa48/benchmarks/load_test.c) in our CI workflow.

After applying the below changes, I see a **~3.5x improvement** in the above benchmark:
```
# Deno (with below changes)
Msg/sec: 258291.750000
```

- [ ] Optimize `socket[_idleTimeoutDuration]` logic. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16317
- [x] Do not create bunch of Error objects during timer cancellation. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16316
- [x] Make `op_cancel_handle` use fast calls. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16318
- [ ] Use `core.byteLength` instead of doing full utf8 string encoding. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16320
- [ ] Fast path for sending `string` and `binary` values. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16320
- [ ] `op_try_send_[type]` fast call to try to send values in one go without creating a future. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16320
- [ ] Optimize `op_ws_next_event`: 
  - [ ] Make it `deferred`. We don't want to poll unless there is an event. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16325
  - [ ] Since it's deferred, pushing the future onto the event loop should be a fast call. This takes up 10% of the time.
     - [ ] Do not progress deferred ops immediately. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16361
     - [ ] fast calls.
  - [ ] It should return value ID. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16325
  - [ ] Fast path to get values from ID. `NextEventResponse` is slow. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16325
- [ ] `AsyncRefCell` try_borrow_mut before `borrow_mut().await`. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16320
- [x] Optimize Event and MessageEvent:
  - [x] event timestamp does not have to be perfect.
  - [x] `ReflectDefineProperty` causes deopt. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16365
  - [x] Fast path for internal message dispatch to avoid webidl and `ReflectHas` checks. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16365
- [x] Optimize `op_queue_microtask`. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16359
- [x] Do not create a new Uint8Array for `serde_v8::StringOrBuffer`. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16360

A very sloppy implementation of the above can be found here: https://github.com/denoland/deno/compare/main...littledivy:deno:websocket_perf

title: REPL throws parse error when breaking code line at `.`
body:
This code

```
const plantarray = (await Promise.all((plants.map(p => getplantcharacteristics(p)))))
        .map(a => a.filter(a => {return desiredattributes.includes(a.PlantCharacteristicName)}))
        .map(f => f.filter(a => a.CultivarName === null));
```
and this code
```
const plantarray = (await Promise.all((plants.map(p => getplantcharacteristics(p))))).
map(a => a.filter(a => {return desiredattributes.includes(a.PlantCharacteristicName)})).
map(f => f.filter(a => a.CultivarName === null));

```
causes parse error: Unexpected token `.`.... when running in deno repl.
Works when file is executed with deno run 

Works in REPL as single line: (no breaks)

`const plantarray = (await Promise.all((plants.map(p => getplantcharacteristics(p))))).map(a => a.filter(a => {return desiredattributes.includes(a.PlantCharacteristicName)})).map(f => f.filter(a => a.CultivarName === null));
`

deno 1.26.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.7.193.3
typescript 4.8.3

title: Error outputs `errors` property
body:
If the `Error` object has the `errors` property as an array and is thrown, it will output `errors` in the error message.

```ts
const e = Error()
e.errors = ["xxx"].
throw e
```

out:

```ts
error: Uncaught Error
    xxx
const e = Error()
          ^
```

This seems to have occurred since v1.21.0.
Probably related PR #14285

title: Dislocated FFI calls
body:
Sorry I couldn't come up with a better title. The whole issue is quite confusing.
In summary: in the latest Deno 1.26.2, under Ubuntu 20.04, loading SQlite library (.so) and calling its functions via FFI **seems to call something else**.

Test code:

```
import { Database } from "https://deno.land/x/sqlite3@0.5.2/mod.ts";

const db = new Database(":memory:");
const ver = db.prepare("select sqlite_version()");
const [version] = ver.get();
console.log(Deno.version.deno, version);
```

Which I run like this: `DENO_SQLITE_PATH=/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libsqlite3.so.0 ./deno --unstable run --allow-ffi --allow-env=DENO_SQLITE_PATH ./wat.js`
Previously, it produced the following output:
`1.26.0 3.31.1`
Now, it produces this:
`1.26.2 3.39.2`
I have SQLite 3.31.1 .so installed in Ubuntu where I run the file in question.
Where does it take SQLite 3.39.2 from?

Running `strace ... 2>&1 | grep -E '^open(at)?\(.*\.so'` with the two versions of Deno produces the same output, except the newer one has one extra line:

```
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/etc/ld.so.cache", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libdl.so.2", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libgcc_s.so.1", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
+ openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/librt.so.1", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libpthread.so.0", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libm.so.6", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 3
  openat(AT_FDCWD, "/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libsqlite3.so.0", O_RDONLY|O_CLOEXEC) = 12
```

But that's librt, and libsqlite3 is loaded from the same location

If I compile the latest SQLite myself (`gcc -shared -fPIC sqlite3.c -o libsqlite3.so`) and place it into the same directory with the .js file, running it in Deno 1.26.0 produces the expected output: `1.26.0 3.39.4`. Deno 1.26.2 crashes.

Hypothesis: Deno uses SQLite internally. Can these internal functions come into some weird interaction with the functions from the loaded library?

title: Check write permissions at start of `deno upgrade`
body:
Personally: I keep forgetting that I need to run `deno upgrade` with `sudo` due to having installed Deno with a package manager. This leads to me downloading deno twice every time.

It would be nice if the `deno upgrade` command could check write permissions before it downloads the new version, and exit if the installation is obviously incapable of passing.

title: lsp: should work with local node_modules directory
body:
From https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16332

> LSP doesn't work with local node_modules directory since there's no configuration for this at the moment. I think we should consider making this an option in the deno.json file or perhaps it should detect if there's a node_modules folder and prefer to use that?

When we add suport for this we'll need to ensure it initializes the npm snapshot from the lockfile to ensure it resolves the same as on the command line (otherwise there will be a lot of churn in the node_modules folder).
author: dsherret

title: Consider renaming `deno check --remote` to `--all`
body:
From https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16332

> The `--remote` flag in `deno check --remote main.ts` should probably be renamed `deno check --all main.ts` to align with `deno run --check=all main.ts`. `deno run --check`/`deno check` doesn't type check npm packages by default in these changes and `--remote` doesn't really make sense for that given we have a distinction between "remote" and npm packages elsewhere (ex. `--no-remote` and `--no-npm`)

title: Expose the import-map URL under `import.meta`
body:
Related to #12575

### Summary
If a user specifies an import-map in their Deno config, Deno should/could expose the URL globally. In my opinion, it should go under `import.meta`. It seems that the ECMAScript committee purposely left `import.meta` extensible [^1].

#### Use cases
- Bundling code
- Transforming Deno code for browser-use
- Updating dependencies

### Notes
Assuming the property is read-only, it might be easier for everyone to expose the import-map's object itself. This would mean less parsing. But it should be read-only, to adhere to the specification.

I did see [the WICG's note](https://github.com/WICG/import-maps#programmatic-api) about a programmatic API. The discussions seem to center around browser-usage. The most relevant open issue (https://github.com/WICG/import-maps/issues/128) focuses on exposing the entire import-map as an object (not just the URL), and allowing modifications (not read-only).

[^1]: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-import-meta#will-this-object-be-locked-down-in-any-way

title: `deno repl` hangs without network access
body:
Running the repl in 1.25.4 and 1.26.2 in a debian:11 container hangs when there's no network access. 

There are connection attempts to deno.land / tcp / 443, that seem to do a retry backoff which takes a couple of minutes

```
$ date; docker run -ti me/deno /deno repl --eval 'console.log(new Date())'
Wed 26 Oct 2022 01:45:56 UTC

2022-10-26T01:48:07.230Z
Deno 1.25.4
exit using ctrl+d or close()
> 
```

I think it might be related to the connections being dropped rather than getting an ICMP response. 

The `--no-remote` options don't seem to affect whatever is loading.

Could the remote lookups be disabled optionally? Is there a way to pre cache the remote lookup?
