author: jespertheend

title: napi_get_typedarray_info takes wrong arguments
body:
NAPI has landed! This is so awesome! Congratulations on getting this out, I know @littledivy and @bartlomieju put a ton of effort into this! It is really looking nice!

Ok, there are some bugs, of course :). The most definite and pronounced one I noticed is that the arguments for `napi_get_typedarray_info` are wrong. I think it was implemented roughly the same as `napi_get_buffer_info`, but it actually takes a completely different set of (and far more) arguments:
https://nodejs.org/dist/latest-v18.x/docs/api/n-api.html#napi_get_typedarray_info
And it is important to note that almost all the arguments to `napi_get_typedarray_info` are supposed to be optional; supplying a null to any of them should be properly handled and should just not attempt to output any data to the reference. I believe that may also be an issue with `napi_get_buffer_info`/`napi_get_arraybuffer_info` (should be able to supply null for the length reference).

There also seems to be an additional issue with `napi_get_buffer_info` (which maybe afflicts `napi_get_typedarray_info` too, but hard to know if it is wrong with the wrong args) in that I don't think it is returning the address of the view's offset, it just returns the address of the underlying `ArrayBuffer`. That is, if the buffer's view of the `ArrayBuffer` has a `byteOffset` of 1024, it should return an address/pointer that is 1024 greater than the address of the underlying `ArrayBuffer`'s address.

title: Harden update checker version matching
body:
It appears some folks hit an issue with latest version propagation on our CDN, which might have caused a prompt showing to upgrade from "1.27.0" to "1.26.2". 

We should harden the upgrade checker to only show prompt if the latest available version is actually newer than the currently running version.
author: bartlomieju

title: Upgrade Checker should not change output on `deno run`
body:
Would it be possible to not have the upgrade checker write to output on `deno run`? This is an undesirable behavior in all cases when (1) deno is running in a container, or (2) when the deno version is being managed globally on a shared server. If there is no user to see a prompt - automation tasks - or if the user can't change the version themselves, this feature is at best useless, and possibly just annoying. At worst, it could change output in a tested environment in a way that breaks something.

This feature is changing process output on code that has been tested based on external changes outside the control of the developer. 

`deno check`, `deno lint`, `deno compile`, a dozen others - all fine for changing the output. `deno run` needs to produce the same output when given the same input. Every time. 

Put another way, this feature has the capacity to allow a bug to slip through CI/CD, since it guarantees a process output change at some point in the future, but is not opt-in (strictly opt out at this point). DENO_NO_CHECK_UPDATE=1 needs to be set in every docker definition or for every script. If I release code without it (let's say I forget), my code will get through CI/CD just fine and fail in production next week when a new patch release drops because something changed in the process output.

Put another way, arbitrarily altering the process output on a fixed version of Deno running fixed versions of code just seems like a bad idea to me. If Deno has not changed and code has not changed, I would expect the program to do *exactly* the same thing - forever. This feature seems to me to break that contract.

--- 

I am not sure if I can test with this behavior in place. There isn't a way to force Deno to output this message, so I have to wait for the next update. I am assuming that this is writing to `stderr`. For scripts that just use `stderr` as a garbage dump that is fine and probably won't cause too much of a problem (unless the script is being called a gazillion times - it happens). But `stderr` can't be assumed to be used that way in all cases. If it matters and I want to leave the default behavior in place, I need to test with different versions of Deno to verify that things still work (once 1.27.1 drops). So I am stuck with setting an environment variable everywhere, because I am not going to test like that. 

I hope there is a solution to this, or an evolution of the feature, or something that makes this more tidy.

---

Would it be possible to give this explicit command line support? Not just an environment variable.

title: lsp: Inlay hints option `suppressWhenArgumentMatchesName` meaning flipped
body:
Deno version: 1.27.0
OS: Windows 10

With this VS Code configuration:

```json
{
    "deno.enable": true,
    "deno.inlayHints.parameterNames.enabled": "all",
    "deno.inlayHints.parameterNames.suppressWhenArgumentMatchesName": true
}
```

VS Code displays:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/507710/198653849-d450801d-8523-4cff-996a-26d1e150d3fd.png)


But with the configuration:
```json
{
    "deno.enable": true,
    "deno.inlayHints.parameterNames.enabled": "all",
    "deno.inlayHints.parameterNames.suppressWhenArgumentMatchesName": false
}
```

It displays:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/507710/198654351-c4594b0a-b8ee-4901-8423-9dd2078f8415.png)

I expect that when `suppressWhenArgumentMatchesName` is true that inlay hints when names match are suppressed, not the other way around.

I can't tell if this is a `vscode_deno` issue or an LSP issue, but since the related commit in the VS Code extension doesn't seem to do much except add preset entries, I'm posting it here.

author: Im-Beast

title: Add support for Priority Hints
body:
The Priority Hints spec is in the process of being merged into the fetch and HTML specs and allows for site developers to hint at the relative fetch priority for various resources.

The main changes are:
* Adds a "priority" to fetch's Request and RequestInit classes that uses a string enum of "auto", "high" or "low"
* Adds a "fetchPriority" attribute to the IMG, PICTURE, SCRIPT, LINK and IFRAME html elements with the same enum values

Existing spec: https://wicg.github.io/priority-hints/

This was shipped in Chromium 103 and Mozilla is working on an implementation so it is now in the process of being moved into the HTML and fetch specs.

For deno, the main use case is on the fetch interface to pass through prioritization to the underlying networking stack.
author: pmeenan

title: Bug: Web workers appear to reuse same module cache
body:
This appears to be a regression (was previously reported in #6116). My understanding is that each web worker is supposed to its own separate module cache that is separate from the one used for the main thread and those used for other workers.

**Web Worker References**
- [Deno Manual](https://deno.land/manual@v1.27.0/runtime/workers)
- [MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Web_Workers_API)

## Environment

OS: Windows 11 Home Version 22H2

Observed in Deno v1.27.0

## Steps to reproduce

I initially observed this in a more complicated example and provide that context if desired, but for simplicity sake I reusing the steps from #6116 as I believe they illustrate the same issue and is much simpler.

1. Create a file `script.ts` with the content `console.log('hello')`.

2. Create another file `main.ts` with the following content:

```ts
while(true) {
  const worker = new Worker(new URL("./script.ts", import.meta.url).href, { type: "module"});
  await new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 1000))
  worker.terminate()
}
```

3. Run this command: `deno run --allow-read main.ts`.

4. Modify the content of `script.ts` to `console.log('bye')` and save it.

## Expected behavior

The subsequent log of `deno run --allow-read main.ts` should be `bye`.

## Observed behavior

After step 4, the subsequent log of `deno run --allow-read main.ts` is still `hello`.

## Notes

Testing with JS files yielded the same result. I also tried using `self.close` in the worker rather than `worker.terminate` and again got the same result.

title: WebSocket messages are not being sent.
body:
I did some more testing on f5cb26a82fc74749c03c7c91a80de8df05d7a2e1 and unfortunately it appears like some messages are not being sent somehow. The reason why I believe this is the case is that when switching between `1.26.2` and f5cb26a82fc74749c03c7c91a80de8df05d7a2e1 a bug is introduced that causes players to not respawn. They keep running around while the player model is still rendered in the 'dead' state. So my guess is that the server isn't sending the 'respawn' message for some reason.
I haven't changed any of my code which leads me to believe something in Deno has changed that causes the messages to not get sent. I've also observed other instances that seem to suggest messages are not being sent, like certain in game events not happening for some of the players.

I'll try to make a reduced test case, but I'm worried that similar to #16450 this is something that can only be reproduced with a lot of traffic from different clients.

author: dsherret

title: Import/Cache non-ts files...
body:
Just thinking it could be useful to be able to do something like...

```typescript
import localFilePath from "cache+https://server/path/to/file.ext";
```

Where the file will be downloaded to the local cache directory, and the import statement will in effect have the local path to that cached file.  This could be useful for being able to download/store/cache wasm or dynamic libraries.

Not sure about build outputs, or other features... just thought it would be useful for creating localized bundles, or container images with the resource in place.  Also, not sure about the protocol itself, I know that npm is using an `npm;` protocol... again, just wanting to maybe get a discussion started.

If this if it's a dupe, feel free to reference any existing issue and close, I did a cursory search but didn't find one.

---

Edit: Thinking, may need to be somehow deterministic on usage of a platform/architecture specific instance... The usage above, definitely good for wasm, and if one doesn't mind pulling multiple versions of a library.  In the case of a .so/.dll/.dylib for linux/windows/mac would be potentially excessive... that said, still better than no solution at all.

```
cache[,platform[,arch]]+url

cache,linux,x86_64+https://some.server/path/to/file.so
```

If no match on the cache params for platform/arch, the value will be `undefined` ... this way the references for all can be imported, but only the actual one necessary will be loaded.
author: tracker1

title: "read line" echo server possibly about 1/3 the performance of node
body:
The best documentation I could find indicates this is the way to read lines from a socket stream and echo with deno

```
    const read = conn.readable
      .pipeThrough(new TextDecoderStream())
      .pipeThrough(new TextLineStream())

    for await (const line of read) {
      conn.write(new TextEncoder().encode(line + "\n"))
    }
```

This seems to be the popular approach for node 
```
    let rl = readline.createInterface({ input: socket })
    for await (const line of rl) {
      socket.write(line + "\n")
    }
```

I wrote a client in go to benchmark this.  I'm getting about 1/3 the performance of node.  Am I doing this wrong or are there optimizations to be made?

Everything is here

https://github.com/kswope/node-deno-echo-benchmark

author: ht990332

title: Signal handling not working when installed by Chocolatey
body:
**This is not a Deno issue.**
However, Chocolatey is listed as an installation method in the README, so it is registered as an issue.

When Deno is installed by Chocolatey in Windows, long processing cannot be performed after a SIGINT interrupt.

The current Chocolatey installation method uses the [shimgen][] wrapper.
Shimgen kills the child process immediately it receives the signal.

Fixing this issue requires either:
- Fix shimgen
  - This issue hasn't been resolved since 2016.
    chocolatey/home#134
- Change installation package
  - I made a PR for the change.
    digitalcoyote/chocolatey-packages#42

[shimgen]: https://docs.chocolatey.org/en-us/features/shim

author: bjesuiter

title: Exposing internal APIs in runtime.
body:
Has there been any consideration into allowing access to some useful internals from js?

I'm thinking specifically:
- [`tools::fmt::format_file`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1410e4adea5d399f9551ff04c6863862f169ee7a/cli/tools/fmt.rs#L251) for formatting at runtime without requiring run permission,
- [`tools::lint::lint_file`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/1410e4adea5d399f9551ff04c6863862f169ee7a/cli/tools/lint.rs#L315) for linting at runtime without requiring run permission,
- typescript (i.e. the entire `ts` namespace) for parsing and printing ts code for removing net permission requirement for downloading + eval (maybe not important after microsoft/TypeScript#51387) and reducing memory footprint

Additionally, not what I'm looking for right now, but could be useful:
- `deno doc` that outputs to memory
- `deno bundle` that outputs to memory
- `deno compile` that outputs to memory
- `deno info` (would be useful for building a turborepo or nx clone)

`format_file` seems very straight forward and I can probably submit a pr within a day. Typescript seems very hard, considering that it seems to run in a completely different isolate. The others seems like it would be somewhere in between in due to serializations and writing accompanying type definitions.

This seems like it could be related to #1739, but also sounds different.

author: loynoir

title: Suggestion: 'yes to all' option for specific permission prompts like 'env'
body:
Some Deno modules need permissions for many different things, e.g. lots of different environment variables. When I'm working with a module that I don't know yet and using the permission prompts I sometimes have to confirm a lot of different prompts. When this happens usually I cancel `deno run` and retry with an option like `--allow-env`, but this is a bit of a cumbersome developer experience, because I never quite know in advance how many prompts there are going to be (should I keep going with the prompts or re-run with the --allow-* flag?). For example this is how it can sometimes look on the terminal:

```
Download ⠴ https://deno.land/x/aws_api/services/sts/mod.ts
Warning Implicitly using latest version (v0.7.0) for https://deno.land/x/aws_api/services/sts/mod.ts
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_REGION".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_SESSION_TOKEN".
✅ Granted env access to "AMAZON_ACCESS_KEY_ID".
✅ Granted env access to "AMAZON_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY".
✅ Granted env access to "AMAZON_SESSION_TOKEN".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_SHARED_CREDENTIALS_FILE".
✅ Granted env access to "HOME".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_PROFILE".
✅ Granted read access to "undefined/.aws/credentials".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_RELATIVE_URI".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_CONTAINER_CREDENTIALS_FULL_URI".
✅ Granted env access to "AWS_CONTAINER_SERVICE_ENDPOINT".
⚠️  ┌ Deno requests env access to "AWS_CONTAINER_AUTHORIZATION_TOKEN".
   ├ Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.
   └ Allow? [y/n] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny) >
```

It would be nice to have a 'yes to all' option during the prompts so that all prompts for the same permission like 'env' or 'read' are accepted. Something like:

```
⚠️  ┌ Deno requests env access to "AWS_REGION".
   ├ Run again with --allow-env to bypass this prompt.
   └ Allow? [y/n/A] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny, A = yes to all) > <-- accept all 'env' prompts from this point
```

The permissions being granted should then still be shown so that users can figure out the (minimal) permissions for a production script or Docker image.
author: GJZwiers

title: Type imports are parsed even without `--check`
body:
Consider the following ts file:
```ts
import type {AcornNode} from "https://esm.sh/rollup@2.79.1?pin=v95";

const yes : AcornNode | null = null;
```
When run with `deno run foo.js` a prompt is shown for net access to esm.sh.

The same is true for type imports in jsdoc comments:
```js
/** @type {import("https://esm.sh/rollup@2.79.1?pin=v95").AcornNode | null} */
const foo = null;
```

There's a couple of weird things here:
- A prompt is shown, even though the code on esm.sh should never be run in the first place.
- Every time the script is run, a progress bar for `https://esm.sh/v95/fsevents@2.3.2/deno/fsevents` is shown, the request fails so it is never cached. I have run into cases where the script would be hanging on downloading fsevents, where the only fix seemed to be to run with `--reload`, which brings me to my last point:
- If the imported file points to something on disk, and the file in turn imports a bunch of other files, these all seem to get parsed. The reason why I found this was because I was running a file that seemed completely unrelated to rollup or esm.sh. I kept slowly removing import statements which made me realise the whole project was getting parsed. I'm not sure how much work is being done exactly, but it seems like there is a pretty big performance boost to be found here.

Seems like #15788 is potentially caused by the same issue.

author: user13571113

title: Resolve npm specifiers with the same name by module depth
body:
Right now for npm specifiers, we sort the npm package requirements by name only and then resolve them. This is ok for specifiers with different names, but not when they match. Instead we should probably resolve npm specifiers with the same name by module depth. This would allow npm specifiers with the same name closer to the user to have higher precedence than those at a lower tree depth in remote modules for example.

I will open a PR for this after landing the peer dependency support because resolution has undergone a lot of changes.
author: dsherret

title: Revisiting "Custom URL Schemes or Configurable Resolvers for Imports"
body:
In May of 2019, @rsp opened denoland/deno#2348, in which he describes the feature request of custom URL schemes / resolution.

@ry's response at the time:

> I wouldn't rule out having a general purpose hook for custom URL schemes in the future. (It would be nice to have ipfs:// for example. Then again, that might be better if it was built-in and default.)
>
> However, at the moment I would like to keep this simple and not add a bunch of hooks that we may later regret. (Hooks allow things to get complex.)

Given the recent addition of `npm:`-prefixed imports and the stability of the project as a whole, perhaps it's time to revisit this feature request?

If not, please let me know and I'll close out this issue.

author: kt3k

title: LSP: Import specifier remapping should prefer exact matches
body:
I'm currently using the `postgres` library, which I've mapped using the following in my `imports` of `import-map.json`. I use both the exact name, as well as the prefix, so that I can refer to submodules in that package as `postgres/foo/bar.ts` for example.

```json
    "postgres": "https://deno.land/x/postgres@v0.17.0/mod.ts",
    "postgres/": "https://deno.land/x/postgres@v0.17.0/",
```

Now, in some module in my code, I import `postgres`:

```ts
import { Client } from "postgres";
import { QueryArguments, QueryObjectResult } from "postgres/query/query.ts";
```

On the first line, where I've imported `Client`, I see the following suggestion:

```
Resolved Dependency

Code: https​://deno.land/x/postgres​@v0.17.0/mod.ts

The import specifier can be remapped to "postgres/mod.ts" which will
resolve it via the active import map.deno(import-map-remap)
```

I would think that an exact match, `postgres` should take precedence over the relative mapping, or if the relative mapping is the prefered way to handle these, then maybe a lint rule that suggests I don't add both an exact AND a relative import mapping for the same library.

title: npm: The requested module 'tar' does not provide an export named 'extract'
body:
running deno run -A --unstable npm:create-astro  and getting this error:
error: Uncaught (in promise) SyntaxError: The requested module 'tar' does not provide an export named 'extract'

The contents of `tar/index.js`:

```
'use strict'

// high-level commands
exports.c = exports.create = require('./lib/create.js')
exports.r = exports.replace = require('./lib/replace.js')
exports.t = exports.list = require('./lib/list.js')
exports.u = exports.update = require('./lib/update.js')
exports.x = exports.extract = require('./lib/extract.js')

// classes
exports.Pack = require('./lib/pack.js')
exports.Unpack = require('./lib/unpack.js')
exports.Parse = require('./lib/parse.js')
exports.ReadEntry = require('./lib/read-entry.js')
exports.WriteEntry = require('./lib/write-entry.js')
exports.Header = require('./lib/header.js')
exports.Pax = require('./lib/pax.js')
exports.types = require('./lib/types.js')

```

This is most likely the limitation of our CJS static analysis that should be fixed in `deno_ast`

author: filmaj

title: [feat] Deno LSP: support textDocument/declaration
body:
I am using the deno lsp within neovim. Currently deno lsp implements
- `textDocument/definition`
- `textDocument/references`
- `textDocument/executeCommand`

All of these are useful commands, but deno currently lacks the api to tell you where a variable is declared. This is very handy for jumping around code. I am curious if there are blockers to implementing this functionality, or if it is just a fairly time consuming feature to add. In any case, this issue should at least serve as a means to measure how important this feature is to the community

author: va9iff

title: Deno.memoryUsage Returning rss < heapTotal
body:
Installed version of deno: 1.27.2. This is an issue across multiple applications and not specific to any particular tech stack.

I'm currently having an issue where the rss returned from Deno.memoryUsage (across applications) is returning an rss size that is less than the heapTotal. This is an issue that just started in the past few days. It appears as though heapTotal is acting like the rss and the rss is acting like the heapTotal; rss is shrinking in tandem with the heapUsed as garbage collection occurs, but the heapTotal is consistently the largest variable. Rss starts higher in some applications and then crosses below heapTotal in applications without known memory leaks. Below is an example of console logs from an application with a known memory leak. 

![Screen Shot 2022-11-09 at 3 21 17 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200944325-a0744dad-f234-4213-8980-b0d2670a1b46.png)

Below is an example of the memory for an application that has no known memory leaks graphed over 10 seconds. Notice the rss starts larger and then crosses over the heapTotal.

![Screen Shot 2022-11-09 at 3 24 52 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200944869-fda6ce5c-fefe-4f7a-ab3b-6a82453aaf3e.png)

This is a ten second snapshot of the same application, about a minute later. Notice that the rss has stayed below the heapTotal. 

![Screen Shot 2022-11-09 at 3 26 25 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200945126-92054146-d1be-4200-a7c3-e7646cc98881.png)

Here is an example of console logs in this application of Deno.memoryUsage. This is a direct invocation, showing that the graph of the memory is not the issue, but the function itself is giving back data with the rss < heapTotal. 

![Screen Shot 2022-11-09 at 3 27 34 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200945313-1d8c5efe-d53f-4787-aa8a-e613c5f7f702.png)

Could someone provide some insight onto what's going on? My understanding based on every article I've read and from the Deno docs is that rss should represent the total memory of the process in RAM. Which means it should encompass both heapTotal, heapUsed, and external memory. It should never fall below heapTotal. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200945756-ca86f611-e7ee-4343-a296-edda4aec17bc.png)

![Screen Shot 2022-11-09 at 3 30 22 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/45442700/200945845-240292af-68a7-4660-9829-a37881300a99.png)
author: kangelopoulos

title: JSON imports not strictly typed
body:
JSON file imports are currently not strictly typed but are instead given loose types, mainly with arrays being typed as eg. `string[]` instead of `["param"]`.

The use case for a strict typing is two fold:
1. Going to a less-strict typing is always possible and relatively easy, as any such lesser type (interface or other) will necessarily already exist and can thus be referenced to, whereas going to a stricter type requires repeating the same data that was already in the JSON file.
2. Deno FFI's symbols API requires strict typing. For large-scale FFI projects the symbols object becomes unwieldy, and individual JSON files matching eg. source header files would make sense as a symbol declaration language.

title: Node compatibility mode documentation out of date
body:
I find the Node compatibility situation quite confusing. According to [Node Compatibility Mode](https://deno.land/manual@v1.27.1/node/compatibility_mode) it's still in v 1.27.1.

However, in the [release notes for v1.25.2](https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.25.2) `--compat` has been removed. From what I understand that implies that the whole Node compatibility mode is removed.

If I try to run with `--compat` on v 1.27.1 I get an error:

```
deno run --compat --unstable test.ts
error: Found argument '--compat' which wasn't expected, or isn't valid in this context

	If you tried to supply `--compat` as a value rather than a flag, use `-- --compat`

USAGE:
    deno run [OPTIONS] <SCRIPT_ARG>...

For more information try --help
```
author: jonasb

title: Interview partners for research about communication in GitHub projects wanted
body:
Hi. My name is Verena Ebert, and I am a PhD student at the University of Stuttgart in Germany.  
A few months ago, I have examined 90 GitHub projects to see what communication channels they use and how they write about them in the written documents, for example README or wiki. If you are interested in the previous results, you can find them here:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2205.01440
Your project was one of these 90 projects and, therefore, I am interested in further details about your communication setup.

To gather more data about your communication setup, I kindly ask one of the maintainers to do an interview with me. The interview will be about 30-35 minutes long and via Skype, WebEx or any other provider you prefer. The interviews should be done in November 2022, if possible.

In this interview, I would like to ask some questions about the reasons behind the channels, to understand the thoughts of the maintainers in addition to the written information.

The long goal of my PhD is to understand how communication works in GitHub projects and how a good set of communication channels and information for other maintainers and developers looks like. One possible outcome is a COMMUNICATION.md with instructions and tips about which channels could be useful and how these channels should be introduced to other projects participants. Of course, if you are interested, I will keep you up to date about any further results in my research.

If you are interested in doing an interview, please respond here or contact me via email ([verena.ebert@iste.uni-stuttgart.de](mailto:verena.ebert@iste.uni-stuttgart.de)). We will then make an appointment for the interview at a time and date that suits you.

If you agree, I would like to record the interview and transcribe the spoken texts into anonymized written texts. In this case, I will send you the transcript for corrections afterwards. Only if you agree, the transcripts or parts of it would be part of a publication.
author: verenya

title: Suggestion: Deno.bench() Memory metrics
body: As a feature request, perhaps it would be reasonable to add some memory metrics to the benchmark tool.
author: kedicesur

title: `deno.lock` causing prompts to `NODE_DEBUG` environment variable
body:
In a project with a `deno.lock` file I am being prompted for env access to NODE_DEBUG when `deno run`ning any kind of program. The lockfile contains remote Deno URLs and npm specifiers. This only happens on the canary at the moment and not on the stable release. After debugging I found it starts beginning with commit https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/fca539e65c3b824990fae3b992389d984aac7a95.

author: Dennis-Zhang-SH

title: `deno test --watch` only works when files are provided explicitly
body:
Other commands like `deno fmt/lint --watch`, will watch and fmt/lint all the files in the project if no files are passed on the command line, but this does not seem to work for `deno test --watch`.

```ts
// test.ts
import { assertEquals } from "https://deno.land/std@0.163.0/testing/asserts.ts";

Deno.test({
  name: "myTest",
  fn: () => {
    assertEquals(1+1, 2);
  },
});
```

With `deno test --watch`:
```
Watcher Test started.

ok | 0 passed | 0 failed (0ms)

Watcher Test finished. Restarting on file change...
```

Making changes to the test file will not trigger the file watcher.

With `deno test --watch test.ts`:
```
Watcher Test started.
running 1 test from ./test.ts
myTest ... ok (24ms)

ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (47ms)

Watcher Test finished. Restarting on file change...
```

With `deno test`:
```
Check file:///C:/Users/GJZwiers/repos/test_deno_test/test.ts
running 1 test from ./test.ts
myTest ... ok (8ms)

ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (33ms)
```

author: glebbash

title: cli args - Show warning when putting permission flag after module name
body:
Suggestion from @lucacasonato. It might be useful to show a warning when someone puts a permission flag after the module name. For example:

```
deno run main.js --allow-all
```

...that is incorrect and should be `deno run --allow-all main.js` because in the above it will instead provide `--allow-all` as an argument for `Deno.args` in main.js.

An issue with doing this might be that some apps expect having an argument like this though.
author: dsherret

title: `deno compile` cannot resolve npm modules
body:
It seems that `deno compile` has trouble compiling scripts using npm modules.

Given the following script:

```
// main.ts
import express from "npm:express@4.18";
const app = express();

app.get("/", function (req, res) {
  res.send("Hello World");
});

app.listen(3000);
console.log("listening on http://localhost:3000/");
```

When I do
```
deno compile main.ts
```

I get the following error:
```
Check file:///Users/fred/Code/Own/deno/main.ts
error: Uncaught Error: could not find npm package directory for 'express@4.18'
    at <anonymous> (deno:cli/tsc/99_main_compiler.js:616:28)
```

I use the following deno version:
```
deno 1.28.0 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.1
typescript 4.8.3
```
On OSX version 12.6

Using `deno run` on the same script works, as does `deno compile` for scripts that do not use npm packages.

The error also happens when using npm packages other than express
author: cb-fred

title: MongoDB Node.js Driver doesn't work when connecting to MongoDB Atlas from Deno
body:
Potentially related to issue #15824, but the [`mongodb`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/mongodb) driver cannot be used directly via Deno when targeting a [MongoDB Atlas](https://www.mongodb.com/atlas) cluster. This was reported at [NODE-4782](https://jira.mongodb.org/browse/NODE-4782) however it doesn't appear to be an issue with the Node driver itself.

```js
// file: main.ts
import { MongoClient } from "mongodb"

const URI = "mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster0.abcde.mongodb.net/test"
const client = new MongoClient(URI)

async function main() {
  console.log('connecting')  
  await client.connect()  
  console.log('connected')
  const collection = client.db('test_db').collection('test_collection')
  await collection.deleteMany({})
  await collection.insertOne({ a: 2.3 })
  var results = await collection.count({ a: 2.3 })
  console.log(`found ${results}`)
  process.exit(1)
}

main()
```

For example, when running the preceding using `npx ts-node main.ts` the script will connect to Atlas, authenticate/authorize, clear the collection, insert a new doc and execute a count.

```
connecting
connected
found 1
```

To make this run with Deno the script was updated as follows:

```js
// file: main.ts
import { MongoClient } from 'npm:mongodb' // updated for Deno

const URI = "mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster0.abcde.mongodb.net/test"
const client = new MongoClient(URI)

async function main() {
  console.log('connecting')  
  await client.connect()  
  console.log('connected')
  const collection = client.db('test_db').collection('test_collection')
  await collection.deleteMany({})
  await collection.insertOne({ a: 2.3 })
  var results = await collection.count({ a: 2.3 })
  console.log(`found ${results}`)
  process.exit(1)
}

main()
```

When the above script variation is run using `deno run --unstable --allow-all main.ts` it will fail with the following error as it doesn't appear to be able to resolve a [DNS Seed List Connection String](https://www.mongodb.com/docs/manual/reference/connection-string/#std-label-connections-dns-seedlist):

```
connecting
error: Uncaught MongoAPIError: Server record does not share hostname with parent URI
    at resolveSRVRecord (file:///Users/alex/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/mongodb/4.11.0/lib/connection_string.js:62:19)
    at async file:///Users/alex/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/mongodb/4.11.0/lib/mongo_client.js:120:31
    at async main (file:///Users/alex/temp/main.ts:17:3)
```

Changing the `URI` constant to use a [Standard Connection String](https://www.mongodb.com/docs/manual/reference/connection-string/#std-label-connections-standard-connection-string-format) of `mongodb://user:pass@cluster0-shard-00-00.abcde.mongodb.net:27017,cluster0-shard-00-01.abcde.mongodb.net:27017,cluster0-shard-00-02.abcde.mongodb.net:27017/test?ssl=true&replicaSet=Cluster0-shard-0&authSource=admin&retryWrites=true&w=majority` and re-running results in the operation just timing out:

```
connecting
error: Uncaught MongoServerSelectionError: connection timed out
    at Timeout.<anonymous> (file:///Users/alex/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/mongodb/4.11.0/lib/sdam/topology.js:293:38)
    at https://deno.land/std@0.162.0/node/timers.ts:21:15
    at Object.action (deno:ext/web/02_timers.js:147:13)
    at handleTimerMacrotask (deno:ext/web/02_timers.js:64:12)
```

Note this was tested with:
```
deno 1.27.2 (release, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.8.168.4
typescript 4.8.3
```

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Sign up for a free M0 Atlas Cluster
2. Get a [Connection String](https://www.mongodb.com/docs/guides/atlas/connection-string/) (pick an older version of any driver to get the `mongodb://` version instead of `mongodb+srv://`)
3. Update the `URI` constant with your connection string
4. `deno run --unstable --allow-all main.ts`

title: Object.entries does not exist on type ObjectConstructor
body:
Hi, I'm using IntelliJ with deno, and it seems like deno plugin/LSP does not like `Object.entries()`, because it says that I have to change the lib compiler property to es2017 or later. This has been present on 1.27 and now on 1.28.

Compiler options in deno.json are empty and I have also tried setting the module and target to esnext/es2022/es2017 but the error remains the same.

title: `deno run` generates `deno.lock`, should be mentioned in changelog v1.28.0 and docs
body:
Files are given like:
```sh
$ echo '{ "importMap": "./deps.json" }' > deno.json
$ echo '{ "imports": { "std/": "https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/" } }' > deps.json
$ echo 'import { grant } from "std/permissions/mod.ts"; console.log(grant)' > foo.js
$ ls
deno.json  deps.json  foo.js
```

In Deno 1.27.1:
```sh
$ deno run foo.js
[AsyncFunction: grant]
$ ls
deno.json  deps.json  foo.js
```

In Deno 1.28.0:
```sh
$ deno run foo.js
[AsyncFunction: grant]
$ ls
deno.json  deno.lock  deps.json  foo.js
```
Note: `deno run --no-config --import-map=deps.json foo.js` does not generates `deno.lock` file.

Using Arch Linux x64.

body:

While using npm compatibility, one of my dependencies establishes a http2 connection, and then tries to close the session calling `session.unref()`, which causes the following error 

```
error: Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'unref')
    at Subchannel.createSession (file:///Users/yzalium/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/@grpc/grpc-js/1.7.3/build/src/subchannel.js:413:17)

    at processTicksAndRejections (https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/node/_next_tick.ts:62:10)
```

This happens using the Cerbos package like so:

```typescript
import { GRPC } from 'npm:@cerbos/grpc';

const client = new GRPC('localhost:3592', { tls: false })

client.close();
```
```

I've checked, and it doesn't seem like neither Cerbos nor its grpc dependency has postinstall scripts.

Thank you.

title: NPM types: TSX warnings about JSX.IntrinsicElements in React
body: I have a TSX project with the following `deno.json` configuration: ```json { "compilerOptions": { "jsx": "react-jsx", "jsxImportSource": "npm:react" } } ``` In VS Code I see the following warning: This doesn't happen if the JSX import source is preact (`npm:preact`). I think the reason is because the preact NPM package includes the types (https://github.com/preactjs/preact/blob/master/src/index.d.ts) so Deno can load them. React needs to fetch the types from `@types/react` and this is not implemented in Deno. In the NPM roadmap (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15960) I can see the uncompleted task `automatic acquisition of @types package for type checking`. Is there any plans to implement this at anytime soon? I think it should be quite useful. At this moment, the only way to use React in Deno without TSX errors is by importing from esm.sh.

title: Support user-defined functions in sqlite3
body:
SQLite's regex syntax [errors out](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_expr.html#regexp) unless a user-defined regex function is defined.

User-defined functions are also useful for compensating for SQLite's poor datetime logic.

They can also be used with triggers to mimic Postgres' `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` behavior.

I'd expect an implementation to resemble `better-sqlite3`'s [support for user-defined functions](https://github.com/WiseLibs/better-sqlite3/blob/master/docs/api.md#functionname-options-function---this).

title: [Debug Mode] Debugger doesn't terminate when code finishes or error **IF** a NPM package is inside deno.lock
body:
Title, and related to  #16628

**CODE RUNS FINE WITHOUT DEBUGGING**

Tested on 
```
deno 1.28.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.1
typescript 4.8.3

OS: Pop 22.04 jammy
Kernel: x86_64 Linux 6.0.3-76060003-generic

With VSCode & Chrome Debugger
```

In this example code you can attach/launch a debugger with no problem when finishing program
**Lock file will contain only remote imports**
```ts
import { crypto } from "https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/crypto/mod.ts"
import { encodeToString } from "https://deno.land/std@0.85.0/encoding/hex.ts";
// import { keccak_256 } from "npm:@noble/hashes@1.1.2/sha3"

console.log(crypto.randomUUID())
// console.log(encodeToString(keccak_256("testing")))
```

Here you will add the NPM package noble/hashes to your lock file to run and debugger will not close when finishing
**Lock file will contain remote & npm imports**
```ts
import { crypto } from "https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/crypto/mod.ts"
import { encodeToString } from "https://deno.land/std@0.85.0/encoding/hex.ts";
import { keccak_256 } from "npm:@noble/hashes@1.1.2/sha3"

console.log(crypto.randomUUID())
console.log(encodeToString(keccak_256("testing")))
```

Here you can comment back the NPM package BUT it will still not close debugger.
**Lock file will contain remote & npm imports**
```ts
import { crypto } from "https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/crypto/mod.ts"
import { encodeToString } from "https://deno.land/std@0.85.0/encoding/hex.ts";
// import { keccak_256 } from "npm:@noble/hashes@1.1.2/sha3"

console.log(crypto.randomUUID())
// console.log(encodeToString(keccak_256("testing")))
```
Only way to fix it is by removing NPM packages from `deno.lock` or delete so it can generates back with only remotes.

title: Not implemented: crypto.generateKeyPairSync error with mineflayer
body:
**Describe the bug**

Using mineflayer from npm causes a "not implemented" error with the crypto module from the node compatibility layer

**Steps to Reproduce**

Running the code in this `index.ts` file
```js
import mineflayer from 'npm:mineflayer'

const bot = mineflayer.createBot({
    username: 'use any value here',
    host: 'localhost',
    auth: 'microsoft'
})

bot.on('login', () => {
    console.log('Succesfully logged in!')
})
```

Results in this error
```js
% deno run --allow-all index.ts
Error: Not implemented: crypto.generateKeyPairSync
    at notImplemented (https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/node/_utils.ts:23:9)
    at Object.generateKeyPairSync (https://deno.land/std@0.164.0/node/internal/crypto/keygen.ts:662:3)
    at MicrosoftAuthFlow.initTokenManagers (file:///C:/Users/Link/AppData/Local/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/prismarine-auth/1.7.0/src/MicrosoftAuthFlow.js:66:28)
    at new MicrosoftAuthFlow (file:///C:/Users/Link/AppData/Local/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/prismarine-auth/1.7.0/src/MicrosoftAuthFlow.js:33:10)
    at Object.authenticate (file:///C:/Users/Link/AppData/Local/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/minecraft-protocol/1.36.1/src/client/microsoftAuth.js:16:20)
    at Object.createClient (file:///C:/Users/Link/AppData/Local/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/minecraft-protocol/1.36.1/src/createClient.js:46:23)
    at Object.createBot (file:///C:/Users/Link/AppData/Local/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/mineflayer/4.5.1/lib/loader.js:94:35)
    at file:///C:/Users/Link/OneDrive/Documents/minecraft/bots/deno/index.ts:3:12
```

Cause of this bug is this line that is used inside a dependency of the package

```js
const keyPair = crypto.generateKeyPairSync('ec', { namedCurve: 'P-256' })
```

The error triggers when the `createBot` method has this option
```js
auth: 'microsoft'
```

**Expected behavior**

```js
% node index.ts
Succesfully logged in!
```

**Environment**

- OS: Windows 11
- deno version: 1.28.0
- std version: 0.164.0

author: harrysolovay

title: `Deno.Command` API needs better documentation
body:
#16516 introduced a consolidated replacement for the "spawn" API. This is an ergonomic improvement, but usage is not documented clearly enough.

Link to current documentation: https://deno.land/api@v1.28.1?unstable=&s=Deno.Command

For example, given a command instance:

```ts
const c = new Deno.Command("ls");
```

Are `c.output()` and `c.outputSync()` and `c.spawn()` the only methods which **don't throw**?

The documentation for the [`spawn`](https://deno.land/api@v1.28.1?unstable=&s=Deno.Command#method_spawn_0) method says:

> Spawns a streamable subprocess, allowing to use the other methods.

But it doesn't define what the "other methods" are. Are they "all other methods other than `output` and `outputSync`"?

Further, all of the previous subprocess APIs had behaviors which created an actual subprocess as part of the initial function invocation (e.g. `Deno.run()`, `Deno.spawn()`, etc.).

This new API seems to create an inert command instance (e.g. `new Deno.Command()`) but doesn't actually create a subprocess until another action is taken. Which methods actually create the subprocess? (It appears to be `spawn`, `output` and `outputSync`.)

Can `command.pid` be used with `output`? (It appears not.) However, it also appears that `command.pid` can be used on a command that was spawned but also already terminated.

Which methods can be used after the `spawn` method? Which methods can be used after the `output`/`outputSync` methods?

I think all of these behaviors, interactions, etc. need to be documented and explained.

title: excessive readlink syscalls on startup when running with require('npm:express')
body:
when i run this simple application on Ubuntu 22.04, importing 'npm:express', deno seems to be doing 21147 (exactly same number every time) readlink syscalls at startup, with 21145 failures. looks like module cache gets into some kind of loop and keeps making this series of calls over and over again.

it doesn't have much performance impact but seems to indicate a bug somewhere.

it happens whether run on a normal user account or as root, on bare metal and inside docker. when it happens, the terminal is completely locked up and won't respond to CTRL+C, indicating it is in some kind of busy loop.

This needs some further investigation as I cannot reproduce locally on Ubuntu 18.04. Possibly could be related to newer kernel/OS.

## Deno Version
```
deno 1.28.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.1
typescript 4.8.3
```

## OS
```
Linux f939b7944f45 5.15.0-33-generic #34-Ubuntu SMP Wed May 18 13:34:26 UTC 2022 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```
## Reproduction
```
import express from "npm:express";

const app = express();

app.get("/", (_req, res) => {
  res.send("Hello World");
});

app.listen(7001, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:7001/`);
});
```

run with
```
strace -e readlink deno run -A app.js
```

## Log
```
readlink("/root", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/root/.cache", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/root/.cache/deno", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/root/.cache/deno/npm", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/root/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)
readlink("/root", 0x7fff93417230, 1023) = -1 EINVAL (Invalid argument)

title: Add more special permission cli variables
body:
The goal is to make specifying permission (in the readme for example) more accessible

Say I have an app that requires `--allow-read=~/.cache`, this is a pain to give instructions for, as I have to say 
```
Unix: --allow-read=~/.cache
Windows: --allow-read=/APPDATA/
macos: --allow-read=macostuff
```

One way to improve this is to add another cli variable for example `<cache_dir>`, so I can just say only `--allow-read=<cache_dir>/restofthepath`

Deno already have these kind of variables `<module> <CWD> <exec_path> ..` so I'm just suggesting on expanding on those, and `<cache_dir>` is an example

title: `HttpCon.prototype.close()` doesn't flush body before closing on macOS
body:
Hey 🙋🏻‍♀️ I'm new to the low-level `http` API in Deno (as opposed to the http module from the standard library) and noticed something odd about `HttpCon.prototype.close()`

The following code produces different results on Debian (first) and Windows (second) vs. macOS (third).
```ts
async function main() {
        const conn = Deno.listen({port: 8080});
        const httpConn = Deno.serveHttp(await conn.accept());
        conn.close();
        const e = await httpConn.nextRequest();
        await e?.respondWith(new Response("Hello World\n"));
        httpConn.close();
}
main();
let res = await fetch("http://localhost:8080");
console.log(await res.text())
```


My question: Is this a bug in Deno, or is my code unsafe and relying on a platform-specific race condition?

![Debian](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5297725/203457811-3f8c760f-9def-47e5-83f0-22f98d518548.png)
![Windows](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5297725/203457826-be11c1f0-ec1e-4a75-a5ad-37087a08fa74.png)

typescript 4.4.2

Windows (works)

title: npm specifiers types not working for package with no "types" entry and another with an incorrect "types" entry
body:
I have two packages here that appear to be packaged with typescript types, but may provide them in unexpected ways. 
```ts
import * as scriptserver from 'npm:@scriptserver/core'
import * as rx from 'npm:rxjs'
```

an explanation from a discord convo in the [deno help forums](https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/1029565831149011004/1045115223666868296) w/ @dsherret gives the proper context here:
> It looks like rxjs has an entry for `"types": "index.d.ts",`, but then that file doesn't exist in the package, so it looks like in that scenario we should use the `exports -> . -> types entry` 

> for `@scriptserver/core` it distributes its types, but then it doesn't have a `"types"` entry in its package.json, but it seems like typescript still figures it out by resolving the `"main"` entrypoint and looking for the corresponding declaration file


the resulting behavior is that deno type checking considers these imports to both be `any`.

author: khrj

title: Incorrect guidance from test resourceSanitizer
body:
When using the Deno.Command() api, if one fails to manually close the streams to prevent resource leaks, Deno test sanitizer wonderfully suggests a fix  like so:

```
error: AssertionError: Test case is leaking 1 resource:

 - A child process stderr (rid 19) was opened during the test, but not closed during the test. Close the child process stderr by calling `proc.stderr.close()`.

    at assert (deno:ext/web/00_infra.js:295:13)
    at resourceSanitizer (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:413:7)
    at async Object.exitSanitizer [as fn] (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:431:9)
    at async runTest (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:836:7)
    at async runTests (deno:runtime/js/40_testing.js:1091:22)
```

However, it took me a while to realize why the suggested solution doesn't work. It's because, while stdin needs to be closed with `stdin.close()`, stderr needs to be closed with `stderr.cancel()` . I believe the error message should be updated to reflect that. Small issue, but the incorrect advice can cause a great deal of confusion.

author: oscarotero

title: @deno-types doesn't work until focusing the file that contains the comment
body:
**Describe the bug**

I'm re-exporting an npm module in `mithril.ts`; notice the `@deno-types` comment:

```ts
// @deno-types='npm:@types/mithril@2.0.11'
export { default } from 'npm:mithril@2.2.2'
```

I have the file mapped to `mithril` in `import_map.json` (and I have `{ "importMap": "./import_map.json" }` in `deno.json`):

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "mithril": "./mithril.ts"
  }
}
```

...so that in `app.ts` I can import [Mithril.js](https://mithril.js.org/) like this:

```ts
import m from 'mithril'

m.mount()
```

The `m` variable has the correct type (via `@deno-types`) – the Deno extension correctly complains that `m.mount()` expects two parameters.

And here's the bug:

If I reload (close and reopen) VS Code, the `m` variable in `app.ts` has type `any` until I open and focus `mithril.ts`. After focusing `mithril.ts`, the `m` variable in `app.ts` has the correct type, even if I close `mithril.ts` and keep it closed.

**To Reproduce**

1. Add those two TS files to your project and configure the import mapping
2. Focus any other file than `mithril.ts`. `mithril.ts` can be left open as long as its tab is not focused. Alternatively close all files
3. Reload VS Code e.g. via Command Palette: "Developer: Reload Window"
4. Open/focus `app.ts` and notice how `m` has type `any`
5. Open/focus `mithril.ts`
6. Open/focus `app.ts` again and notice how `m` has the correct type

**Expected behavior**

I would expect the 5th step above to be unnecessary – the `m` variable in `app.ts` should have the correct type already in step 4, without the need to focus `mithril.ts`.

**Screenshots**

N/A

**Versions**

- VS Code: 1.73.1
- Deno: 1.28.1
- VS Code extension: 3.14.1

```bash
$ deno --version
deno 1.28.1 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.1
typescript 4.8.3
```

author: orvitpng

title: Suggestion: Support WebCodecs API
body:
Hi!

Just wanted to put a suggestion out there for the possible inclusion of the relatively new [WebCodecs API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/WebCodecs_API) within Deno (currently supported in Chromium). 

This API offers Video, Audio and Image codecs and primarily works on Frames of `UInt8Array` (decoupled from anything DOM related). The image codecs in particular are quite appealing and may allow Deno to perform common image operations when used in tandem with WebGPU (without the requirement of native imaging dependencies), as well as allowing Deno to carry out video transcoding tasks (often requiring one to integrate with ffmpeg)

For consideration
author: sinclairzx81

title: Add CLI flag to disable internal writes to stdio streams
body:
Over time, quite a few features have been developed into Deno which provide informational messaging output to standard I/O streams: either `stderr` or `stdout`.

Some of these have corresponding environment variables to control them — for example: [version updates](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16375)

while others do not — for example: [runtime argument checking for specific strings](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16734)

Those examples represent just two of many cases, and — while all of these can be very helpful messages in certain cases — there are also scenarios where the user needs to have complete control over the stdio streams and ensure that Deno won't hijack them under any circumstance — for example: in programs where `stderr` and `stdout` are both used for different, specific communication data — and where any writes to those streams would corrupt that communication.

I would like to see a flag that prevents Deno from using those streams **entirely, for any reason** — except, of course, for uses by the user in the runtime script itself.

Such a flag would also necessarily opt in to other behavior — for example: `--no-prompt`, as otherwise the process would just hang on an attempt to use a feature behind an ungranted permission.

title: `deno run` does not output type checking errors
body:
When running `deno run` with a script containing a type error, no errors are produced:

`script.ts`:
```
function test(input: string) {
  console.log(input);
}
test(2); # 2 is not a string so the TypeScript compiler should raise an error 
```
```
$ deno run script.ts
2
```

`deno check` outputs type errors as expected:

```
$ deno check script.ts
Check script.ts
error: TS2345 [ERROR]: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'.
test(2);
     ^
    at script.ts:5:6
```

author: webdeploy-davoli-alessio

title: lsp: Deno lsp does not support clients without the `workspaceConfiguration` setting.
body:
Close if a duplicate or already discussed somewhere.


[The Deno docs](https://deno.land/manual@v1.28.2/advanced/language_server/overview#settings) strongly imply that the Deno LSP works with clients that don't implement the `workspaceConfiguration` call.

> When the language server receives a workspace/didChangeConfiguration notification, it will assess if the client has indicated if it has a workspaceConfiguration capability. If it does, it will send a workspace/configuration request which will include a request for the workspace configuration as well as the configuration of all URIs that the language server is currently tracking.

> If the client has the workspaceConfiguration capability, the language server will send a configuration request for the URI when it received the textDocument/didOpen notification in order to get the resources specific settings.

> If the client does not have the workspaceConfiguration capability, the language server will assume the workspace setting applies to all resources.

According to the good folks over at lapce, they cannot support Deno (presumably without significant dev work), with this being the case.

> [We can't support deno] because deno lsp doesn't work without workspaceConfiguration

They also claim to say that this is already known by the team at Deno, but I can't find any discussions/issues on GitHub or any discussion in the discord related to this. There is [this issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/14455) that seems to mention this being an issue on some roadmap but there has not been much attention to it.

To get to the concrete issue one of the following is true: 
1. the docs are poorly worded, #14455 is still relevant, and editors like lapce can't support deno
2. #14455 was changed/fixed at some point and editors like lapce have not gotten the memo yet

I'm hoping that either the docs can be updated for clarity or for some clarification on where the lsp workspace rework is on the roadmap.

title: Fetch allow modifying Host header
body:
Currently it is impossible to modify the Host header when calling `fetch()`.

This is *not* a duplicate of #11017 , because that asked to disallow the header to be modified, while I am requesting for a way to allow modifying it.

It is needed for us to communicate with an internal system that only ever expects the `Host` header to be an external-facing domain. (a reverse proxy or changing the machines `/etc/hosts` seems a bit overkill)

It could be part of `Deno.createHttpClient` (`{allowCustomHost: true}` or similar) as described in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/6868#issuecomment-762013710

title: Property 'xxx' does not exist on type 'object'.
body:
Deno version: 1.18.2

Deno code:
```
import caseInsensitive from 'npm:case-insensitive';
import {assert} from "https://deno.land/std@0.165.0/testing/asserts.ts";

assert(caseInsensitive('TeSt').includes('test'))
```

Result:
```
error: TS2339 [ERROR]: Property 'includes' does not exist on type 'object'.
assert(caseInsensitive('TeSt').includes('test'))
                               ~~~~~~~~
    at file:///Users/xxx/WebstormProjects/xxx/test.ts:4:32
```

I checked the repository of this package, and it only contains a single index.js file. There are no types, so why this import is an object instead of any?

https://github.com/lamansky/case-insensitive/blob/master/index.js
author: Lesiuk

title: Failure to execute cli command for npm:web-ext
body:
[web-ext](https://github.com/mozilla/web-ext) is a CLI tool for developing web-extensions created in node.js.

Since npm is now supported by deno, I tried to see if I could run it with deno, but had problems in some cases.

### 1. Unable to read CommonJS format configuration files

I haven't looked into it in depth, but for some reason the polyfill is not working?

```shell
> deno run -A npm:web-ext@7.4.0 run -s dist
Error 'could not find npm package for 'file:///C:/Users/fuji44/git/dlsite-enhancer/web-ext-config.js'' contains boxed error of unknown type:

import { getAuth } from "npm:firebase-admin/auth";

unfortunately that leads to this error:

The documentation dont give any hint that about a missing implementation: https://deno.land/std@0.166.0/node/crypto.ts?s=getCurves

**Steps to Reproduce**

author: mdunisch

title: Writing to CompressionStream/DecompressionStream causes fatal "Module evaluation is still pending" error
body:
Version: 1.28.2
System: Arch Linux

Running for example
```ts
const stream = new CompressionStream('gzip')
const writer = stream.writable.getWriter()
await writer.write(new Uint8Array(1))
await writer.close()
```
causes deno to exit with
```
error: Module evaluation is still pending but there are no pending ops or dynamic imports. This situation is often caused by unresolved promises.
```

author: dsherret

title: deno install should default to --quiet
body:
Now that deno has more deno messages, (like there is a new upgrade) and it might even have more in the future, I feel like those are out of place in the executables created by deno.

So I'm suggesting that `deno install` defaults to `deno install --quiet`

title: suggestion: automatically add copyright header to files missing one
body:
Currently, the licence check tool prints the list of files missing a copyright header and throws an error. It'd be cool if the tool could instead insert the copyright at the top of the file as defined by the `COPYRIGHT` constant. It should be easy to implement.

The tool is located at `_tools/check_licence.ts` and is run using `deno task lint:licence-headers`.
author: iuioiua

title: Run deno repl with Deno banner line suppressed (or changed)
body:
Is it possible to run the Deno REPL, preventing the first line from being output like "Deno 1.28.2"?

I tried `deno repl | tail -n +2`, but this makes the output line-by-line and breaks interactivity: I can't see the command I'm typing.

Use case: I am writing a large toolkit consisting of many independent functions and I want to allow the user to use them interactively from within the REPL. At the same time, I would like my own banner to be displayed on the first line, instead of (or, at least, in addition to) Deno's banner. Of course, I could write my own REPL for this, but why, when the Deno REPL fits perfectly?

In Python, for example, we can write the following script:
```python
from code import interact

def hello():
    print('Hello, world!')

# locals to inject:
_locals = {
    'hello': hello
    # ...
}

interact(banner='Our own fantastic banner message', local=_locals)
```
and run the program through it.

It would be great to have the same functionality in Deno, or at least the ability to replace the default banner via an environment variable or command line option.

title: LSP test runner doesn't detect `Deno.test` calls that don't have statically analyzable names
body:
**Describe the bug**
If I use `Deno.test({ name, fn })` then it works as expected but if I use a function to create the object passed into Deno.test it seems to not work.

**To Reproduce**

1. create a function such as
```ts
const example = () => ({ name: 'example', fn: () => {} })
```
2. Pass it into `Deno.test(example())`
4. Look in vscode test panel

**Expected behavior**
I expect the test to appear in the test panel and for it to be runnable via vscode test integration

**Actual Behavior**

The test is not found in the list and cannot be run via vscode test integration

**Screenshots**

#### The test `huh` is found but the test on line 15 is not found.

![Capture](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/10974/204855062-a70b48f4-e617-476e-9412-dec5d15a17fe.JPG)

**Versions**

vscode: `1.73.1` deno: `1.28.2` extension: `3.14.1`

title: Deno.Command#spawn stdout/err pipes should be `inherit` by default?
body:
This was surprising to me:

```ts
const command = new Deno.Command(Deno.execPath(), {
  args: [
    "eval",
    "--unstable",
    "--no-check",
    code,
  ],
  stdout: "piped",
});

const child = command.spawn();
// etc...
```

Then in the test output:

```ts
error: AssertionError: Test case is leaking 1 resource:

 - A child process stderr (rid 90) was opened during the test, but not closed during the test. Close the child process stderr by calling `proc.stderr.close()` or `await child.stderr.cancel()`.

    at assert (deno:ext/web/00_infra.js:295:13)
    at resourceSanitizer (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:413:7)
    at async exitSanitizer (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:431:9)
    at async Object.applyPermissions [as fn] (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:533:9)
    at async runTest (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:836:7)
    at async runTests (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:1091:22)
```

I would have expected that if I don't set `stderr` to `"piped"` explicitly, then it would be "inherit". I don't think "piped" should be the default in this case because I believe it could lead to a deadlock if `stderr`'s buffer fills up since nothing is reading from it. Plus, in this case it means I have to explicitly handle it.

cc @crowlKats, what is the correct behaviour here?

title: napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is not implemented
body:
Hi. I have been trying to use [lighningcss](https://github.com/parcel-bundler/lightningcss) in Deno and it seems to work fine but I got some warnings in the terminal:

```
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
napi_add_finalizer is not yet supported.
```

Node API support is marked as done in the [NPM roadmap](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15960), but the [referrer issue](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13633) has some Functions unmarked, like `napi_add_env_cleanup_hook` and `napi_add_finalizer`.

Is there any plans to implement them?

title: deno_crypto v 0.82 fail to compile with rust 1.60 and deno v1.25.2
body:
Hi,

I'm trying to package **Deno** for Guix using **rust 1.60**, and actually i encounter these errors with deno crypto :

I use this version v1.25.2 because this is the packaged needed by Quarto software, see configuration into latest release : https://github.com/quarto-dev/quarto-cli/releases/tag/v1.2.269

Edit : I also try with rust 1.65, with same error

author: Lesiuk

title: Node: Sending chunked responses
body:
When using node req/res seems that responses are not chunked. An example: ```js import express from 'express' const app = express() app.use('*', (req, res) => { res.header('transfer-encoding', 'chunked') res.write('

Hello...

') setTimeout(() => { res.write('...World') res.end() }, 1000) }) app.listen(4000) ``` When running on NodeJS response is chunked correctly ... If you open `http://localhost:4000` in a browser you will see: **Hello** one second passed ...World From the example above replace the import with: ```import express from 'npm:express'``` Then run: ```sh deno run -A --unstable server.js ``` Now the response isn't chunked if your open `http://localhost:4000` you have to wait 1 seconds then see all output.
author: gffuma

title: Suppress unimplemented napi logs
body:
Hi! I'm currently experimenting with importing existing napi modules in Deno and am getting error messages such as:

```
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
```

This is fine, I'm willing to tolerate whatever brokenness I encounter. But there doesn't appear to be a way to suppress these logs and they present as significant noise in the console. eg. from [`cli/napi/env.rs`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a2488ae79200abfcbad0f0f8b084a3ff8e183880/cli/napi/env.rs#L62):

```
#[napi_sym::napi_sym]
fn napi_add_env_cleanup_hook(
  _env: *mut Env,
  _hook: extern "C" fn(*const c_void),
  _data: *const c_void,
) -> Result {
  eprintln!("napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported");
  Ok(())
}
```

## Possible solutions

### 1. Do nothing. Wait for support for the NAPI APIs

I'm sure they aren't implemented for a reason. We can tolerate the log noise if they will be in the short-term though.

### 2. Add a way to ignore them with an environment variable

This doesn't seem to warrant a dedicated CLI option so an environment variable would make the most sense. Suggestions are:

```
# Suppress all
DENO_SUPPRESS_NAPI=1

# Suppress particular method
DENO_SUPPRESS_NAPI=napi_add_env_cleanup_hook
```

There's precedent for this sort of environment variable in Node.

### 3. Suppress them automatically when `--quiet` is used

As the title implies. `--quiet` is currently described as "Suppress diagnostic output". Unsure if this would fit within the scope of that flag.

author: GJZwiers

title: Deno.run() doesn't find `.cmd` executables in the PATH
body:
# Problem:

`Deno.run()` is unable to execute .cmd files unless they include the `.cmd` extension.

# Expected:

When running on Windows, "foo" should be able to execute command `foo` whether it has an `.exe` or `.cmd` extension. (And possibly others? Do people still use `.bat`?)

# Reproduction:

```typescript
// bug.ts
async function run(...cmd: string[]) {
    const p = Deno.run({cmd})
    await p.status()
}

// is an .exe:
await run("protoc", "--version")

// workaround works, but requires always checking for Windows.
await run("npm.cmd", "--version")

// Does not work:
await run("npm", "--version")
```

# Output:

```
deno run -A bug.ts

libprotoc 3.20.2
7.19.0
error: Uncaught (in promise) NotFound: program not found
    const p = Deno.run({cmd})
                   ^
    at opRun (deno:runtime/js/40_process.js:33:16)
    at Object.run (deno:runtime/js/40_process.js:116:17)
    at run (file:///C:/Users/Cody/code/feoblog-client/private/protobuf/bug.ts:3:20)
    at file:///C:/Users/Cody/code/feoblog-client/private/protobuf/bug.ts:9:7
```

Note:in both the Windows Command Prompt and PowerShell, both `protoc` and `npm` are runnable as commands without specifying a file extension: 

```
> get-command protoc |Format-List

Name            : protoc.exe
CommandType     : Application
Definition      : C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\bin\protoc.exe
Extension       : .exe
Path            : C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\bin\protoc.exe
FileVersionInfo : File:             C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\bin\protoc.exe
                  InternalName:     protoc.exe
                  OriginalFilename: protoc.exe
                  FileVersion:      3.20.2.0
                  FileDescription:  Compiled with MSVC 19.0.24215.1 - Chocolatey
                  Shim
                  Product:          Protocol Buffers - Google's Data Interchange
                  Format - Chocolatey Shim
                  ProductVersion:   3.20.2.0 - Chocolatey Shim: 1.0.0
                  Debug:            False
                  Patched:          False
                  PreRelease:       False
                  PrivateBuild:     False
                  SpecialBuild:     False
                  Language:         Language Neutral


> get-command npm |Format-List

Name            : npm.cmd
CommandType     : Application
Definition      : C:\Program Files\nodejs\npm.cmd
Extension       : .cmd
Path            : C:\Program Files\nodejs\npm.cmd
FileVersionInfo : File:             C:\Program Files\nodejs\npm.cmd
                  InternalName:
                  OriginalFilename:
                  FileVersion:
                  FileDescription:
                  Product:
                  ProductVersion:
                  Debug:            False
                  Patched:          False
                  PreRelease:       False
                  PrivateBuild:     False
                  SpecialBuild:     False
                  Language:

```

# Workaround

You can check whether you're on windows and add a `.cmd`. However, this also requires you to know whether a particular tool is distributed on Windows as an `.exe` or a `.cmd`.  (i.e.: a blanket: "if windows, add .cmd" isn't a suitable fix, since it breaks anything that's distributed as an `.exe`.)

title: Deno panic (because of --fail-fast?)
body:
Got this panic output:

```
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.28.3
Args: ["deno", "test", "--fail-fast=3", "--allow-net", "--allow-env", "--allow-read", "--unstable", "--", "--env", "undefined"]

thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Os { code: 32, kind: BrokenPipe, message: "Broken pipe" }', cli/tools/test.rs:1650:56
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: core::result::unwrap_failed
   3: core::result::Result<T,E>::unwrap
   4: deno::tools::test::TestOutputPipe::flush
   5: deno::tools::test::TestEventSender::send
   6: <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
error: Uncaught Error: Command: "deno test --fail-fast=3 --allow-net --allow-env --allow-read --unstable -- --env " exited with code 1
    throw new Error(
          ^
    at Module.run (file:///Users/andrew/workspaces/ethf/bls-wallet/aggregator/programs/helpers/shell.ts:16:11)
    at async file:///Users/andrew/workspaces/ethf/bls-wallet/aggregator/programs/premerge.ts:34:7
    at async file:///Users/andrew/workspaces/ethf/bls-wallet/aggregator/programs/premerge.ts:15:3
```

Since this is a broken pipe, I'm guessing this has to do with the `--fail-fast` option nuking a stream in a way that doesn't get handled correctly.

This has reproduced every time for me so far. To reproduce:

```
git clone git@github.com:web3well/bls-wallet
cd bls-wallet
git checkout 0bfaf98dfa4e0ad24ddebc5e687ea8e643729e38
cd aggregator
cp .env.example .env
./programs/premerge.ts
```

Running the test suite 'properly' requires some other set-up, but it's not necessary to reproduce the panic.

To clarify, this bug involves some failing tests in my project, but they aren't caused by the deno bug (ie I'm not asking for help with my own tests).
