author: oscarotero

title: Error importing npm:postgraphile
body:
**Describe the bug**

The Deno extension doesn't correctly detect the type of re-exported functions/object/etc from npm packages, like so:

```js
export { a, b, c } from './another-file';
export { d, e, f } from 'another-package';
```

This results in errors failing to be reported in the UI and a lack of type information being presented to the developer.

**To Reproduce**

Write any code that uses re-exported functions/objects/etc, and you'll see something like the following:
![Deno Example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/606857/205705379-0e945e8b-4499-4d45-acf9-108f9dee9061.jpg)


**Expected behavior**

The types of the code being used should be correctly detected and any errors reported, as it does in a standard Node project:
![Node Example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/606857/205705523-89f5382f-26e0-410e-b513-e1e8aed3ff07.jpg)

**Versions**

vscode: 1.73.1 deno: 1.28.3 extension: 3.14.1
author: spencerhakim

title: Raw/gzipped files distribution (?)
body:
Hi, Deno folks! Not sure if this is a right place for this issue, of if it is an issue at all, or is it how it is intended to be (although I believe that at least the error messages should look differently and, probably, appear earlier (see below)). 

### Background
For some reason I started my journey in Deno Land from a bit weird place: I wanted to make the MNIST dataset package which will allow TS-lovers to try machine learning essentials with Deno. My first intent was to load datset from original [Yann LeCun's page][1] over the network and cache it in `tmp`. However I decided that that will be unnecessary external dependency: in unlikely case if Yann LeCun's page go down, user's still could download the package but it won't work. So I decided to pack gzipped versions of the data files inside the package. 

[1]: http://yann.lecun.com/exdb/mnist/

### The problem
I [was requesting the gzipped files][2] with
```ts
  const zipFile = new URL(`../data/${filename}.gz`, import.meta.url).pathname;
```
That worked well locally. However when published the package to `deno.land/x/` I got an error:
```
deno run --allow-read ./show.ts
error: Uncaught (in promise) NotFound: No such file or directory (os error 2), open '/x/mnist@v1.0.0/data/train-images-idx3-ubyte.gz'
  const reader = await Deno.open(src, {
                 ^
    at async Object.open (deno:runtime/js/40_files.js:55:17)
    at async gunzipFile (https://deno.land/x/compress@v0.4.4/gzip/gzip_file.ts:22:18)
    at async getContent (https://deno.land/x/mnist@v1.0.0/lib/load_mnist.ts:66:5)
    at async readImages (https://deno.land/x/mnist@v1.0.0/lib/load_mnist.ts:31:19)
    at async Promise.all (index 0)
    at async loadMnist (https://deno.land/x/mnist@v1.0.0/lib/load_mnist.ts:12:62)
    at async file:///home/user/tests/mnist-test/test.ts::7:15
```
Although, apparently, [the file **is** actually there][3].
I've tried to load the library from `raw.githubusercontent`, to use [`path.dirname()`][4] [dirname][5] and [deno-dirname][6] – all with the same result. Then I had an idea that I could try to load the file directly as `https://deno.land/x/mnist@v1.0.0/data/t10k-images-idx3-ubyte.gz` and see if it caches the files locally. When I tried to do this through VSCodes I got an error `invalid utf-8 sequence of 1 bytes from index 1`. And when I tried to run script from CLI, I got **many** pages of integers with the following error at the end:

```
...
229, 163, 153, 110, 217, 215, 240, 136, 136, 136, 241, 172, 239, 168, 90, 163, 79, 253, 201, 
142, 110, 244, 254, 162, 92, 237, 65, 124, 48, 78, 108, 203, 51, 70, 83, 145, 103, 123, 238, 
131, 134, 67, 115, 230, 204, 153, 32, 37, 95, 252, 149, 229, 127, 0, 223, 59, 55, 145, 16, 161, 
119, 0], error: Utf8Error { valid_up_to: 1, error_len: Some(1) } }
error: invalid utf-8 sequence of 1 bytes from index 1
    at file:///home/user/tests/mnist-test/test.ts:5:8
```

[2]: https://github.com/RB-Lab/deno-mnist/blob/6c8fa066f96790414405dc1a764b9fc248a36213/lib/load_mnist.ts#L59
[3]: https://deno.land/x/mnist@v1.0.0/data/t10k-labels-idx1-ubyte.gz?source=
[4]: https://github.com/RB-Lab/deno-mnist/blob/ceada07a2ab681ccba8d5e2201dec3d632ed174d/lib/load_mnist.ts#L61
[5]: https://deno.land/x/dirname@1.1.2
[6]: https://github.com/rsp/deno-dirname

### Expected behavior (?)
I would expect that Deno would either
1. Cache and load raw/gzip file normally, or
2. Forbid to publish packages with such files, or at least
3. Not print tons of integers in the output 😄 

--
Thanks!
author: RB-Lab

title: thread 'main' panicked at 'Custom error class must have a builder registered', core/error.rs:115:6
body:
Hi,
I seem to be running into the panic issue.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.28.3

thread 'main' panicked at 'Custom error class must have a builder registered', core/error.rs:115:6
stack backtrace:
   0:        0x1054307bc - <std::sys_common::backtrace::_print::DisplayBacktrace as core::fmt::Display>::fmt::h2990a6f24ccff6db
   1:        0x104bdcd7c - core::fmt::write::h91497fd291c8b104
   2:        0x105406a24 - std::io::Write::write_fmt::h23fa41342cffacf4
   3:        0x105434b94 - std::panicking::default_hook::{{closure}}::hb41cdb784f4c17ac
   4:        0x105434858 - std::panicking::default_hook::hdfe992d5fb29a991
   5:        0x104b26fe8 - deno::setup_panic_hook::{{closure}}::h5e935c84e6e3da4b
   6:        0x1054350f0 - std::panicking::rust_panic_with_hook::h2eb0e4b718773521
   7:        0x105434ed8 - std::panicking::begin_panic_handler::{{closure}}::h06a693206403b4d5
   8:        0x105434e4c - std::sys_common::backtrace::__rust_end_short_backtrace::h8998defd518dbcad
   9:        0x105434e28 - _rust_begin_unwind
  10:        0x104bdaf0c - core::panicking::panic_fmt::ha46aa9af97eb193d
  11:        0x104be0fac - core::panicking::panic_display::h0b3cf32cf3b35897
  12:        0x104be0f70 - core::panicking::panic_str::hedee8590c93b39b0
  13:        0x104be0f54 - core::option::expect_failed::h0b457b49698c9371
  14:        0x104c70ae4 - deno_core::error::to_v8_error::h406ecfc9d61f68bb
  15:        0x104f459ec - <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn::h1613deedc870ec81

## Things I noticed / tried

I've gone through my code line by line to see which part seems to be triggering the issue in the hopes that it helps?:
```typescript
// Commenting out the `${curr.id!}` here seems to fix the issue?
prev[`${curr.id!}`] = {
          ...
          // OR: setting history here to an empty array also solves the issue
          history: stationData.filter((sd) => sd.station_id === curr.id),
}
```
The code above is from a reduce which at the end contains an object in the shape I want but I've also tried it using a regular for-of loop with the same panic.

Maybe it's also worth noting that the function (static) which executes the aforementioned code is called from within a generator function which was imported dynamically (```import()``` syntax).

I've also tried inlining the logic of the aforementioned static function within the generator function (as opposed to calling the static method). This returned the same error (changing the ```${curr.id!}``` herein still fixes the panic).

I've also tried calling the static method from a simple file with no extra logic (and thus no dynamic imports) and that seems to work perfectly fine.

TLDR: removing either ```prev[`${curr.id!}`]``` or ```history: stationData.filter((sd) => sd.station_id === curr.id)``` stops the panics.

title: [FEATURE REQUEST] Search modules online
body:
Hi.

Since Deno handles modules by itself without external PMs, it would be nice to provide a flag allowing the user to find module online, something like:

```bash
% deno search <module-name> --registry=<npmjs.com|deno.land|etc.>
```

for example:

```bash
% deno search postgres --registry=deno.land

1. postgres
2. postgresjs
3. postgres_shift
4. denodb
```

and an info:

```bash
% deno info module denodb --online

denodb (Popular)
Description: MySQL, SQLite, MariaDB, PostgreSQL and MongoDB ORM for Deno.
Module: https://deno.land/x/denodb/mod.ts
```

Also, making it easy to the user by showing the module entry point (e.g: `https://deno.land/x/denodb/mod.ts`).

What do you think?

title: Overly aggressive autocomplete with arrow function parameter names
body: **Describe the bug** Typing a `,` in this context adds the top autocomplete suggestion and auto-imports it: This is the result after I hit the `,` key: This is bad on a few levels: 1. There's no reason for Deno to think I want to name my function parameter `xor` instead of `x`, which is what I typed. 2. That auto-import is crazy and breaks at runtime. **To Reproduce** 1. Write an arrow function that takes multiple parameters. 2. Type `,` after the first parameter 3. Observe that the first autocomplete suggestion is used. **Expected behavior** If I type `(x, i) =>` to define an arrow function, I expect it to come out as `(x, i) =>` rather than `(xor, i) =>`. **Screenshots** Here's the code from the screenshots: ```ts declare let num: number[]; const squares = num.map((x ``` The `xor` symbol is coming from lodash, which I've imported in a `deps.ts` file: ```ts // deps.ts // @deno-types="npm:@types/lodash" import {default as ld} from "npm:lodash"; export const _ = ld; ``` TypeScript alone doesn't seem to have this issue -- I can't reproduce it on the TypeScript playground and have never run into this in non-Deno TypeScript in VS Code. **Versions** - vscode: 1.72.1 - deno: deno 1.28.3 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin) / v8 10.9.194.5 / typescript 4.8.3 - extension: v3.14.1

title: Uncatchable error after repeated calls to Deno.statSync
body:
```js
for (let i = 0;; i++) {
  console.log(i);
  Deno.statSync("file that exists");
  try { Deno.statSync("file that does not exist"); } catch {}
}
```

One would expect this loop to continue indefinitely, since any error thrown by `Deno.statSync` should be caught by the surrounding `try` statement. However, it eventually terminates with an uncaught error.

```shellsession
$ touch 'file that exists'
$ deno run --allow-read a.js
0
1
2
…
4079
4080
4081
error: Uncaught NotFound: No such file or directory (os error 2), stat 'file that does not exist'
  try { Deno.statSync("file that does not exist"); } catch {}
             ^
    at Object.statSync (deno:runtime/js/30_fs.js:322:9)
    at file:///cwd/a.js:4:14
$ deno --version
deno 1.28.3+791e623 (canary, x86_64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.8.3
```

title: Allow clearing `localStorage` from the CLI for a specific location or file
body:
I think this could also be implemented by allowing the `file` protocol for the `--location` flag and running the repl/eval subcommand:

```sh
deno eval --location ./path/to/module.js 'localStorage.clear()'
deno eval --location file:///path/to/module.js 'localStorage.clear()'
deno eval --location https://example.com/module.ts 'localStorage.clear()' # already works
```

I'm trying to use lodash from npm.

```js
console.log(_.VERSION); // This line causes the error
```

## Expected
I would expect the types to be downloaded together, but I know this is still part of the roadmap.

What can I do though?

author: BriCo84

title: Is there an equivalent way to achieve Node's readable.unshift method using Deno's ReadableStream?
body:
I want to port some code written for Node to Deno, but I ran into an issue with Deno's [ReadableStream](https://deno.land/api@v1.28.3?s=ReadableStream) lacking an unshift method, like what Node has with [readable.unshift](https://nodejs.org/api/stream.html#readableunshiftchunk-encoding).

I know there is Node Compatibility Mode in Deno, but the Deno ReadableStream I have to use is coming from [this Deno API](https://deno.land/api@v1.28.3?s=Deno.Conn) so I cannot substitute it for the Node Compatibility Mode API.

Is there a way to emulate the behavior of Node's unshift method using a Deno ReadableStream, so data can be put back onto the Deno stream?

body:
## Reproduction

1. Create a new directory.
2. In that directory, create `post.md` with the following content:

```markdown
---
title: "Cactus Harvesting: Cycle-Aware Reference Counting in Rust"
publishDate: "2019-07-15"
slug: cactus-harvesting
summary: >-
  🌵 CactusRef lets you build cyclic data structures using strong references and
  knows how to deallocate unreachable cycles. You can use CactusRef to implement
  a doubly linked list. The CactusRef API is compatible with std::rc.
---

🌵 CactusRef is a single-threaded, cycle-aware, reference counting smart pointer
[[docs]] [[code]]. CactusRef is nearly a drop-in replacement for [`std::rc`]

[docs]: https://artichoke.github.io/cactusref/cactusref/index.html
[code]:
  https://github.com/artichoke/ferrocarril/tree/0052dc1d0b234c2535b8dd87a096e048bdc0819e/cactusref
```

author: lopopolo

title: Lets create a community wide NodeJS Shim
body:
Hi i am frank i work on a lot of stuff and mainly i come to the conclusion that nodejs and deno it self are not needed anymore for everything that they get used for today.

That leaves us for compat reasons to a state where it would be great if we would have API Shims of NodeJS and Deno.

This would allow me to create PoCs of deno without deno and nodejs without nodejs. 

i bring that up here as you and me we have shared interest in a NodeJS Api shim at last and other projects that i know have the same interest and needs.

I Guess a ECMAScript version of the NodeJS API and the Deno API without the final function calls so that we can inject them would be highly beneficial for the process of moving most of the Applications directly into the browser

project like deno would have less to do with import logic problems and could focus on better internals like mksnapshot to create high speed dumps 

I am mainly chromium engineer last week audit stack blitz complet  that's where i got now the final conclusion from that it is time to deprecate the old patterns.

Today the browser it self got flags to run directly isolates. 


## Reasoning
There are 2 main ways for you to support the NodeJS Api
- Implementation via Rust create a Equal api or compile the existing some how so take internals call it a day
- Or create a Real API Shim in ESM like browserify but more modern that is reusable everywhere where you want to use it.

most people used so much typescript that they have forgotten that Polymorphism is one of the highest and strongest part of ECMAScript we can take existing applications when we abstract at the right levels and run them everywhere.

as we started creating engines no one knowed what he does. Even not me after i was into Operating System Design and Architecture i understood finally the linkings.


The API shim does not need to support much mainly FS, NET, PROCESS, child_process. then some of the WebStream Adapters so parts of the Stream interfaces. that's it. we are done TLS and other internals should already match enough i guess. 

i did some migrations for example from nodejs projects to graaljs which is the same situation as when i would port to deno as porting is always the same overall i identified that most dependencies come from wrong development fundamentals so if we only implement a minimal Api that runs in Deno and NodeJS and the Browser we can move everything into the browser

the browser is not the blocking part the developers and users are the blocking part we can help them via offering the right levels of abstraction.

title: Surprising autocomplete with string literal
body:
**Describe the bug**

I was working on [this Advent of Code problem](https://adventofcode.com/2018/day/12) and had an odd bug where I printed `#.` instead of `.` which gave me a wrong answer. Oh well, probably fat fingered it.

For part two of the problem, I spent quite a while debugging my incorrect solution before eventually realizing I'd made the exact same mistake a second time. Now I was suspicious. It turns out it was the VSCode Deno extension's fault. It was quietly changing `'.'` to `'#.'` as I typed the former. That string (`'#.'`) never appears on its own elsewhere in my code, only as a substring of larger strings:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/98301/206913072-3ae59f2d-c411-4441-a6bc-5c251da7c39c.mov

**To Reproduce**

1. Open up this file in VS Code https://github.com/danvk/aoc2022/blob/49917882eb0f2b64f407857eab052c1f674e3551/warmup/y2018day12/y2018day12.ts#L32
2. Rewrite the `'.'` by typing `'` then `.` then `'` as in the video
3. Scratch your head as the Deno extension changes the string to `'#.'`.

The surprising change happens when I type the `.` character.

**Expected behavior**

When I type `'.'`, I would expect to get that string in my source code, rather than a different string.

For comparison, the TypeScript playground does exactly what I expect with [this same source code][1].

**Screenshots**

Here's the video again, where I type `'` then `.` then `'`:

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/98301/206913072-3ae59f2d-c411-4441-a6bc-5c251da7c39c.mov

**Versions**

- vscode: 1.72.1
- deno: deno 1.28.3 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin) / v8 10.9.194.5 / typescript 4.8.3
- extension: v3.14.1

[1]: https://www.typescriptlang.org/play?ssl=32&ssc=1&pln=33&pc=1#-hoggAeZAQQzwYoAPjKAFlaLIAQ8nj0yvBSKR6BxkOC3n4RNhsDpkA9sCghPhBLRhGSYNgALzYM7IFovMxvPRQWRnQTHADk325HxeFjeWTxBSK3lO50FrPRz0EklpNLpZg+WzxBKJJMEZIpaFp2EZzGwf0BwNBEJFYS82FO-2J+rMGXtAOJyFJ5Mpjz0sp68qdiAA1AGPji8XjEBobVqdZ6YC9ENilXTucB+aGw+G3dqPWg9JwTohVbiw8Fi6XixqfJR0VB9YbsDC4QiUEjsCi0Rjwcci3iNNbbT5EPqTP7sC6GS1vRhfZJ-UGQ75i+qDkT48hZCs6i0IPRBIn6VKE2yOUIebTwdh+Za8dXJ0UuSgN4It1Adz4k5fUz2Qq5RYVilyIC3lAeg9P8PJxJB-LfuwggQDA1Bru6up1DuLTCiI5Y7MgeyIAc2AqLQOFoMcHJEWk3wmkCIJgjAkIUGhFyNvCjwtsiqLokR4ILhWK4+Iy1SCCM9DkbWdL1lRZq0eClqVj8FTIAAGmUVTVIp+4KapxwvJBZEzFAnyWn21IDtg1T6gpil4NgI5mWOdKqVZAY2U61Tzumy6Ej4sgUsICH6gEegwERYHHOZuA2WU5nOQAzB8ehVLIxxDjSF56WkeiSAUyUhgA-J+3LYHyej8noABWpAoDyV5LsSkbHMBoHgT5MjFMgHw8RmeICUJIn6XmRAnNU354qWo3FrB8HUD1wmiVkWG7Ps1A+d4FikX1FHGv81HmnRqE1t8zHNs87EdlxZTIN8UnFF8Bo0cU6bGTa6KDg6o4usgc7Bh52DpWJBFEJgRFaCRf37S035jfifH3AFbIgtpukbQZHy8YSHbeqQPQ8sgNLchdZTcq+hh4wM36yE8vWietokwf8U33PNrjYbh+EUygVP6TT+mUdt110Z10NefJKDKeUNTqRZlQ1IjcR-YZaNQBjTDY4FwXPCRlkqRLAYWPFiXHFo56-cjmXZVoeUFUVl4lfFFVVdy0FM9si14dQUA6OwnQaH5AByxyXXdu2C3Jsj6imwCQZBwCR1HcfxwnidJ8nKcx3okex2nqcZ3EMfcsenLVUWis+IY5nidZFgAGyWi99ye3qypGf2dcpdgjrOr9U5yrOxLuYuGYRjasjxomSoFYLGYe14eoBnSxxl9gzmFteP7jTe9P+dP7DO+IBq0IgqgEbIFOkLQWiSN8JgxfcaAEkQtayA9FSMG7FCkFQD-GwAfhYN92PYKAYgJCIBIAUBQwDrJMFIAAaytNSWgv1fJtSdNcFoxIfAPFoEgM4AAvPUQw-LYF9u-T+UBcqQIirCDkdx5R6mAtgdQjDJD0MYow8gZUQRh2MswcYXAKS--lUUIx0nLZGCs8RFLAK4IeyUcjeASv8WgCUD7tXTHJCc-1aC8KEJcRo5x2iRG6H0bsskYZIWzChBiNZ9xHElIyTpAbhbUDpIMYYYwJhoGmFyE5ZA8y9CgNKb8CrczIu5CtHwFh8ZINWslLMMY0AdIuvCxWRLC3jB8CYUt9LvZ+wDnW0sQA

```sh
  14:        0x100fdb3b0 - deno::errors::get_error_class_name::h2a9918ddd642a50a
  15:        0x10131b200 - deno_core::error::to_v8_error::h406ecfc9d61f68bb
  16:        0x10167e340 - deno_runtime::ops::process::op_run::v8_func::h56971477b8014cd2
  17:        0x10167940c - <extern "C" fn(A0) .> R as v8::support::CFnFrom<F>>::mapping::c_fn::h78d0641241932d68
```

## How to reproduce
At first, please clone this repository.

```sh
$ git clone https://github.com/skanehira/deno_runtime_example
$ cargo build
```

And add this script at project root directory.

```typescript
import $ from "https://deno.land/x/dax@0.17.0/mod.ts";

const cmds = [];
for (let i = 0; i < 5000; i++) {
  cmds.push($`./target/debug/deno_runtime_example`.text());
}
await Promise.all(cmds);
```

Run script and we got panic.

```sh
deno run -A main.ts
```

Maybe add to https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped?

title: Corporate Proxy results in BadCertificate Alert

**Describe the bug**

A corporate proxy with its own HTTPS certificate results in the error/alert
`Sending fatal alert BadCertificate`
in the command line. The proxy cert is signed by the corporate CA with the root certificate imported into the OS cert store.

(Somewhat related to denoland/deno#11491)

**Steps to Reproduce**

```bash
#!/bin/bash

export DENO_TLS_CA_STORE=system,mozilla

deno run -Ldebug \
  --allow-env \
  --allow-net \
  ./test.ts
```

```typescript
import { serve } from "https://deno.land/std@0.167.0/http/server.ts";

function handler(req: Request): Response {

}

serve(handler);
```

Is the task "[ ] automatic acquisition of @types package for type checking" related to type checking npm specifier imports? I'm getting errors such as:

```
TS2305 [ERROR]: Module '"deno:///missing_dependency.d.ts"' has no exported member 'Box3'.

```

My deno.json:

author: itsdouges

title: Ability to resolve npm specifier imports in bundlers made for npm
body:
Hi! As mentioned in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15960 I'm opening a new issue to talk about this issue I'm having.

Thanks to #15427 and npm: specifiers hitting stable you can run Vite in Deno which works. One issue is that for any dependencies in the frontend graph that aren't executed in Deno they don't ever get their dependencies resolved/cached. 

Because of this you have to rely on importing them in any file that is executed in Deno, e.g. in the React example here https://deno.land/manual@v1.28.2/node/how_to_with_npm/react#add-a-router you show importing the dependencies used in the frontend app in the config file.

This will then "install" the dependencies on run, and then make them available in node_modules for Vite (and any other bundler). This unblocks usage but causes a few problems:

1. Without importing the dependencies in a module ran in Deno they'll never be installed
1. The frontend app can't use npm: specifiers, but instead the node_module name (`react` instead of `npm:react`)
1. When using the node_module name (`react` instead of `npm:react`) they aren't type checked (because Deno doesn't know about the module). So you need to resort to creating a `import_map.json`, example below:
1. When setting jsxImportSource to `react` it will add to the source file the `react/jsx-runtime` import... but of course that isn't a real dependency for Deno so it's another entry to add to the import_map

```{
  "imports": {
    "@faze/editor-backend": "./packages/editor-backend/mod.ts",
    "@faze/editor-cli": "./packages/editor-cli/mod.ts",
    "@faze/editor-frontend": "./packages/editor-frontend/mod.ts",
    "@react-three/drei": "npm:@react-three/drei@9.45.0",
    "@react-three/fiber": "npm:@react-three/fiber@8.9.1",
    "react-dom": "npm:react-dom@18.2.0",
    "react-dom/client": "npm:react-dom@18.2.0/client",
    "react-router-dom": "npm:react-router-dom@6.4.4",
    "react": "npm:react@18.2.0",
    "react/jsx-runtime": "npm:react@18.2.0/jsx-runtime",
    "three": "npm:three@0.144.0",
    "three-stdlib": "npm:three-stdlib@2.20.4"
  }
}
```

Doing all of the above at the very least gets it into a usable state but it's not ideal. If we could resolve Deno npm specifier imports (and during resolution it installs/caches them) I can imagine a plugin being written for bundlers to make use of it so it has better interop.

I will say the interop between a natively installed npm package (via npm, and hopefully other package managers) and Deno npm:specifiers worked suprisingly well and the two seem to work together with each other, very great stuff.

Let me know if you need more info 😄.
author: itsdouges

title: Error while using ioredis in deno

body:
It seems that when you ask to open a folder with the "open" command from "node/fs/promises", the error code returned is incorrect. Instead of "EISDIR" the program returns "EACCES" despite the given permissions.

I'm on a windows machine, I didn't take the time to test under macOS & linux.

```ts
import { resolve } from "https://deno.land/std@0.167.0/path/mod.ts"
import { open } from "https://deno.land/std@0.167.0/node/fs/promises.ts"

try{
  await open(resolve('.'));
}catch(e){
  console.log(e.code)
}
```

Current output :
```bash
$ deno run -A ./main.ts
EACCES
```
Desired output :
```bash
$ deno run -A ./main.ts
EISDIR
```

title: Deno doesn't support "allowSyntheticDefaultImports" compiler option which means a lot of npm dependencies that ship types won't typecheck properly
body:
Hello! There's quite a few npm dependences that have typedefs for default exports that look like, well, a default export! Currently in Deno it results in the following error:

```
This expression is not callable.
```

As more folk start using Deno they're going to run into this problem, and it'll be a friction point that ideally shouldn't exist. 

Other related issues:

- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16458
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16475
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16506

Can Deno support the `allowSyntheticDefaultImports` compiler option, or do something to transparently make this work?

title: Add `experimental.enableProjectDiagnostics` setting.
body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

TypeScript has a setting for enabling project wide diagnostics. It gives us error messages and refactoring capabilities for files that are not open in the editor.
```
typescript.tsserver.experimental.enableProjectDiagnostics
```

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Add an equivalent setting for Deno.
```
deno.tsserver.experimental.enableProjectDiagnostics
```

Hello, I have an idea.

it is a command that only available in repl

```

console.log : (...data: any[]) => void

undefined

```
of course, this feature is only available to any symbol that has a typescript declaration

title: `deno install` with NPM specifier puts lockfile in PATH
body:
This is not _detrimental_, just a minor paper-cut with the newly released ability to `deno install` with `npm` specifiers. Assuming you have your `deno bin dir` on your system `PATH`, when you install an `npm` specifier module, the generated `lockfile` will now show up in tab completion for the installed module:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34140052/208158160-7e41f666-ec22-4049-86d0-9e132681f646.png)

> pictured above, the result of running `ls -lah` in a default `deno bin` dir, then again after running `deno install npm:cowsay`, then the result of typing `cowsay [Tab]` showing that both the `cowsay` binary and its `lockfile` show up as part of the tab completion.

For context, this is on `Ubuntu` with `zsh` as the shell, and my `PATH` includes  the `deno bin` directory. Here's my `deno --version` output:

```sh
deno 1.29.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
``` 

and here's the relevant bit of my `PATH`: `/home/vscode/.deno/bin:/bin:/usr/local....`

Perhaps the lockfile could be a dotfile (e.g. `.cowsay.lock.json`)? This would make it so the lock doesn't sort directly next to the script, but maybe that's less annoying than having tab completion contain an unexpected result?

Thanks as always for y'alls work on Deno!

title: Deno REPL allows combining && and || with ??
body:
The nullish coalescing operator is able to be combined with && and || in the REPL.

It should be a syntax error.

MDN docs:

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Operators/Nullish_coalescing#description

They describe the following as a SyntaxError (the first code-block after the 'Description' subheading if you follow that link)

```
null || undefined ?? "foo"; // SyntaxError

null && undefined ?? "foo"; // SyntaxError
```
Node's REPL reports it as an error:

```
Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token '??'
```
Also, the ts-node repl reports:
```
<repl>.ts:5:10 - error TS5076: '&&' and '??' operations cannot be mixed without parentheses.
```

title: feat: partial `v8` module support for `npm:` imports
body:
I understand that supporting Node's `v8` module is undesirable because of how funky it is.

However, there are a few things inside of it that already have Deno equivalents. I'd love to see these things be mapped, hence giving us partial support of the `v8` module when using `npm:` packages.

Concretely speaking, I am thinkg of `v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()` which could be implemented via https://deno.land/api@v1.29.1?s=Deno.memoryUsage.
author: KnorpelSenf

title: Empty test name makes language server panic, test doesn't exit
body:
**Describe the bug**

If I write a valid test and a test with an empty name and run either of them, the test never exits. Looking at Deno Language Server output, it seems to panic:
```
Executing test run with arguments: deno test --allow-all --no-check --config deno.json

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos x86_64
Version: 1.25.1
Args: ["/Users/admin/.deno/bin/deno", "lsp"]

thread '<unnamed>' panicked at 'assertion failed: `(left == right)`
  left: `0`,
 right: `1`', cli/lsp/testing/execution.rs:687:5
note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
[Info  - 3:05:25 PM] Connection to server got closed. Server will restart.
```

**To Reproduce**

1. Write two tests like this:
```ts
Deno.test("valid", () => undefined);
Deno.test("", () => undefined);
```
2. Run either test using the green triangle VSCode button.

**Expected behavior**

1. The test should exit, not run forever.
2. It should say what's wrong. When I run `deno test`, it correctly returns this:
```
Check file:///[...]/src/scratch/api.ts
Uncaught error from ./src/scratch/api.ts FAILED
valid ... cancelled (0ms)

 ERRORS 

./src/scratch/api.ts (uncaught error)
error: TypeError: The test name can't be empty
Deno.test("", () => undefined);
     ^
    at Object.test (deno:cli/js/40_testing.js:633:15)
    at file:///[...]/src/scratch/api.ts:180:6
This error was not caught from a test and caused the test runner to fail on the referenced module.
It most likely originated from a dangling promise, event/timeout handler or top-level code.

 FAILURES 

./src/scratch/api.ts (uncaught error)

FAILED | 0 passed | 2 failed (21ms)

error: Test failed
```
(That's why I report it here first)

**Screenshots**

![Test running forever](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/103094889/208306033-64cb79a5-6da9-49d3-8b3a-a317aa4465f0.png)

**Versions**

vscode: 1.74.1 (Universal) deno: 1.29.1 extension: v3.15.0
author: pkb-pmj

title: `deno install` dependencies are added to lock file of local project
body:
Reproduction:

- `deno init my_project`
- `cd my_project`
- `deno install` a module, e.g. `deno install --allow-read --allow-write -fn dci https://deno.land/x/dci@v2.7.0/mod.ts`

```
deno --version

deno 1.29.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

Probably not expected behavior because the dependencies are not specific to the local project but to the (globally) installed module.

title: Can we use a NPM package that depends on other NPM package BUT replace it with native Deno one?
body:

author: emilekm2142

title: Feature request: Scaffold a deno project from a custom template project
body:
There's already a `deno init`, which uses a hardcoded scaffolding project. I'd like to be able to start a project with deno init, but use my own custom templating project.

For instance, with an API like:

```sh
# Scaffold a project using a git template project
deno init --git https://github.com/gewoonwoutje/deno-scaffolding.git

# Scaffold a project save the template as default
deno init --save-default --git https://github.com/gewoonwoutje/deno-scaffolding.git
# next time deno init is run, the same project is scaffolded, unless ran with:
deno init --reset-default

# Scaffold a project, and save it by name 
deno init --save my-custom-scaffolding --git https://github.com/gewoonwoutje/deno-scaffolding.git
# Scaffold the project from the saved name
deno init my-custom-scaffolding
```

author: lucacasonato

title: Deno run can't resolve NPM dependencies when ran with implicit version
body:
Works

```
deno run --unstable --allow-read --allow-write https://deno.land/x/deno_changesets@0.0.1/main.ts --help
```

Doesn't work


I also can't run this without `--unstable` - is that intended or a remnant of going stable now?

```
➜  deno_changesets git:(main) ✗ deno run https://deno.land/x/deno_changesets@0.0.1/main.ts --help
error: importing npm specifiers in remote modules requires the --unstable flag (referrer: https://deno.land/x/deno_changesets@0.0.1/src/changeset.ts)
```
author: itsdouges

title: How would you independently version invdividual subdirectories to deno.land/x via Github tags?
body:
Hi there! A few things work well today when versioning (tagging) a repository:

1. A single module in a single repository
2. Multiple modules in a single repository that should be versioned together

But one thing that I'm scratching my head at currently is how best to handle independent versioning. If I only change module a, and module b has no changes, ideally I should be able to version module a by itself and module b remains untouched. 

As versioning on https://deno.land/x/ is done via git tags, I'm not sure how this could be possible, unless we could have granular control over the web hook to say only publish for xyz change 🤔. Not sure.

For context I'm interested in making a Deno native version of [changesets](https://github.com/changesets/changesets), I have a working version here https://deno.land/x/deno_changesets for top level modules only. 

Any thoughts?

title: 1.29.0 breaks Request.clone() for OPTIONS requests
body:
When I upgrade to Deno 1.29.0, my backend service can no longer handle OPTIONS requests due to a `TypeError`:

```
TypeError: Body is unusable.
    at Request.clone (deno:ext/fetch/23_request.js:486:15)
    at new DrashRequest (https://deno.land/x/drash@v2.7.0/src/http/request.ts:47:38)
    at Function.create (https://deno.land/x/drash@v2.7.0/src/http/request.ts:83:17)
    at Server.#handleRequest (https://deno.land/x/drash@v2.7.0/src/http/server.ts:282:43)
    at Server.handler (https://deno.land/x/drash@v2.7.0/src/http/server.ts:158:41)
    at Server.#respond (https://deno.land/std@0.146.0/http/server.ts:298:37)
    at Server.#serveHttp (https://deno.land/std@0.146.0/http/server.ts:340:20)
```

It's [this line](https://deno.land/x/drash@v2.7.0/src/http/request.ts?source=#L47) (`this.#original = originalRequest.clone();`) that's causing the issue. 

This seems like a very similar issue to #15858 but this is a recent regression, has a different error message, and only affects some requests (GET seems fine, OPTIONS does not).

Affects: Deno 1.29.0, 1.29.1
Doesn't affect: Deno 1.28.3 and earlier versions.

Tested on:
```
deno 1.29.0 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

title: "deno info" should ignore lock file
body:
Currently if there's a "deno.json(c)" and "deno.lock" then "deno info" will print out all npm packages that are listed in the lockfile, even if the entry point is not an npm package (nor does it have npm imports). This is unexpected behavior and led to some confusion in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17167#issuecomment-1363397034.

Since `deno info` doesn't have `--lock` flag available in this case the lockfile should be ignored.

author: sigmaSd

title: node: support socket option in `tls.connect`
body:
`tls.connect` takes `socket` and it can use that socket as a base tcp connect and start TLS on top of it. ref: https://nodejs.org/api/tls.html#tlsconnectoptions-callback

Currently std/node doesn't support this usage of `tls.connect`. This is necessary for supporting `npm:mssql`, `npm:nodemailer` etc.

related:
- https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18303
- https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/3035

title: Inspector performance profile saving hangs on sufficiently long FFI calls
body:
**Steps to hopefully reproduce:**
1. Create a Deno program that on some callback (for me this is an FFI callback but I hope an HTTP server will work) performs a 500ms+ FFI call.
2. Connect a VS Code debugger session to the Deno program.
3. Start performance profile recording
4. Trigger the long FFI call.
5. Stop performance profile recording.

**Expected result:**
The profile should be saved correctly.

**Actual result:**
The "saving" message in VS Code bottom bar never seems to finish. A new profile saving cannot be started. Disconnecting the debugger fixes the status.

**Notes:**
* The same issue does not seem to occur with Chrome debugger, so this might not actually be an issue with Deno.
* I was sometimes able to trigger this with multiple short FFI calls as well. It might also that FFI callbacks are related to this issue.

title: vs code quick fix not suggesting importing from module in import map
author: KyleJune

title: [Node Compat] `http.ClientRequest` does not emit `upgrade` event (or is buggy)
body:
Hi,

I tried to figure out how to solve https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16899, and discovered that the HTTP client of the `Node Compat` layer does not emit the `upgrade` event.

This means, that no NPM package that contains a WebSocket client will work properly.

I tried really hard to figure out what's going on, and how to fix it, but I'm not really a Typescript programmer, and the code is hard to follow for me.

It seems, the HTTP client just does not implement the event (it is implemented for the server, however).

The [`Event: 'upgrade'` example](https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#event-upgrade) in the docs of the Node.js `http` module is a good way to reproduce the issue.

You can run it, as follows:

1. Save the [`Event: 'upgrade'` example](https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#event-upgrade) in a file
2. Change the import statement at the top to `import * as http from "https://deno.land/std@0.170.0/node/http.ts";`

If you now execute the script, it will just hang. With Node.js, it prints the expected text to the terminal.

Also (because it took me bit to figure this out), here is the easiest way to debug the issue, I've found:

1. Clone the standard lib: `git clone https://github.com/denoland/deno_std`
5. Change the import statement at the top of the script to `import * as http from '/$PATH_TO/deno_std/node/http.ts'`
6. Now you can alter the stdlib code
7. The `http` module is at `node/http.ts`
author: d4h0

title: Cannot use ":" in import maps for packages
body:
I started making an import map today and was using the syntax "esm:npm-module-name". That worked for other modules too but when I tried adding packages via trailing slashes like (https://github.com/WICG/import-maps#packages-via-trailing-slashes), I started getting errors.

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "x:oak": "https://deno.land/x/oak@v11.1.0/mod.ts",
    "x:oak/": "https://deno.land/x/oak@v11.1.0/"
  }
}
```

If I try importing from the `x:oak/` directory, I get an error at runtime.

```
error: Unsupported scheme "x" for module "x:oak/mod.ts". Supported schemes: [
    "data",
    "blob",
    "file",
    "http",
    "https",
]
```

In vs code, I get a warning saying `Uncached or missing remote URL: "x:oak/mod.ts".deno(no-cache)`. If I try the quick fix, that fails. It probably fails for the same reason it fails at runtime.

To work around this issue, I decided to change to using "$esm/" and "$x/" as my prefix instead of "esm:" and "x:".

@bartlomieju suggested I open this issue and said that deno should provide an error or warning in such situation.
author: KyleJune

title: Incorrect suggestion for absolute imports in import map
body:
https://deno.land/manual@v1.29.1/basics/modules/import_maps#import-maps

This page shows an example of how to use your project root for absolute imports. It suggests the following import map.

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "/": "./",
    "./": "./"
  }
}
```

If you do this, vs code will show suggestions on all imports using an absolute path.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3241653/209613084-0765f6d9-448f-4df1-9b30-91c2eb5545a3.png)

The quick fix is wrong and will add a . to the start of the import which is incorrect. Here is the error you will get if you do that. The quick fix for this breaks it even more.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/3241653/209613233-6687a3cb-c406-4d51-88c8-56f0e4630aad.png)

It will also show a suggestion on imports starting with `./` and `../` based on that import map. When I did try those suggestions they were wrong too. It would suggest `./` to replace the whole chain of `./` and `../` before the text paths.

The expected behavior would be for the absolute import to not show a suggestion like in the first screenshot. It should just be considered valid. The same applies for import paths starting with `./` or `../`.

author: kt3k

title: Deno mixes JS and TS if shebang is used but no file extension
body:
Hi, I use Deno for OS scripting. Scripting files use shebangs but no file extension, e.g.

```
// my_script
#!/bin/env -S deno run --allow-env --allow-read
```

When I set the `shebangs` setting for JavaScript (but not for TypeScript) the language is correctly recognized as JavaScript.

```
# languages.toml
[[language]]
name = "javascript"
shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
auto-format = true
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "javascript" }

[[language]]
name = "typescript"
# shebangs = ["deno", "node"]
auto-format = true
config = { enable = true, lint = true, unstable = true }
language-server = { command = "deno", args = ["lsp"], language-id = "typescript" }
```

But error messages are TypeScript specific. So there is is strange mix of JavaScript and TypeScript functionalities.

As soon as the file is renamed to `my_script.js` (or `my_script.ts`) everything works as expected.

Also, if I wanted to use JS and TS with shebangs only there is no way the differentiate between the two (if I get it correctly). 

Maybe there should be a way to set the language in the shebang line, e.g. with

```
#!/bin/env -S deno run --allow-env --allow-read --language=javascript
```

Thanks a lot!

```js
console.log(Foo, Bar);
```

```sh
[Function: Foo] [Function: Bar]
```

```sh
[class Foo] [class Bar extends Foo]
```

Is this intentional?

title: [fmt] Supporting remove semicolons
body:
The closing comment of this issue say: https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/13616#issuecomment-1149836312

> This discussion is the perfect example why we will not be introducing additional options to the built in formatter. All source code written for Deno should use the same formatting options for consistency, and to reduce this kind of discussion. In our eyes ecosystem consistency is much more important than opinions of individuals. This is also why we limit configuration options of the runtime and type checker as much as we do.

But there are already many options:

```
--options-use-tabs
--options-line-width
--options-indent-width
--options-single-quote
--options-prose-wrap={always,never,preserve}
```

Beside the existing options, an option about semicolon is almost the only option that people want.

title: A way to get a transform stream from a Deno.Child process
body:
The `Deno.Child` should have a method to obtain from it a `TransformStream` which writes to the process's stdin and reads from its stdout. (Something like `Deno.Child.asTransform(ops)`)

Another approach would be to make `Deno.Child` itself a "`TransformStreamLike`" as someone pointed out at https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/3015#issuecomment-1358944326

For more details on my motivation for this proposal, see the description of my PR https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/pull/3015

title: When I run `deno init`, `import_map.json` is not automatically loaded.
body:
## Overview

Running `deno init` creates `deno.jsonc` at the same time.
However, this doesn't load `import_map.json` by default, so I wasted a lot of time before realizing the problem...

## Proposal

1. Add `importMap` line and create an empty `import_map.json`.

```diff
--- a/deno.jsonc
+++ b/deno.jsonc
@@ -1,4 +1,5 @@
 {
+  "importMap": "import_map.json", // <= HERE!!
   "tasks": {
     "dev": "deno run --watch main.ts"
   }
```

2. Generate the above with optional arguments.
ex: `deno init --import-map`

It's a minor issue, but please take care of this.

title: deno run - Uncaught PermissionDenied: Permission denied (os error 13) #5816
body:
deno 1.29.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4

Originally commented the following here https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/5816#issuecomment-1367713864, but then I realized this seems to be a different error and doesn't appear to have anything to do with TSL.

I was fighting and fighting with this error:
```sh
error: Permission denied (os error 13)
```

during this command:
```sh
setuidgid $MY_USER deno cache --import-map ./import_map.json --lock ./deno.lock main.ts
```

What fixed it was simply one of these:
```sh
chown $MY_USER deno.lock
```

or
```sh
chmod 666 deno.lock
```

Keep in mind `664` did NOT work. So something is requiring write permissions on the lock file.

title: How to import `*.mjs` files that are actually have `*.mts` extension?
body:
The standard TypeScript compiler (`tsc`), [since v4.7](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-4-7.html), recognizes `import "./foo.mjs"` as a TypeScript + ESM file, but Deno does not. How can I write a TypeScript + ESM file that both `tsc` and `deno` compile?

## Details

File `./foo.mts`:


```typescript
export function hello() {
    console.log("hello");
}
```

File `./index.mts`:

```typescript
import { hello } from './foo.mjs';

hello();
```

The above two files are perfectly valid in TypeScript 4.7, as `ts-node` does:
```
$ npx ts-node --esm src/index.mts
hello
```

but Deno cannot run:
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.29.1 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
$ deno run src/index.mts
error: Module not found "file:///path/to/ts-import-mjs/src/foo.mjs".
    at file:///path/to/ts-import-mjs/src/index.mts:1:23
```

Demo repo: https://github.com/gfx/ts-import-mjs

title: Support forcing colors
body:
If deno is not running in a tty there is currently no way to override it so that colors are shown

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/98bbf87742969802b392130c536454aa61a20395/runtime/js/99_main.js#L250

Some options are:

- `FORCE_COLOR` env var - this would ignore the other values and never set `noColor`
- `--color` flag - similar to `ls` and other CLI utils
  - `auto` -> current behavior
  - `never` -> same behavior as `NO_COLOR`
  - `always` -> force color on

I think it would be super helpful to have this working well on Replit as there is a large community of developers who could build projects using Deno. There are already a number of templates which could be so much more useful with the benefits of lsp.

I'd love to help figure out what the next steps here could be to get this working. I'm new to all aspects of the request, but eager to help. 😄

title: programmatic API for registry cache invalidation
body:
My team is working on an API that provides import completions based on files on disk. As the end developer CRUDs JSON files in their workspace, the API (running on localhost) updates a list of *importable* items based on those JSON files.

For instance, let's say I have a directory with a `some_file.json` and `mod.ts`. While running the local server, I should be able to import / see completions for a corresponding `some_file.ts`.

```ts
import {} from "http://localhost:8000/some_file.ts";
```

Upon deleting `some_file.json`, the local server should invalidate this import / no longer provide the corresponding completion.

Is there a standard means by which one can achieve this?

> Note: [`deno_cache`](https://github.com/denoland/deno_cache) seemingly doesn't cover this functionality

title: Dependencies not resolved when combining TypeScript generics and type imports
body:
I noticed that imports of the form `import("module").Foo<import("module").Bar>` do not resolve dependencies.

This issue can be reproduced as follows:

```ts
// child_a.ts
export type Foo<T> = T;
```

```ts
// child_b.ts
export type Bar = string;
```

```ts
// parent.ts
type A = import("./child_a.ts").Foo<import("./child_b.ts").Bar>;
// ^^^ I expected `string` but got `any`
```

Type `A` in `parent.ts` should be `string`, but is of type `any` because the dependencies are not resolved.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/40050810/210191826-f3441fb5-d146-4492-a528-4443f6b01bb6.png)

Additionally, I looked at the output of `deno info` and it should contain `child_b.ts`, but it does not.

```
> deno info parent.ts
local: C:\Users\ayame\work\deno\test\parent.ts
type: TypeScript
dependencies: 1 unique
size: 90B

file:///C:/Users/ayame/work/deno/test/parent.ts (65B)
└── file:///C:/Users/ayame/work/deno/test/child_a.ts (25B)
```

I found this at https://esm.sh/v102/@octokit/rest@19.0.5/dist-types/index.d.ts .
Potentially, many type checks in esm.sh may be disabled.

version: Deno 1.29.1
author: ayame113

title: NPM peer dependency causes double instantiation
body:
I'm trying to make [Rakkas](https://rakkasjs.org/) (a Vite-based React metaframework) run on Deno. The `rakkasjs` package has Vite as its peer dependency (`"vite": "3 || 4"`). But installing both `rakkasjs` and `vite` causes two copies of Vite in the `node_modules` directory. They are actually hardlinked but it still causes double instantiation.

For example when I run the following in an empty directory:

```sh
deno cache --node-modules-dir npm:vite@4.0.3 npm:rakkasjs@0.6.11
```

I get two (hardlinked) copies of Vite in `node_modules/.deno/vite@4.0.3` and `node_modules/.deno/vite@4.0.3_1`. This, in turn, causes Vite to get instantiated twice, once because of the direct import and once because of the indirect import via `rakkasjs`.

To see the problem you can create the following Vite config file and save it as `vite.config.mts`:

```ts
import { defineConfig } from "npm:vite@4.0.3";
import rakkas from "npm:rakkasjs@0.6.11/vite-plugin";

export default defineConfig({
  plugins: [rakkas()],
});
```

and typecheck it with `deno check --node-modules-dir vite.config.mts`. You'll get a type error because Deno resolves the peer dependency to `.../node_modules/.deno/rakkasjs@0.6.11/node_modules/vite/dist/node/index.d.ts` and the main dependency to `.../node_modules/.deno/vite@4.0.3/node_modules/vite/dist/node/index.d.ts`. These two don't even resolve to the same "realpath". The first one points to `vite@4.0.3_1` and the second to `vite@4.0.3`.

Of course the type mismatch is the lesser of the problems here. This also causes the `vite` module to be instantiated twice. I verified it by manually adding `console.log("vite:", import.meta.url)` on top of the `node_modules/vite/dist/node/index.js` file and running the program with `deno run --node-modules-dir -A npm:rakkasjs@0.6.11 --no-clear-screen`. The output is:

```
vite: .../node_modules/.deno/rakkasjs@0.6.11/node_modules/vite/dist/node/index.js
vite: .../node_modules/.deno/vite@4.0.3_1/node_modules/vite/dist/node/index.js
```

which proves that the module is instantiated twice.

I believe storing a single copy in `node_modules/.deno/vite@4.0.3` and following the symlinks when resolving packages would solve this problem.

Tested with Deno version 1.29.1.
author: cyco130

title: Integrate autobahn test suite into CI
body:
Versions 1.27 - 1.28.1 had an issue where websocket messages would not be sent in certain cases (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16511)

In _at least_ version 1.28.2 - 1.28.3 I've found that Deno's websocket implementation will send a message **twice** if the message is an `ArrayBuffer` over ~100kb. This seems to have been fixed in 1.29.0, and it seems like nobody noticed.

But in both cases I had to spend a day debugging what was happening, only to find out that Deno's websocket implementation was not working as expected. Would it make sense for Deno to introduce some more tests for the Websocket implementation, so that things like these work as expected?
author: sgwilym

title: CLI: Box-drawing characters in the permission prompt is broken
body:
# Environment

```
$ deno --version
deno 1.29.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

* OS: Ubuntu 22.04
* Terminal: Alacritty v0.11.0 or Ubuntu's bundled Terminal app
* Font: Ubuntu Mono 13


# Problem

Box-drawing characters in the permission prompt is broken.

## Screenshot



![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54318333/210249694-8cf7da65-6865-4adc-bd28-b8a40cf714cb.png)


# Cause

The box-drawing character is printed in italic style.

[runtime/permissions/prompter.rs](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/f729576b2db2aa6ce000a598ad2e45533f686213/runtime/permissions/prompter.rs#L204-L208)

```rust
    let msg = format!(
      "   ├ Run again with --allow-{} to bypass this prompt.",
      name
    );
    eprintln!("{}", colors::italic(&msg));
```

This code should be like:


```rust
    let msg = format!(
      "Run again with --allow-{} to bypass this prompt.",
      name
    );
    eprintln!("   ├ {}", colors::italic(&msg));
```

(not tested)

author: ealib

title: Support for SvelteKit
body:
Prompted by discussion on Discord I tried to get SvelteKit working in Deno.

After an hour of debugging I found the main blocker: by default SvelteKit installed Node adapter, which does a bit of setup that is not needed in Deno and makes the project crash, namely:
https://github.com/sveltejs/kit/blob/e7bc0be2b25aff5ac151e3d83b771ad80cac1ab8/packages/kit/src/exports/node/polyfills.js#L6-L28
which installs polyfills from `undici` for various Web APIs like `fetch` or `Response`. These objects work slightly differently in Deno and arguably installing them in Deno is not needed. 

I think the way forward would be to get a Deno dedicated adapter that would essentially be a noop as there's no need to change anything to make SvelteKit work.

Of course it would be very nice if `deno run -A npm:create-svelte@latest` would set up a project in a way that requires no changes (namely making specifiers use `npm:` prefixes).

title: NPM compatibility - Crypto module cannot be resolved
body:
On usage of the NPM Module [@azure/storage-blob](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@azure/storage-blob) in the following context:

```
import * as AzureStorageBlob from "npm:@azure/storage-blob"

const BlobClient = AzureStorageBlob.BlobServiceClient.fromConnectionString(configs.azureconnectionstring);
const containerClient = BlobClient.getContainerClient(configs.azure_container);

//fetch image
//...


let uuid = crypto.randomUUID();
const response = await fetch(image.url);
await containerClient.getBlockBlobClient(`${user.id}/${uuid}${image.url.substring(image.url.lastIndexOf("."))}`).upload(response.body, response.body.length);
```

the following error is thrown:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) Error: Cannot find module 'crypto'
Require stack:
```

author: macdevign

title: `Warning: Not implemented: ChildProcess.prototype.disconnect` when using Prisma
body:
When using `prisma generate` we can see in the logs at the bottom
```
Warning: Not implemented: ChildProcess.prototype.disconnect
```

Full example
```
deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma generate --data-proxy
Environment variables loaded from .env
Prisma schema loaded from prisma\schema.prisma
napi_add_env_cleanup_hook is currently not supported
✔ Created ./package.json

added 2 packages, and audited 3 packages in 7s

found 0 vulnerabilities

added 2 packages, and audited 5 packages in 9s

found 0 vulnerabilities

✔ Installed the @prisma/client and prisma packages in your project

✔ Generated Prisma Client (4.9.0-dev.15 | dataproxy) to .\node_modules\@prisma\client in 164ms
You can now start using Prisma Client in your code. Reference: https://pris.ly/d/client
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client'
const prisma = new PrismaClient()

To use Prisma Client in edge runtimes like Cloudflare Workers or Vercel Edge Functions, import it like this:
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client/edge'

You will need a Prisma Data Proxy connection string. See documentation: https://pris.ly/d/data-proxy

Warning: Not implemented: ChildProcess.prototype.disconnect
```

What triggers it is the telemetry in the Prisma CLI which does the following
```
  // Spawn the child to send telemetry request
  const child = spawn(state)
  child.unref()
  // Closes the IPC channel between parent and child
  child.disconnect()
```

And the warning can be found at https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/b4239898d6c6b4cdbfd659a4ea1838cf4e656336/node/internal/child_process.ts#L273

It does not impact the functionality as far as we know, but it could be scary for users who might think something is broken (although it is not).

Is it planned to be implemented, maybe? I'm curious to know what you think about this.

author: Lesiuk

title: Attempts while getting deno installed on Windows / git bash
body:
I'm trying to install deno on git bash, and I failed after multiply attempts and researching.

First, I tried to run `curl -fsSL https://deno.land/x/install/install.sh | sh` directly in git bash, and this is what I got:
```
curl: (60) SSL certificate problem: unable to get local issuer certificate
More details here: https://curl.se/docs/sslcerts.html

curl failed to verify the legitimacy of the server and therefore could not
establish a secure connection to it. To learn more about this situation and
how to fix it, please visit the web page mentioned above.
```
I asked a friend who is using Linux at this point to check if the site's certificate is really valid, and soon we confirmed that **the certificate problem is actually originated from Microsoft's library**, not from deno's side.

And then I tried something like `curl -k` and `curl --insecure` , but git bash's curl ignores these parameters.

After some researching, I tried using `winpty -Xallow-non-tty curl -kfsSL https://deno.land/x/install/install.sh | sh` instead and this time I got:
```
sh: line 1: #!/bin/sh: No such file or directory
sh: line 4: $'\E[0K': command not found
sh: line 5: set: - invalid option
set: usage: set [-abefhkmnptuvxBCEHPT] [-o option-name] [--] [-] [arg ...]
```
And after more researching, I found someone said I should try installing deno on the Windows system itself, and git bash will be able to access it, then I tried `irm https://deno.land/install.ps1 | iex` on PowerShell, and I got:
```
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
  0     0    0     0    0     0      0      0 --:--:-- --:--:-- --:--:--     0
curl: (35) schannel: next InitializeSecurityContext failed: Unknown error (0x80092012) - 撤銷功能無法檢查憑證的撤銷。
tar.exe: Error opening archive: Failed to open 'C:\Users\user\.deno\bin\deno.zip'
Remove-Item : 找不到 'C:\Users\user\.deno\bin\deno.zip' 路徑，因為它不存在。
位於 線路:39 字元:1
+ Remove-Item $DenoZip
+ ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : ObjectNotFound: (C:\Users\user\.deno\bin\deno.zip:String) [Remove-Item], ItemNotFoundExc
   eption
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : PathNotFound,Microsoft.PowerShell.Commands.RemoveItemCommand
```
After some researching, I realized that there's no easy way to make PowerShell to bypass the "certificate problem", and maybe because I'm not very familiar with PowerShell, I couldn't find a solution by myself and decided to just start asking others for support.
author: BolnayaTPrincessa

title: NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY seemingly ignored on subsequent runs of `deno cache`
body:
Deno 1.29.1
macOS 11.6.4

I added my first `import` for an `npm:` specifier and ran `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://registry.mycompany.com/path/to/destination/ deno cache --node-modules-dir` to download a local copy through my company's Artficatory (public NPM is blocked) and it worked fine. However, when I run `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://registry.mycompany.com/path/to/destination/ deno cache ...` again, I get `BadCertificate` errors with additional output showing that it seems to be trying to use the default NPM registry again.

```
Sending fatal alert BadCertificateg/<npm-package> (0/#)
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
Sending fatal alert BadCertificate
error: failed reading lockfile '/path/to/project/deno.lock'

Caused by:
    0: Error getting response at https://registry.npmjs.org/<dependency-package>
    1: error sending request for url (https://registry.npmjs.org/<dependency-package>): error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
    2: error trying to connect: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
    3: invalid peer certificate contents: invalid peer certificate: UnknownIssuer
```

After some trial and error, I found that I could run the `deno cache` command again if I deleted the lock file (which oddly doesn't capture the registry URLs of the NPM packages).

Not captured in the above output as the first line because it gets overwritten by the error output is:

```
Download https://registry.npmjs.org/<dependency-package>
```

Which seems to confirm that it is falling back to the default registry. When I run the command after deleting the lock file, that first line of output shows the correctly overridden registry URL.

I did some digging into the Deno codebase to find how the NPM registry is set and why it would fail on subsequent runs of `deno cache` with `NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY` set and I found [this](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/10e4b2e14046b74469f7310c599579a6611513fe/cli/npm/registry.rs#L230-L262) where the Registry URL is produced. I don't know Rust at all so figuring out where this is called in relation to the `deno cache` command proved to be challenging, but I noticed the warning when using the old private ENV var `DENO_NPM_REGISTRY`. I deleted the lock file, switched the `deno cache` command to use the private one, and saw the warning defined in `RealNpmRegistryApi::default_url()` in the output. Then when I ran it a second time, I got the above error output with no sign of the ENV var warning, which seems to indicate that the registry override is not being retrieved the same way on subsequent runs of `deno cache` when a lock file is present.

My full `deno cache` command (with a dummy Registry URL) that I committed to a Task command is:

```
$ NPM_CONFIG_REGISTRY=https://registry.mycompany.com/path/to/destination/ deno cache --reload --lock-write --node-modules-dir app.ts
```

author: milaz

title: How to prevent deep dependencies duplication?
body:
The following situation is quite common:

- I'm using library A
- I'm using library B that is also using library A

There are very high chances that the two pinned versions of A are not the same, which will cause a duplication of the`A` library.

If A is a big library, it becomes problematic. Furthermore, there are libraries that do not work when imported multiple times, like React and Yjs.

How to deal with this issue in Deno?

title: `deno install` ignores `--no-config` flag
body:
I noticed a significant performance degradation of one of my CLI tool which is installed with `deno install`.

```sh
deno install -f --name=hacks -A --no-config src/cli.ts
# ✅ Successfully installed hacks
```

```sh
time hacks > /dev/null
# hacks > /dev/null  0.17s user 0.08s system 85% cpu 0.289 total

time hacks > /dev/null
# hacks > /dev/null  0.17s user 0.10s system 61% cpu 0.432 total

time hacks > /dev/null
# hacks > /dev/null  0.17s user 0.10s system 67% cpu 0.392 total
```

## `deno run` without `--no-config`

The running times are similar to the ones when `deno run` has no `--no-config` flag passed:

```sh
time deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.17s user 0.10s system 61% cpu 0.430 total

time deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.16s user 0.10s system 64% cpu 0.395 total

time deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.16s user 0.10s system 64% cpu 0.401 tota
```

## `deno run` with `--no-config`

`deno run` is consistently faster with `--no-config` flag:

```sh
time deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.09s user 0.04s system 83% cpu 0.157 total

time deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.09s user 0.04s system 84% cpu 0.147 total

time deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null
# deno run -A --no-config src/cli.ts > /dev/null  0.09s user 0.04s system 80% cpu 0.167 total
```

## Deno version

```
deno 1.29.2 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

title: Deno test --watch flag only works when specifying a path
body:
Specifying a file isn't required to run deno test.  It will run all the tests in the directory I'm running the command in.

```
$ deno test -A
Check file:///home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app/env_test.ts
running 10 tests from ./env_test.ts
...
```

I found that if I add the `--watch` flag, it wasn't finding any of the files.

```
$ deno test --watch -A
Watcher Test started.

ok | 0 passed | 0 failed (0ms)

Watcher Test finished. Restarting on file change...
```

I found that if I add a path like `.`, it is able to find the test files like it can without the `--watch` flag.

```
$ deno test --watch -A .
Watcher Test started.
running 10 tests from ./env_test.ts
...
```

I believe that the test command should be able to find the test files without a path when using the `--watch` flag, like it can without the `--watch` flag.

```
    at CommandChild.pipedStdoutBuffer (file:///V:/dax/src/command.ts:545:17)
This error was not caught from a test and caused the test runner to fail on the referenced module.
It most likely originated from a dangling promise, event/timeout handler or top-level code.

 FAILURES

./mod.test.ts (uncaught error)

FAILED | 0 passed | 68 failed | 90 filtered out (816ms)

error: Test failed
```

At the minimum, we should remove all those "cancelled" test outputs and maybe only say how many tests were ignored in a file with matches. For files with zero matches, we could maybe have some progress message output that gets overwritten and finalizes saying how many files were ignored.

title: Add command-line arguments `--no-console`
body:
On Windows systems (I don't know what happened with Linux and OS X), running Deno automatically activates a console window. If I want to release a GUI program via `deno compile`, this causes problems

I can implement a GUI program by calling the `win32 API` via `FFI`, which is low feasibility, but at least a possibility
author: tianxiadys

title: `MessageEvent.source` is always null
body:
In the browser console you can type:
```js
const e = new MessageEvent("message", {source: window})
```
And then `e.source` will be the current browser window. But if you do the same in Deno it will be `null`.

I'm writing a test for browser code and wanted to use the existing built-in MessageEvent.

[`MessageEvent.source` on mdn](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/MessageEvent/source)

author: sant123

title: WebSocket messages are not being sent (again)
body:
It looks like Deno's WebSocket implementation is dropping messages (again), much like #16511.

I have created a limited reproduction, along with a description of the problem: https://github.com/sgwilym/deno-ws-bug-repro.

In this reproduction, a file's contents are sent in pieces over a WebSocket using a readable stream from `Deno.open`. A server logs the size and SHA-256 hash of each piece it receives.

If the reproduction is run locally, no messages are dropped.

However, when the same server is deployed to Fly.io using the Dockerfile in the reproduction case, or to Deno Deploy ([playground here](https://dash.deno.com/playground/useful-dog-20)), only the first four of eight expected messages come through, stopping when the total size of messages reaches 256kb.

`socket.onerror` is never triggered by either side.

This error seems to happen with all 1.29 releases.
author: sgwilym

title: feature request: prompt to add import map entry if deno finds bare import.
body:
This proposal tries to improve the usability of `npm:` compat feature.

---

If the module resolver finds a bare specifier (and it can'be resolved using import maps), then prompt the user to add import map entry of the form `"foo": "npm:foo@[latest-version-of-foo]"` (or `"foo": "npm:foo@^[latest-version-of-foo]"`) and if the user answers 'yes', then keep resolving it using the updated import map.

(If there's no import map applied, then CLI automatically creates it and specify it in `deno.json`'s `importMap` entry. If `deno.json` doesn't exist, then CLI also automatically create it.)

---
Example:

If the user runs the below script:

example.js
```ts
import express from 'express';
const app = express();
app.listen(3000, () =>
  console.log('Example app listening on port 3000!'),
);
```

Then, it prompts like:

```shellsession
$ deno run example.js
⚠️  ┌ Deno tries to import from "express" (example.js:1:22)
   └ Do you want to map "express" to "npm:express@^4.18.2"? [y/n] (y = yes; n = no) >
```

If the user answers 'yes', the code runs using the 'npm:' dependency:

```shellsession
$ deno run example.js
✅ Added mapping from "express" to "npm:express@^4.18.2" in "path/to/import_map.json".
Example app listening on port 3000!
```

author: dsherret

title: Deno does not enforce `const`
body: Hello esteemed Deno authors, I was playing around in Deno today and caught this: Is this intended behavior? Am I missing a flag?
author: sgammon

title: use URLPattern for allow-net permission
body:
We should use URLPattern for the allow-net permission to allow more granular control.

This would fix #5242 and #6532
author: crowlKats

title: deno panics when using prisma cli commands
body:
I am using a manually built version of deno which includes this [commit](https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/dd2829be0c1f5c40ec38a045ea0a14bec34a82c5). 
The issue **does not** occur with the latest release (1.29.2)

## Steps to reproduce

- Run `deno run --allow-read --allow-env --allow-write npm:prisma@^4.5 init`
- Run `deno run -A --unstable npm:prisma format`

```
var set and include the backtrace in your report.
  ```

author: dsherret

title: serde_v8 gives unhelpful errors for some APIs
body:
Consider code below:
```js
Deno.dlopen("./foo.so", { "add": { paramaters: ["i64"], result: "i64" } });
```

There's a type there - `paramaters` instead of `parameters`.

If we run with `--check` flag it's okay, because TS will give us a nice error:
```
❯ deno run --unstable -A --check foo.ts
Check file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo.ts
error: TS2322 [ERROR]: Type '{ paramaters: string[]; result: "i64"; }' is not assignable to type 'ForeignFunction<readonly NativeType[], NativeResultType, boolean> | ForeignStatic<NativeType>'.
  Object literal may only specify known properties, and 'paramaters' does not exist in type 'ForeignFunction<readonly NativeType[], NativeResultType, boolean> | ForeignStatic<NativeType>'.
Deno.dlopen("./foo.so", { "add": { paramaters: ["i64"], result: "i64" } });
                                   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    at file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo.ts:1:36

```

But running without type checking:
```
error: Uncaught TypeError: Error parsing args at position 0: data did not match any variant of untagged enum ForeignSymbol
Deno.dlopen("./foo.so", { "add": { paramaters: ["i64"], result: "i64" } });
     ^
    at new DynamicLibrary (deno:ext/ffi/00_ffi.js:276:39)
    at Object.dlopen (deno:ext/ffi/00_ffi.js:374:12)
    at file:///Users/ib/dev/deno/foo.js:1:6

```

It's a guessing game 🤷 if you don't know what's wrong then you can't really say how to fix it. Ideally the error would say something like: "`paramater` is not recognized option".
author: bartlomieju

title: `deno install` does infer executable name from redirect
body:
If you access an install behind a link shortener at `https://tinyurl.com/get-started`, but the redirect target is `https://deno.land/x/mymod/bin/my-cmd.ts` It will use `get-started` as the bin name, not `my-cmd`.

Also, in a related case, if you have a vanity install domain like `https://get-my-cmd.com` where there is no path at all in the initial url, and it redirects to `https://deno.land/x/mymod/bin/my-cmd.ts` it throws:

```
error: An executable name was not provided. One could not be inferred from the URL. Aborting.
```

title: npm:mocha - Fails when running in parallel mode
body:
## Problem
Fails when running mocha in parallel mode
```sh
deno run -A npm:mocha@10.2.0 --parallel 'test.mjs'
```
## Reproduction

### test.mjs
```js
import chai from "npm:chai";
const assert = chai.assert;

describe("Example", () => {
  it("test", () => {
    assert.equal(1, 1);
  });
});
```
## Error Output
```sh
Warning: Not implemented: process.connected

error: could not find npm package for 'file:///Users/xxxx/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/mocha/10.2.0/lib/nodejs/worker.js'
```

title: inconsistent config detection between windows & ubuntu
body: While working on https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17338, a test was failing on windows because an assertion was failing due to an actual value having the config message, whereas the expected didnt, so i updated it and the test passed on windows, however fails on ubuntu due to the opposite issue: it didnt detect a config file.

`constants.ts:198  Uncaught ReferenceError: Deno is not defined`

- Change supabase version
- Change deno version

author: ghost

title: suggestion: use `main` branch for Deno standard library module
body:
Deno's standard library uses the canary release of the Deno runtime [in CI](https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/main/.github/workflows/ci.yml). Doing so provides the benefit of catching early regressions that could be made in the Deno library by causing the CI to fail in the standard library.

However, this repo does not do the same back. Instead, this repo uses a version of the standard library a few versions back and has to be updated manually now and again. The pitfall of this method is that regressions can come up in the standard library that affects a "stable" version of the Deno runtime, affecting users.

I suggest that the Deno runtime repo instead uses the main branch of the standard library as its submodule. Doing so would shorten this feedback loop between the Deno runtime and standard library and catch cross-repo regressions before releases are published.

This is safe enough to do as long as PR merges are frozen when both repos versions are published, which I believe is the case. In other words, both repos end up using the release version code of each other even though they point to the other repo's main branch.

References:
* denoland/deno_std#3111
* #17414
* denoland/deno_std#3112

CC @kt3k
author: iuioiua

title: Add microbenchmarks for ext/webidl
body:
We do not have a microbenchmark we can point contributors to run. See PRs https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16407 and https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16594

webidl is in a lot of hot path so its important that we do not regress there.
author: littledivy

title: Add timeout to `wait_contains()` in watcher tests
body:
## Problem

A common failure indicator of `watcher_tests.rs` is that a test case gets stuck.  
Affected test cases can be debugged by rerunning tests with `--nocapture` and carefully comparing output to expected output.  
This is not possible in CI, and inefficient on developer machines.

## Steps to reproduce

Change the `s` argument of any `wait_contains()` call and run the relevant test case.

## Suggested solution

Add a timeout, say 60 seconds.  
When the timeout expires, abort the test case and log `s` (The text being waited for).

Bonus points if `stdout` and `stderr` can be logged, this would be especially useful for failures that only occur in CI.
