author: riefer02

title: Node compatibility: "Uncaught Error: Not implemented: crypto.Cipheriv"
body:
**Describe the bug**

Trying to use https://github.com/codetheweb/tuyapi

Attempting to connect to a device using protocol v3.3 triggers this stack-trace

    at notImplemented (https://deno.land/std@0.170.0/node/_utils.ts:23:9)
    at new Cipheriv (https://deno.land/std@0.170.0/node/internal/crypto/cipher.ts:140:5)
    at Object.createCipheriv (https://deno.land/std@0.170.0/node/crypto.ts:187:10)
    at F._encryptPre34 (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:1741)
    at F.encrypt (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:1699)
    at C._encodePre34 (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:8787)
    at C.encode (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:8678)
    at v.get (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:11788)
    at v._finishConnect (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:14825)
    at Socket.<anonymous> (https://esm.sh/v102/tuyapi@7.5.1/deno/tuyapi.js:2:16523)

**Steps to Reproduce**

Bit awkward as you need a v3.3 physical "smart" device (e.g. a smart lightbulb).  This is reliably triggered using the "basic usage" example in https://github.com/codetheweb/tuyapi

**Expected behavior**

crypto.Cipheriv implemented

**Environment**

$ deno --version
deno 1.29.3 (release, aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
author: matt1

title: Unexpected parse error inside deno REPL
body:
This piece of code
```jsx
> function mergeSort(arr){
     if( arr.length <= 1) return arr
     let left = mergeSort( arr.slice( 0 , arr.length/2 ) )
```
keeps giving `parse error: Expected '}', got '<eof>' at 3:50`, can't seem to go past it without using `Ctrl+S`.
And it works fine in node
author: Shifta-Robel

title: v1.29.3 panic in Docker: "OS can't spawn worker thread: Operation not permitted"
body:
Hi, my Dependabot found a rust panic when updating a project from `denoland/deno:alpine-1.29.2` to `denoland/deno:alpine-1.29.3`.

The issue happened when running a deno command on CircleCI's remote docker with Docker Engine version `20.10.7`. It happens every time (tried 3 runs).
I successfully worked around the panic by updating Docker Engine to `20.10.18`.
I'm not sure what reproducability is like elsewhere, though I imagine having the correct docker engine version would be step one.

I'm reporting this because it's a panic and panics are bugs :) but updating docker is a reasonable workaround for many, I'd hope.

```
Step 5/5 : RUN deno cache *.ts
 ---> Running in 4636805d6740
Download https://deno.land/x/digest_fetch@v1.2.1/mod.ts

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64

title: Unwrapping DirEntry can make a panic
body:
## Related
https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/df4d0c55c09a1dc6f227b0bce399202160693445/runtime/ops/fs.rs#L1302

## `deno --version`
```
deno 1.29.2 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

Using [ArchWSL](https://github.com/yuk7/ArchWSL) (which uses tmpfs) in Windows 10.

## How did I get this
In short: `deno eval 'Deno.readDir("/path/to/dir/that/contains/very/long/filename")'` can make the panic.

Some filesystem cannot store very long filename:
```
$ touch 안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요
touch: cannot touch '안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세 요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요안녕하세요': File name too long
```

But in Windows 10, it's completely valid filename length. Accessing from WSL to there makes a cute panic:

```
$ RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno eval 'Deno.readDir("/mnt/c/path/to/that-folder")'

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: linux x86_64
Version: 1.29.2
Args: ["deno", "eval", "Deno.readDir(\"/mnt/c/path/to/that-folder\")"]

thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at 'called `Result::unwrap()` on an `Err` value: Os { code: 5, kind: Uncategorized, message: "Input/output error" }', runtime/ops/fs.rs:1299:27
stack backtrace:
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

Node.js makes a Promise exception correctly:

```
$ node -e 'fs.promises.readdir("/mnt/c/path/to/that-folder")'
node:internal/process/promises:289
            triggerUncaughtException(err, true /* fromPromise */);
            ^

[Error: EIO: i/o error, scandir '/mnt/c/path/to/that-folder'] {
  errno: -5,
  code: 'EIO',
  syscall: 'scandir',
  path: '/mnt/c/path/to/that-folder'
}

Node.js v19.4.0
```

Similar Rust code that makes same error:

```rust
fn main() {
    let path = std::env::args_os()
        .skip(1)
        .last()
        .expect("Put a folder path to argument");

    let entries = std::fs::read_dir(path).expect("Error at read_dir call");

    for entry in entries {
        entry.expect("Error at DirEntry unwrapping"); // Error causes from here
    }
}

// $ cargo run /mnt/c/path/to/that-folder
//
// thread 'main' panicked at 'Error at DirEntry unwrapping: Os { code: 5, kind: Uncategorized, message: "Input/output error" }', src/main.rs:10:15
// note: run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=1` environment variable to display a backtrace
```

title: Deno panics when using prisma
body:
Deno version - `12.9.4` (built from source)
Prisma version - `4.8.1`

# schema.prisma
```prisma 
generator client {
  provider = "prisma-client-js"
  previewFeatures = ["deno"]
  output          = "./generated/client"
}

datasource db {
  provider = "postgresql"
  url      = env("DATABASE_URL")
}

model user {
  id    Int     @id @default(autoincrement())
  email String  @unique
  name  String?
}
```

# run.ts
```typescript
import { createRequire } from "https://deno.land/std@0.171.0/node/module.ts";
const require = createRequire(import.meta.url);

const { PrismaClient } = require("./generated/client");
const prisma = new PrismaClient();

await prisma.user.findMany().then(() => console.log("ok"));

title: `Deno.execPath()` resolves symlink on Linux (doesn’t on Mac)
body:
```sh
$ echo "console.log(Deno.execPath())" > foo.ts
$ deno compile -A foo.ts
$ ./foo
/src/foo
ln -s foo bar
$ ./bar
/src/foo
```

On macOS the output is `/src/bar`. Resolving the symlink is not typical, many tools use the name of the symlink to change behavior, which is what we are trying to do.

I will happily PR a fix, just point me at the relevant source file and where I should add a regression test. Thanks.

```
$ deno --version
deno 1.29.2 (release, aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
```

title: `deno run` doesn't support disabling colors
body:
Here's a screenshot of my text editor (Neovim):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7507990/213555164-8edabb5e-12e5-4ae8-8b60-32e74b31e8a9.png)

What you're seeing is me running `:!deno run -A %` in Neovim, which runs the current file (via `%`) within the editor. This is useful for immediate, quick feedback, but I'm unable to use this because `deno run` doesn't have a `--disable-colors` option. As a result, the color escape sequences are embedded in standard output.

I think it would be useful to add a `--disable-colors` option.

title: Current Roadmap
body:
We’ll be writing up a more detailed explanation of this soon, but for now take a look at these examples to see how these features play together:

1. [node-compat-app](https://github.com/denoland/experimental-deno-specifiers-example/tree/main/01-deno-node-compat-app). `package.json` specifies dependencies, which can be imported from npm or deno.land/x. `"type": "module"` is necessary. Lockfile used is `deno.lock`. `deno task` reads from deno.json first, then falls back to `package.json`.

1. [deno-canonical-app](https://github.com/denoland/experimental-deno-specifiers-example/tree/main/02-deno-canonical-app). `deno.json` supports the import map keys. Can specify an npm package via npm specifiers or deno.land/x package via deno specifiers. Lockfile used is deno.lock. main.ts is identical in this to the first example.

1. [deno-lib](https://github.com/denoland/experimental-deno-specifiers-example/tree/main/03-deno-lib)

Feedback is welcome!

Tasks:

- [x] Option to embed an import map in `deno.json` (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15816#issuecomment-1399451393)
- [x] Support package.json auto-discovery and bare specifiers (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17491)
  - [x] Run scripts from package.json https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17492
- [x]  Node built-in modules are available to be imported with `node:` prefix -- ex. `import { readFile } from "node:fs";` (#16223)
- [x] Unflag `npm:` specifier usage in http/https imports (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17455#issuecomment-1399453228)
- [x] When built-in Node.js modules are imported without `node:` prefix a friendly error message is shown (#17493)
- [ ] When global Node.js objects are referenced, Deno will error out with a friendly error message (https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17494)
- [x] Built-in support for semvered specifiers on the deno.land/x registry #17495
- [x] Import map unfurling on deno.land/x publish (https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/3)
- [x] ~~Redirect module path to exact mod.ts/js index.ts/js file https://github.com/denoland/website_feedback/issues/10~~
- [x] Deprecate `deno bundle` - Ref https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15463 but unlike this issue states, we will not add a replacement but instead allow this to be done in userland.
- [x] Continued compatibility layer improvements
- [x] Besides features above, we are committed to fixing various issue related to npm compatibility as well as LSP/editor integrations - see https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15960.

title: Friendly error message when importing node built-in modules without a `node:` prefix
body:
*This depends on support for `node:` modules in #16223*

When built-in Node.js modules are imported without `node:` prefix a friendly error message is shown:

```js
import { readFile } from "fs";
```

```
Add a "node:" prefix to the following import:
  import { readFile } from "fs";
Like this:
  import { readFile } from "node:fs";
```

This is only relevant to the user code - dependencies imported with `npm:` specifiers will have these modules available in both forms.
author: dsherret

title: When global Node.js objects are referenced, Deno should error out with a friendly error message
body:
*Extracted from https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17475*

Objects that will be handled: ~~`process`~~, `global`, `Buffer`, `setImmediate`, `clearImmediate`, `module`, `exports`.

```
Uncaught ReferenceError: process is not defined. 
    To use Node's process object in Deno, add this to `<filename>`:
        import process from "node:process";
```

For `global` the hint in the error should be `globalThis.global = globalThis`.
author: dsherret

title: Built-in support for semvered specifiers on the deno.land/x registry
body:
*Extracted from https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17475 and rewritten with added context*

**Problem**

On the deno.land/x registry, it's difficult for module authors to publish a module and specify the version constraint of their dependencies. This often leads to module consumers ending up with duplicates of almost the same dependency (ex. 1.2.1 and 1.2.2 of the same module).

The recommended solution today is for module consumers to examine their dependency tree, then do the "module deduplication" themselves. This is hard for several reasons:

1. Users need to remember to inspect their dependency tree.
1. Users need to know if having multiple versions of a dependency in their tree will cause issues. When it does, this can be really painful and hard to figure out.
1. Usually module authors have the best context of what versions of their dependency work well with their module.

**Proposed solution**

To allow users and module authors to easily optionally depend on version constraints of dependencies, built-in support of semvered specifiers for the deno.land/x registry could be added in order to provide a recommended solution to this problem.

```ts
// https://deno.land/x/example/mod.ts
import { compile } from "deno:path_to_regexp@^6.2/mod.ts";
```

The deno CLI could analyze these semvered specifiers and based on them, create an internal implicit import map:

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "deno:path_to_regexp@^6.2/mod.ts": "https://deno.land/x/path_to_regexp@v6.2.1/mod.ts"
  }
}
```

This would solve all the issues above out of the box and allow specifying more terse imports for the deno.land/x registry.

Other tools wishing to use code that has these specifiers, could run them via an import map that could be generated from the output of `deno info --json`.

- The registry needs an endpoint that specifies all the versions of a package. We already do this for LSP auto-completions, but we should have something explicitly for this.
- `deno:` or something else?
- How could we support other registries? Environment variable for sure, but maybe a way to do this in the specifier itself (ex. `deno://crux.land/path_to_regex@^6.2`)?

title: compiled binaries no longer respect permissions from `deno compile`
body: It used to be the case that binaries compile with `deno compile -A ...` would pass on the "allow all" property to the binaries that are created. This was changed in a recent build of Deno and impacts the experience we ship to our users. I believe this change should be reverted. If not, we should at least have a way to opt-in to giving compiled binaries permissions by default.

author: rherrmann

title: Suggestion: Provide LSP FixIts for import map imports
body:
As part of the ongoing drive to drive down the number of keystrokes needed to import dependencies (and, personally, to avoid the need for custom `deno:` imports) I suggest that Deno adds FixIts / auto-completion to import map imports.

An import map could (presumably) be inferred by the JSON file type and the file name:
1. If file name is `deno.json` then the `"imports"` or maybe `"importMap/imports"` object (pending #15816 ) is an imports object.
2. If a `deno.json` is detected and the file name matches `"importMap"` key's value, the file is an import map and the `"imports"` object is an imports object.

When writing a key (quoted or unquoted) in the imports object, or an empty value, offer to fill in the value with a registry URL of that module, based on the defined import hosts (also suggest `npm:`, `esm.sh`?, `skypack.dev`? if key is found in NPM?).

The same could be offered as a CLI command under `deno cache`, eg.
```
deno cache --import-map importMap.json --import-map-write myModule
```
(Naturally, detect import map through `deno.json` if present.)

Since here `myModule` is not a URI, Deno could check configured import hosts for a module by this name and prompt user to choose which one they want to import from.

The same could also be offered as a FixIt when user writes a plain import in a JS / TS file.
author: aapoalas

title: Improve error messages related to FS access
body:
When a file is not found with, at least, `Deno.realPathSync`, the following error is printed:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) NotFound: No such file or directory (os error 2)
      return Deno.realPathSync(join(baseDir, filePath));
                  ^
    at Object.realPathSync (deno:runtime/js/30_fs.js:146:16)
```

There are two unfortunate parts with this error message:

First and foremost: "No such file or directory" tells the error, but not what it's about. Seeing this error, I have no idea what file or directory the error relates to. It would generally be preferable (at least in my opinion) for the error message to contain not only what it was trying to do (the function being called) and the error it ran into (NotFound) but also what it was operating on (the name of the file).

The second much less important unfortunate part is the `Object.realPathSync`. The `Deno` object should probably have its `ToStringTag` symbol get reassigned so that the stack would actually print `Deno.realPathSync`, just for consistency's sake.
author: aapoalas

title: Suggestion: Provide LSP and CLI commands to detect duplicate dependencies
body:
It would be useful if Deno provided commands in LSP and maybe in CLI as well to detect duplicate dependencies based on semver-like parts in dependency URL.

Something like:
```
deno info --duplicates main.ts
```

and similarly in LSP with a command similar to `Cache Dependencies`, eg. `Deduplicate Dependencies`. The output could be eg. a ready-made import map, or directly mutating an existing import map, or possibly a "snippet" of an import map `"imports"` object that can be copy-pasted into an existing import map.

title: 🐛 POSIX: `deno upgrade` leaves temporary directory with large files in */tmp*
body:
When upgrading from Deno v1.29.4 to v1.30.0, the upgrade failed because of tmpfs exhaustion. In tracking the issue, it was apparent that when using `deno upgrade`, Deno leaves a temporary directory in */tmp* with the downloaded zip file and its expansion. At least one prior version left the same residual files. As it didn't fail at the time, I can't determine which earlier versions are affected (maybe all of them).

```shell
$ ls -Al .tmpQ2GQJW
total 141460
-rwxr-x--x 1 root administrators 105538456 2023-01-16 16:42 deno
-rw-r----- 1 root administrators  39309948 2023-01-25 18:42 deno.zip
```
Obviously, these files are relatively large and this can exhaust the */tmp* storage quite quickly if not removed promptly. Please clean up after the upgrade is completed.

title: Make `deno fmt` options CLI args less verbose
body:
Related to #17529

The `deno fmt` options are too verbose. We should alias flags like `--options-indent-width 4` to without `options-` like `--indent-width 4` then deprecate the options flags in 2.0.

1. This change requires only modifying https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/cli/args/flags.rs 
1. The `--options-indent-width`-like args should not show up in the help anymore (be hidden), but should still work if used
author: dsherret

title: `deno upgrade` does not provide any feedback about failing to upgrade a symlinked executable
body:
May be related to #14272. ## Background A while ago, I just plopped the `deno` binary down in my `~/.local/lib`, where I usually put stand-alone binaries local to my user profile, and symlinked it into my `~/.local/bin` like so: ```sh ln -s ~/.local/lib/deno ~/.local/bin/ ``` The few times I've poked at it, `deno` has been working flawlessly in this setup. ## Repro Now I wanted to `deno upgrade`, but it does not appear to be happening, and there is no feedback about any kind of failure: ```sh deno --version; deno upgrade && deno --version ``` ```plain deno 1.24.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) v8 10.4.132.20 typescript 4.7.4 Looking up latest version Found latest version 1.30.0 Checking https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v1.30.0/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip 37.6 MiB / 37.6 MiB (100.0%) Deno is upgrading to version 1.30.0 Archive: /tmp/.tmpsHluxS/deno.zip inflating: deno Upgraded successfully deno 1.24.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) v8 10.4.132.20 typescript 4.7.4 ``` Note how the `deno upgrade` output says "Upgraded successfully", when in fact it is evidently false. It is also unclear where the extracted binary has gone, as it sure has not replaced the old one. ## Resolution Ideally this would be resolved so that Deno does actually update the binary. If that is not possible, it should at the very least provide the feedback that it has failed to do so. ### Workaround *(For anyone that may be looking for a solution, not having figured this out yet.)*
Exists:

Since the downloaded zip was still where `deno upgrade` reported to have put it, I was able to manually unzip it over the old executable: ```sh unzip -fo /tmp/.tmpsHluxS/deno.zip -d ~/.local/lib/ && deno --version ``` ```plain Archive: /tmp/.tmpsHluxS/deno.zip inflating: /home/chris/.local/lib/deno deno 1.30.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) v8 10.9.194.5 typescript 4.9.4 ``` Not optimal, but at least now I do have the latest release on my system.

## OS key|value -:|- arch|x86_64 kern|5.15.0-58-generic distro|Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS

title: fetch() won't work inside docker container: denoland/deno:debian
body:
I'm facing a problem with fetch(), this code working fine on local Mac.
But won't work when running inside docker container using **denoland/deno:debian** image behind **Traefik** reverse proxy

``` typescript
fetch('http://localhost:9000/path') // Work! -> hit using localhost 
fetch('https://appdomain.com/path') // Not Work! -> hit using domain, it should route to it self (localhost:9000) by Traefik
```

``` console
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: error sending request for url (https://appdomain.com/path/): error trying to connect: tcp connect error: Connection timed out (os error 110)
at async mainFetch (deno:ext/fetch/26_fetch.js:267:14)
at async fetch (deno:ext/fetch/26_fetch.js:491:9)
```

Update:
1. Sample code:

``` typescript
async function main() {
  console.log('Calling fetch...')
  // const result = await fetch('https://dummyjson.com/products/1') // Pointing to another domain OK!
  const result = await fetch('https://appdomain/path/') // Pointing to self using domain os error 110 above
  console.log('result', result)

  setTimeout(main, 5000)
}

main()
```

``` console
HTTP Server started ~ http://localhost:9000/
Calling fetch...

error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: error sending request for url (https://appdomain.com/path/): error trying to connect: tcp connect error: Connection timed out (os error 110)
at async mainFetch (deno:ext/fetch/26_fetch.js:267:14)
at async fetch (deno:ext/fetch/26_fetch.js:491:9)
```

title: `Deno.watchFs()` is returns `serde_v8 error: ExpectedString`
body:
When I run `Deno.watchFs(undefined)`, it returns `serde_v8 error: ExpectedString`.

```sh
> Deno.watchFs(undefined)
Uncaught TypeError: Error parsing args at position 0: serde_v8 error: ExpectedString
    at new FsWatcher (deno:runtime/js/40_fs_events.js:19:23)
    at Object.watchFs (deno:runtime/js/40_fs_events.js:64:12)
    at <anonymous>:2:6
> 
```

I think we should validate `""`,`undefined`, `null` and throw error.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/7f38f30a5c0eb06c5b85d1c7ac26a480c210aab6/runtime/js/40_fs_events.js#L64

I will create a PR if my opinion is accepted :)

title: "unexpected end of file" error for some requests using fetch
body:
Hello!

I'm reporting an issue that I also found on Node.js. I am having a bit of trouble explaining the issue there (https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/46359) but I think I finally found the root cause, at least in Node. You can look at the Node.js issue because I think many things there are useful to understand the issue I'm creating here.

Actually I recommend reading that issue first, it could provide some context that might be missing here, and why I'm assuming it's a gzip encoding issue (or why I even show gzip here at all).

Here's my environment:

```
deno 1.30.0 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4
Darwin MacBook-Pro-de-Ricardo.local 22.2.0 Darwin Kernel Version 22.2.0: Fri Nov 11 02:03:51 PST 2022; root:xnu-8792.61.2~4/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000 arm64
```

I start a new repl session on Deno, and for this particular URL this happens:

```
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> let x = await fetch("https://elza-c332f.firebaseapp.com/__/auth/handler.js")
console.log(await x.text())
Uncaught TypeError: error decoding response body: unexpected end of file
    at async readableStreamCollectIntoUint8Array (deno:ext/web/06_streams.js:822:21)
    at async consumeBody (deno:ext/fetch/22_body.js:232:11)
    at async <anonymous>:3:13
```

Ok, the first thing that the Node.js team thought is "the server is just returning a truncated response". If this issue is happening both on Deno and Node, it's easy to believe this to be true. But if you think about the wider context, this is a Firebase response. Just imagine if *Firebase*, a google-backed service, widely used everywhere in the world, would start returning a truncated script file! This is Firebase Auth, many services would stop working correctly. It's a massive problem that would've been investigated for at least 2 days now by the Firebase devs. And this is just not happening, everything on their servers is looking OK (their SDK isn't, which is partly the reason why I ended up here).

And indeed, browsers deal with this URL just fine:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4030858/215145657-ead3f853-5814-4c46-8db6-f168ea16963b.png)

Python also has no issue at all decompressing this content:

```
>>> import requests; x = requests.get("https://elza-c332f.firebaseapp.com/__/auth/handler.js", {'accept-encoding': 'gzip'})
>>> x.headers
{'Connection': 'keep-alive', 'Content-Length': '85989', 'Cache-Control': 'max-age=1800', 'Content-Encoding': 'gzip', 'Content-Type': 'text/javascript; charset=utf-8', 'Strict-Transport-Security': 'max-age=31556926; includeSubDomains; preload', 'Accept-Ranges': 'bytes', 'Date': 'Fri, 27 Jan 2023 15:18:10 GMT', 'X-Served-By': 'cache-cgh11125-CGH', 'X-Cache': 'HIT', 'X-Cache-Hits': '1', 'X-Timer': 'S1674832691.732797,VS0,VE1', 'Vary': 'x-fh-requested-host, accept-encoding', 'alt-svc': 'h3=":443";ma=86400,h3-29=":443";ma=86400,h3-27=":443";ma=86400'}
>>> len(x.content) == 274237
```

However, gzip also has the same problem as deno & node:

```
$ curl --http1.1 -o compressed -H 'accept-encoding: gzip' https://elza-c332f.firebaseapp.com/__/auth/handler.js
# downloads 85,989 bytes

$ gzip -c -d compressed | wc -c
gzip: compressed: unexpected end of file
gzip: compressed: uncompress failed
  262144
```

If I take this compressed file that curl downloaded and try decompressing it on some online decompressing websites, I get mixed results:
 
 - https://extract.me/ just gives me a truncated response, there's 12kb missing from the result.
 - https://gzip.swimburger.net/ works just fine, all 274237 bytes are there.

So this file is definitely decompressable, all the bytes seem to be there. But some implementations are not being able to decompress it for some reason, and this includes the gzip command line utility, node, deno, and potentially others.

You might ask: Why is he trying to download this file from node? The answer is that I'm gonna run some things on Vercel, Firefox and Safari are not dealing with all the auth redirects the same way Chrome does, some local storage/cookie things are not happening, and I am proxying the request through Vercel so that the auth and app domain are the same. This worked using a lower-level node API that doesn't download it in gzipped format (no accept-encoding: gzip). 

The only thing I ask from the Deno team is to at least take an interested, serious look at this. Maybe the issue is simply fixable by using a newer version of zlib, if that's what you guys are using?
author: ricardopieper

title: Incorrect behavior of node:vm.runInThisContext()
body:
**Describe the bug**

The node:vm.runInThisContext() function doesn't behave correctly on deno.

Node:
`vm.runInThisContext("let p = 12"); console.log(p)`
12

Deno:
`vm.runInThisContext("let p = 12"); console.log(p)`
Uncaught ReferenceError: p is not defined
    at <anonymous>:3:13

On deno this function is implemented as ` return eval.call(globalThis, this.code);` (https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/blob/main/node/vm.ts#L14).
It is not possible with eval to define local variables (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/eval).

In the past this didn't cause any issues for me since ` Deno.core.evalContext()` provided an equivalent API.
Sadly this API was dropped in deno version 1.30.

`const _tmp_err = Deno.core.evalContext(code)[1];
if (_tmp_err !== null)
throw _tmp_err.thrown;`

The behavior of the function should be implemented using ` Deno.core.evalContext()`.

**Steps to Reproduce**

Start deno
> import * as vm from "node:vm";
> vm.runInThisContext("let p = 12"); console.log(p)

**Expected behavior**

`12` should be printed to the console.

**Environment**

- OS: Archlinux
- deno version: 1.30
- std version: 0.174.0
author: BerndAmend

title: Use name of config file in `deno install`
body:
It seems `deno install` generates a `.my-module.tsconfig.json` regardless of what config file is passed by `--config`. It would be clearer if it used the name of the config file instead, e.g. `.my-module.deno.jsonc`.

This is a purely cosmetic issue. The functionality works as expected.

## Steps To Reproduce

```
deno install -n my-module --config=deno.jsonc src/main.ts
```

Note: It seems the config file is not auto-discovered yet, see https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17572

## Expected Result

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --config /Users/foo/.deno/bin/.my-module.deno.jsonc 'file:///Users/bar/baz/src/main.ts' "$@"
```

## Actual Result

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --config /Users/foo/.deno/bin/.my-module.tsconfig.json 'file:///Users/bar/baz/src/main.ts' "$@"
```
author: vwkd

title: `install` doesn't auto discover config file
body:
It seems `deno install` currently doesn't auto-discover a `deno.json` or `deno.jsonc` config file.

This is suggested in the help entry for `deno install -help`.

```txt
    -c, --config <FILE>
            The configuration file can be used to configure different aspects of
            deno including TypeScript, linting, and code formatting. **Typically
            the configuration file will be called `deno.json` or `deno.jsonc`
            and automatically detected; in that case this flag is not necessary.**
            See
            https://deno.land/manual@v1.30.0/getting_started/configuration_file
```

## Steps To Reproduce

```
deno install -n my-module main.ts
```

## Expected Result

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --config /Users/foo/.deno/bin/.my-module.deno.json 'file:///Users/foo/my-module/main.ts' "$@"
```

## Actual Result

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --no-config 'file:///Users/foo/my-module/main.ts' "$@"
```

title: Dynamic import of npm packages that depend on the Node API fails
body:
## Environment ```shellsession $ deno --version deno 1.30.0 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu) v8 10.9.194.5 typescript 4.9.4 ``` ## Details * `main.ts` ```ts import * as cp from "https://deno.land/std/node/child_process.ts"; import { createProtocolConnection, Logger, InitializeRequest } from "npm:vscode-languageserver-protocol"; import { StreamMessageReader, StreamMessageWriter } from "npm:vscode-languageserver-protocol/node.js"; class NullLogger implements Logger { error(_message: string): void { } warn(_message: string): void { } info(_message: string): void { } log(_message: string): void { } } const svc = cp.spawn("deno", ["lsp"]) const clientConn = createProtocolConnection( new StreamMessageReader(svc.stdout), new StreamMessageWriter(svc.stdin), new NullLogger(), ); clientConn.listen(); console.log( await clientConn.sendRequest( InitializeRequest.type, { rootUri: 'file:///home/test', processId: svc.pid, capabilities: {}, workspaceFolders: null, }, ), ) ``` * `dynimport.ts` ```ts import { toFileUrl } from "https://deno.land/std/path/mod.ts"; await import(toFileUrl('/path/to/main.ts').href) ``` `deno run -A main.ts` is working.
`deno run -A main.ts`
```shellsession $ deno run -A main.ts { capabilities: { textDocumentSync: { openClose: true, change: 2, save: {} }, selectionRangeProvider: true, hoverProvider: true, completionProvider: { resolveProvider: true, triggerCharacters: [ ".", '"', "'", "`", "/", "@", "<", "#" ], allCommitCharacters: [ ".", ",", ";", "(" ] }, signatureHelpProvider: { triggerCharacters: [ ",", "(", "<" ], retriggerCharacters: [ ")" ] }, definitionProvider: true, typeDefinitionProvider: true, implementationProvider: true, referencesProvider: true, documentHighlightProvider: true, documentSymbolProvider: { label: "Deno" }, workspaceSymbolProvider: true, codeActionProvider: true, codeLensProvider: { resolveProvider: true }, documentFormattingProvider: true, renameProvider: true, foldingRangeProvider: true, workspace: { workspaceFolders: { supported: true, changeNotifications: true } }, callHierarchyProvider: true, semanticTokensProvider: { legend: { tokenTypes: [Array], tokenModifiers: [Array] }, range: true, full: true }, inlayHintProvider: true, experimental: { denoConfigTasks: true, testingApi: true } }, serverInfo: { name: "deno-language-server", version: "1.29.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)" } } ^C ```
But, `deno run -A dynimport.ts` returns an import error. ```shellsession $ deno run -A dynimport.ts error: Uncaught Error: Cannot find module 'util' Require stack: - /home/tennashi/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vscode-jsonrpc/8.0.2/lib/node/ril.js - /home/tennashi/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vscode-jsonrpc/8.0.2/lib/node/main.js - /home/tennashi/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vscode-jsonrpc/8.0.2/node.js - /home/tennashi/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vscode-languageserver-protocol/3.17.2/lib/node/main.js - /home/tennashi/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/vscode-languageserver-protocol/3.17.2/lib/node/main.js at Function.Module._resolveFilename (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:637:17) at Function.Module._load (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:469:29) at Module.require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:680:21) at require (deno:ext/node/02_require.js:820:18) at Object. author: tennashi

title: Update wgpu for TersorflowJS WebGPU support
body:
In mid 2022, I wanted to migrate my tensorflow.js projects into Deno, only to find that their WebGPU backend is mysteriously failing https://github.com/tensorflow/tfjs/issues/6842.

It turns out `naga` was interpreting wgsl in lexical order https://github.com/gfx-rs/naga/pull/2075, luckily the new module level scoping has been released in `naga` 0.11 and `wgpu` 0.15.

Is it possible for Deno to update `wgpu` to 0.15?

title: regression: `--watch=foo` only reloads on the first change to `foo`
body:
This is a regression between v1.29.2 and v1.29.3; likely caused by #15466.

To reproduce:
```sh
echo 'a' > file.txt
echo 'console.log(await Deno.readTextFile("file.txt"))' > cat.ts
deno run -A --watch=file.txt cat.ts &
echo 'b' >> file.txt
echo 'c' >> file.txt
echo 'd' >> file.txt
```

On v1.29.2, each echo causes the watcher to trigger; on v1.29.3 and v1.30.0, only the first echo does.

title: npm compat tracking issue
body:
Tracking issue for npm/node compat issues in `std/node`

Note: Currently we are moving Node polyfill implementation from `std/node` (this repository) to `ext/node` (Deno main repository). ref. https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17724

Please wait for a while to contribute to node compat layer

## High priority items
- [ ] socket option in tls.connect https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17842 (affects `npm:mssql`)
- [ ] http(s) requests can't upgrade to websocket requests
  - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17846 https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17847 (affects `npm:discord.js`)
  - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17496 (affects `npm:puppeteer`, `npm:ws`)
  - https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16899 (affects `npm:playwright`)
- [x] http.ClientRequest.setTimeout https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17852 https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18198 (affects `npm:aws-cdk-lib`)
- [x] crypto.Sign https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17843
- [x] crypto.createSecretKey https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17844 (affects `npm:next-auth`, `npm:jsonwebtoken`)
- [x] `npm:aws-cdk init` throws TypeError https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/2916 (affects `npm:aws-cdk` (windows)
## Medium priority items
- [x] crypto.generateKeyPairSync https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18651 (affects `npm:mineflayer`)
- [ ] missing APIs in `node:os` https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17850
- [x] X509Certificate https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18455
- [x] node:vm.runInThisContext() https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17853
- [x] TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'remoteAddress') #18325
- [x] Use of global process in _events.mjs https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17854 (affects `npm:bullmq`
- [x] primisify(execFile) https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/3145 
- [x] crypto.Cipheriv https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/17848 (affects [tuyapi](https://github.com/codetheweb/tuyapi)) @kt3k
- ...

#### Note about priorities

The priorities are higher when the issue affects popular npm modules (when used via `npm:` specifier).

---

<!---
#### Note to contributors

You can replace the internal node shim implementation by the command:

```shell
DENO_NODE_COMPAT_URL=file:///path/to/your/deno_std/ deno run script.ts
```

By setting `DENO_NODE_COMPAT_URL` env var, Deno uses your local deno_std/node as Node APIs, and you can test, check, or inspect the internals by modifying your local deno_std.
--->

title: `deno install` doesn't recoginze remote import_maps in `deno.json`
body:
I started using `imports` in `deno.json` since it was announced for Deno 1.30, but I realized today that when you install something with `deno install` the runner won't recoginze the import.

Example running this ([repo](https://github.com/timharek/wcarbon):
```sh
deno install --allow-net=api.websitecarbon.com \
   -n wcarbon https://deno.land/x/wcarbon/src/cli.ts
```

Will tell you something like this:
```sh
$ wcarbon netlife.com
error: Relative import path "std/flags/mod.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
    at https://deno.land/x/wcarbon@v1.4.0/deps.ts:1:2
```

Even though the project has specified the import in its [`deno.json`](https://github.com/timharek/wcarbon/blob/main/deno.json#L29)

author: juanfra684

title: deno run --watch=./ stops watching after the first restart
body:
I've created a minimal repo to demonstrate the issue: https://github.com/shareup/deno-watch-stops-watching-bug

Deno version: 1.30.0
OS: macOS 13.2, aarch64

The first time a file outside the module graph is updated, the server restarts. The second and further times, files outside the module graph do not trigger a server restart anymore. Files in the module graph always trigger a restart.

This worked correctly, I believe, in version 1.27.x – the server would restart if any watched file changed, even if it was outside the module graph, across server restarts.

author: watzon

title: serde & serde_json dependency version locks cause problems along side newer crates
body:
deno/Cargo.toml includes the following dependencies:

```
serde = { version = "=1.0.149", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "=1.0.85"
```

These crate versions are almost 6 months old.  Unfortunately some newer crates (for example, https://crates.io/crates/async-openai) depend on newer versions of serde & serde_json, preventing those crates from building alongside Deno.
author: luketpeterson

title: `deno fmt` should inherit `.editorconfig` style
body:
### Background
Just give a try to no-nester style indent,  aka linux kernel style.
Found `denoland.vscode-deno` not inherit .editorconfig style, which `esbenp.prettier-vscode` inherit it by default.

### Actual
- `esbenp.prettier-vscode` inherit .editorconfig style
- commandline `prettier ./reproduce.ts` inherit .editorconfig style
- `denoland.vscode-deno` **not** inherit .editorconfig style
- commandline `deno fmt ./reproduce.ts` **not** inherit .editorconfig style

### Expected
- `esbenp.prettier-vscode` inherit .editorconfig style
- commandline `prettier ./reproduce.ts` inherit .editorconfig style
- `denoland.vscode-deno` not inherit .editorconfig style
- commandline `deno fmt ./reproduce.ts` not inherit .editorconfig style

### Reproduce
- Adjust `.editorconfig` with
```
indent_size = 8
```

- Run `prettier -w reproduce.ts`
- Expect indent_size is 8, and actual indent_size is 8.
- Run `deno fmt reproduce.ts`.
- Expect indent_size is 8, but actual indent_size is 2.


### Related
https://github.com/denoland/vscode_deno/issues/803

title: `node:dns` doesn't support `timeout` options
body:
**Describe the bug**
I'm trying to use deno for DNS health check which will respond to a DNS failure. But, I found that the node/dns module handles dns failure poorly, which takes around 15s to report a resolve failure, and this is too slow for my use case. I tried to use a custom resolver like below:
```
const resolver = new DNS.promises.Resolver({ timeout: 1000, tries: 1 })
```

But a failure is still taking around 15 seconds to report. Both the callback and the promise api has this issue.

**Steps to Reproduce**

<!--
Example
1. Create a file with the contents ...
2. Create a script with the contents ...
3. Execute the command ...
4. See error
-->

- Create a js file with the following contents:

```js
import * as DNS from 'https://deno.land/std@0.175.0/node/dns.ts'
const resolver = new DNS.promises.Resolver({ timeout: 1000, tries: 1 })
resolver.setServers(['127.0.0.1']) // You should not have real DNS server on this machine
try {
    await resolver.resolve4('mirror.archlinuxarm.org')
  } catch (e) {
    console.log('dns-fail')
}
```

- Run it with deno: `deno run --allow-all index.js`

- The `dns-fail` will print after 15 seconds.

**Expected behavior**

<!-- A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen. -->

The `dns-fail` message should be printed much earlier. In this case, around 1s as the timeout is set this way.

The behavior of original nodejs is slightly different: the promise will be rejected immediately with `ECONNREFUSED` .  But for my use case a timeout is good enough.

**Environment**

<!-- please complete the following information -->

- OS: macOS 13.2
- deno version: 1.30.0
- std version:  0.175.0

  console.log(ctx.protocol) // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'encrypted')
  console.log(ctx.origin) //  TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'encrypted')
  console.log(ctx.href) // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'encrypted')

The output of other attributes of ctx is the same as that used under nodejs. The above several nodejs are normal, but deno reports an error

  console.log(ctx.ip) //  TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'remoteAddress')

```

author: bartlomieju

title: lsp: Quick fix for redirected imports always adds double quotes
body:
## Steps to reproduce behavior

Consider the following code:

```ts
// @deno-types='https://esm.sh/@types/node'
import 'https://esm.sh/colors';
```

Deno's LSP suggests (justifiably) that both URLs are redirected, and provides a quick fix to "update specifier to its redirected specifier".

However, when applying the fixes, the following code is received:

```ts
// @deno-types='"https://esm.sh/v106/@types/node@18.11.18/index.d.ts"'
import "https://esm.sh/colors@1.4.0";
```

Which is invalid, due to the two pairs of quotes enclosing the `@deno-types` URL.
Also note that the `import` statement changed from single quotes to double quotes.

A similar error happens with triple-slash reference `types` directive:

```ts
/// <reference types=""https://esm.sh/v106/@types/node@18.11.18/index.d.ts"" />
import "https://esm.sh/colors";
```

## Expected behavior

Applying the quick fix doesn't add an extra `"`, and the original quotes are preserved.

## Version

Deno v1.30.0

## Suggested fix

It seems like the extra `"` are added here:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a635c9700c0b99c3ad128c21727fdcfb23bad72f/cli/lsp/diagnostics.rs#L757

I suggest removing them completely:

```diff
-                  new_text: format!("\"{}\"", data.redirect),
+                  new_text: data.redirect.into(),
```

This should fix the case of `@deno-types` and triple-slash directives (as their `diagnostic.range` includes just the URL without its enclosing quotes), but should regress the case of `import` statements (as their `diagnostic.range` includes the URL and its enclosing quotes). Therefore I suggest excluding the quotes from `specifier_range` of `import`/`exports` statements:

https://github.com/denoland/deno_graph/blob/ffc6fca09466b07d8e37e560b6546acb6cbd5a48/src/ast.rs#L322

However this might have some far-reaching consequences that I'm not sure about.
author: relrelb

title: We need an easy way to make HTTP(S) requests through proxy
body:
NodeJS' `node-fetch` supports `agent` field in its options where different agents from external modules which implement different proxy protocols may be provided. There is no such mechanism in Deno.
Because Deno tries to maintain full compatibility with browser JS and the standard, you will not modify v8's fetch, as far as I understand. However, the inability to quickly and conveniently make an HTTP request through a proxy is a big omission for Deno, which positions itself, among other things, as a general purpose scripting tool.
In fact, we can use system sockets for this, but then we will have to rewrite HTTP, SSL, TLS, SOCKS4 and SOCKS5 protocols from scratch. It would be nice to somehow solve this problem.

**UPD.**
Sorry, question removed. I found [Deno.createHttpClient](https://deno.land/api@v1.30.2?s=Deno.HttpClient&unstable=). By the way, it would be nice to point out [here](https://deno.land/api@v1.30.2?s=Deno.CreateHttpClientOptions&unstable=) that this works not only with HTTP proxies, but also with SOCKS.
author: lem0nify

title: `Deno.bench` no longer accepts iteration params
body:
Hi! It looks like the `warmup` & `n` params were removed from `Deno.bench` which could control the number of iterations that run. The last version to include them looks to be [`v1.20.6`](https://deno.land/manual@v1.20.6/tools/benchmarker#iterations-and-warmup-runs).

Was there a reason for this or could they be re-added? Happy to submit a PR to add them back.

author: CreatCodeBuild

title: Deno's date vs. curl's date
body:
Why is it that curl and deno on my system have different opinions on the current datetime?

Here I'm setting up a simple TCP server and interacting with it using curl.

```ts
// example1.ts
const conn = await Deno.listen({ port: 8888 }).accept()
const buf = new Uint8Array(1024)
const n = await conn.read(buf)
console.log("before write: ", new Date())
await conn.write(buf.subarray(0, n!))
console.log("after write:  ", new Date())
conn.close()
```

Deno thinks the time is **20:04:07.404**

```console
$ deno run -A example1.ts
before write:  2023-02-04T20:04:07.404Z
after write:   2023-02-04T20:04:07.405Z
```

cURL thinks it's **21:04:07.013409**

```console
$ curl --trace-time --trace - "http://localhost:8888"
21:04:07.012645 == Info:   Trying 127.0.0.1:8888...
21:04:07.013365 == Info: Connected to localhost (127.0.0.1) port 8888 (#0)
21:04:07.013409 => Send header, 78 bytes (0x4e)
0000: 47 45 54 20 2f 20 48 54 54 50 2f 31 2e 31 0d 0a GET / HTTP/1.1..
0010: 48 6f 73 74 3a 20 6c 6f 63 61 6c 68 6f 73 74 3a Host: localhost:
0020: 38 38 38 38 0d 0a 55 73 65 72 2d 41 67 65 6e 74 8888..User-Agent
0030: 3a 20 63 75 72 6c 2f 37 2e 38 35 2e 30 0d 0a 41 : curl/7.85.0..A
0040: 63 63 65 70 74 3a 20 2a 2f 2a 0d 0a 0d 0a       ccept: */*....
21:04:07.015181 == Info: Received HTTP/0.9 when not allowed
21:04:07.015203 == Info: Closing connection 0
curl: (1) Received HTTP/0.9 when not allowed
```

What gives?
author: denizdogan

title: Dynamic imports require net access even when they are unused
body:
```js
if (false) {
	await import("https://deno.land/std@0.176.0/node/module.ts")
}
```
```
> % deno run foo.js
⚠️  ┌ Deno requests net access to "deno.land".
   ├ Requested by `import()` API
   ├ Run again with --allow-net to bypass this prompt.
   └ Allow? [y/n] (y = yes, allow; n = no, deny) > 
```

I feel like it's only supposed to request access the moment the `await import()` is called, or am I missing something?

For the moment a workaround is to keep the import specifier in a separate variable:
```js
if (false) {
	const str = "https://deno.land/std@0.176.0/node/module.ts";
	const mod = await import(str);
}
```
But this removes type safety and turns `mod` into `any`.

The server will not be restarted. See the output for more information. ``` ### To Reproduce 1. Start a new Deno project 2. In `.vscode/settings.json` make sure you have: ```json { "deno.enable": true, "deno.lint": true, "deno.importMap": "./import_map.json" } ``` 3. Create `import_map.json` with the following contents: ```json { "grammy": "https://deno.land/x/grammy@v1.13.1/mod.ts", "@grammyjs/parse-mode": "https://deno.land/x/grammy_parse_mode@1.5.0/mod.ts" } ``` 4. In `main.ts` put the following: ```ts import { Bot, Context } from 'grammy' import { hydrateReply, parseMode } from '@grammyjs/parse-mode' ``` 5. Attempt to cache the missing dependencies. `grammy` works fine, but `@grammyjs/parse-mode` causes the crash. ### System Distro: EndeavourOS (Arch x86_64 on kernel 6.1.9-arch1-1) Deno: 1.30.2 Code version: 1.75.0
author: watzon

title: deno-fmt Allow imports to be customized to a single line imports
body:
Hello guys, 
I would like to propose a new rule or to modify the existing one for `lineWidth` to allow imports to be preserved to a single line.

1. Proposal for new rule: as described in this [PR](https://github.com/dprint/dprint-plugin-typescript/pull/376) from `dprint`
2. Propasal for changing existing rule as per a similar rule from  `eslint` 

```
   "lineWidth": [
      {
        "width": 80,
        "ignorePattern": "^import"
      }
    ]
```
I understand no-one wants to add new rules or configs to an opinionated formatter, but this doesn't need to be a new rule necessarily.

title: Don't rely on "globalThis" in the runtime code
body: Working on https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17648 @crowlKats and I spotted a few places where the runtime code relies on `globalThis` variable. This is brittle, as nothing is stopping users to `delete globalThis`. We should update all the code to use saved references to `globalThis` instead.

title: prettier npm module fails with "Maximum call stack size exceeded"
body:
`prettier` module [published on NPM](https://www.npmjs.com/package/prettier) is a popular code formatter (~29 million downloads a week. It is necessary for us to migrate to deno away from NodeJS without breaking our workflow. Currently, deno fails on pretter use even with trivial code.

### Repro steps
Contents of `repro.ts`:
```ts
import prettier from 'prettier';
// The code being formatted is just empty string
// The filename is there just to indicate it is JS as opposed to HTML or CSS or TSX or something else
prettier.format('', {filename: '1.js'});
```
Contents of `repro.json`:
```json
{
  "imports": {
    "prettier": "npm:prettier@latest"
  }
}
```
Output of `deno run --unstable --import-map=repro.json --allow-read --allow-env repro.ts`:
```bash
user@pc:~/deno-repro$ deno run --unstable --import-map=repro.json --allow-read --allow-env repro.ts 
No parser and no filepath given, using 'babel' the parser now but this will throw an error in the future. Please specify a parser or a filepath so one can be inferred.
error: Uncaught RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded
    at Object.get (deno:ext/node/01_node.js:35:26)

title: Remove "emit" and "map" from `deno info --json` output
body:
We should remove both "emit" and "map" from the output of `deno info --json`.

```jsonc
{
      "dependencies": [
        // etc...
      ],
      "kind": "esm",
      "local": "C:\\Users\\david\\AppData\\Local\\deno\\deps\\https\\deno.land\\08d9a8e609cf8dd460c936bbd31868d54b9fce2740085096564b2fada923e719",
      "emit": "C:\\Users\\david\\AppData\\Local\\deno\\gen\\https\\deno.land\\08d9a8e609cf8dd460c936bbd31868d54b9fce2740085096564b2fada923e719.js",
      "map": null,
      "size": 14342,
      "mediaType": "TypeScript",
      "specifier": "https://deno.land/x/oak@v11.1.0/util.ts"
    }
```

**Reason to remove "map"**

It is not used at all. This is always `null`.

**Reason to remove "emit"**

1. It is extremely unreliable to depend on the emit file and nobody should be doing this. It might be out of date from the source and be completely wrong. Instead, people should get the local file then use deno_emit to get the emit https://github.com/denoland/deno_emit
2. How we store the emit should be private data only known to the CLI. This will allow us to make performance optimizations.

Getting the emit meta-data with cache could be a single file read instead of multiple for example:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1609021/217916168-253a81c4-2075-4923-9ef3-31d8d0c85bd7.png)
author: dsherret

title: Consider storing remote file cache metadata in same file as text
body:
We're currently loading two files for each cached remote file.

One file contains the `CachedUrlMetadata`:

```rs
/// Cached metadata about a url.
#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub struct CachedUrlMetadata {
  pub headers: HeadersMap,
  pub url: String,
  #[serde(default = "SystemTime::now")]
  pub now: SystemTime,
}
```

The other file contains the actual remote file source text.

We store them separately, which means we have to do two file reads per remote file when loading.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1609021/217975788-ad2c562c-0813-4da0-9a10-563c9f48fac2.png)
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1609021/217975599-8abd934a-1556-4e97-9f2c-1d801bba4aad.png)

If we stored them together in the same file, then we could reduce the number of file reads we have to do from two to one per file.

This has some implications for `deno info --json` because we expose the "local" file. Related #17703
author: dsherret

title: Turn back on dlintPreferPrimordials

I disabled this lint check in #17692 because there were over 200 new errors with the dlint upgrade. We should re-enable this.

title: Failed detection of symlinks with `Deno.stat[Sync]` as well as `Deno.lstat[Sync]`
body:
Thank you all for your hard and excellent work on Deno and the related ecosystem!

The `Deno.stat` and `Deno.statSync` api's, when provided both hard and soft file links, produce `Deno.FileInfo` objects where `isSymlink` is `false`. This is unexpected and _probably not right_ (unless I'm missing something).

I have not tested this on Windows or Mac, so it may be a Linux only issue for all I know.

**Minimal Reproduction Repository**

https://github.com/andrewbrey/deno_symlink_stat_issue

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/34140052/218174160-1e47b3d4-a012-48e7-aa91-6e16a53e0c63.png)


**Expected behavior**

The `Deno.FileInfo` object produced by `Deno.stat[Sync]` should indicate that `isSymlink` is `true` for a link.

**Notes / Environment**

```
deno --version
deno 1.30.3 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4

OS: Pop!_OS 22.04 LTS x86_64 (Linux)

VS Code Version: 1.75.1
```

title: Panic on Deno.server with a HEAD request
body:
Using the following code:

```ts
async function handler() {
  let response = await fetch('https://deno.land')
  let body = await response.text();
  return new Response(body)
}

Deno.serve(handler, { hostname: '127.0.0.1', port: 4500 });
```

Performing a HEAD request using:
```
curl http://127.0.0.1:4500 -I
```

Will result in Deno crashing:

```
============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this

title: `new Date('YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss')` is `Invalid Date`
body:
### Deno
```ts
$ deno
Deno 1.30.0
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> new Date('2023-01-25 13:13:47')
Invalid Date
```

### Node
```sh
$ node
Welcome to Node.js v19.5.0.
Type ".help" for more information.
>  new Date('2023-01-25 13:13:47')
2023-01-25T04:13:47.000Z
```
### Firefox
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/54838975/218264164-cbb41977-17fc-4ba0-bbbe-37bcad989131.png)

i know it's not correct ISO 8601 format, but is this behavior documentated?

title: denols fails to restart in nvim
body:
Good morning everyone.

I'm using nvim in linux with lspconfig. It provides useful commands like `:LspRestart`.

The thing is, if I do an `:LspStop` or an `:LspRestart`, `:LspStart` doesn't work anymore. Here are the logs.

```
Output of :LspRestart

[ERROR][2023-02-12 11:52:10] .../vim/lsp/rpc.lua:733	"rpc"	"deno"	"stderr"	"shutdown request received, shutting down\n"
[ERROR][2023-02-12 11:52:10] .../vim/lsp/rpc.lua:733	"rpc"	"deno"	"stderr"	"exit notification received, stopping\n"
```

If I interact with the lsp I get this log:

```
[ERROR][2023-02-12 11:52:46] .../vim/lsp/rpc.lua:733	"rpc"	"deno"	"stderr"	"language server has exited\n"
```

If I try to manually start denols, I get no logs.

I'm sorry if I'm reporting this to the wrong repo. If so, should I report it to the lspconfig repo?

Thanks for the deno runtime, I'm really liking it :smile:
author: alarbada

title: Deno can panic on import of 'npm:ws:@latest' and similar invalid modules
body:
The following line crashed deno:
```js
import 'npm:ws:';
```

### Explanation

This error occurs in `cli/npm/registry.rs` in `get_package_url`. Code extracts package name from 'npm:ws:' as `ws:` and attempts to `self.base_url.join(name).unwrap()`. After `.join()` the URL turns into WebSockets URL without a host, so `.unwrap()` fails.

### Example output
```

exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> import a from 'npm:ws:';

at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.

var set and include the backtrace in your report.

title: Deno std does not import process
body:
Node has a global `process` module/variable which does not need importing but can be imported from `process`:
```js
import process from 'node:process';
```
In deno (as of `1.30.0`), one does need to import it. Turns out, some of deno's std modules use `process` but do not explicitly import it.

## Repro steps:
This repro attempts to trigger a warning by opening the same file a bunch of times, but instead causes an exception to be thrown `Uncaught ReferenceError: process is not defined`.
### `demo.js`
```js
import fs from 'fs';

for (let i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
  fs.watchFile('./deno.json', {}, () => {});
}
```

### `deno.json` 
```json
{ "imports": { "fs": "node:fs" }}
```

### Run command
```
deno run --allow-read=deno.json demo.js
```

author: Antonio476587

title: Binaries output by `deno compile` don't pass strict MachO validation on macOS
body:
Apple even warns in the manpage that `--strict` is a moving target; however, other major language binaries don't exhibit this issue:

```sh
$ echo 'console.log("hello")' >foo.js
$ deno compile -o foo foo.js
Compile file:///Users/jacob/foo/foo.js
Emit foo
$ ./foo
hello
$ codesign -fv -s - foo
foo: replacing existing signature
foo: main executable failed strict validation
```

Google searching, but no actual exploration of the header, suggests that having the [wrong segment last](https://alexomara.com/blog/adding-a-segment-to-an-existing-macos-mach-o-binary/) is one of the common issues. I'm not sure I have the deep knowledge to dig further into this, but I'll look into as my time permits.

title: 'connection closed before message completed' with fetch
body:
deno 1.30.3 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin) (Apple M1)
v8 10.9.194.5
typescript 4.9.4

'connection closed before message completed' when using fetch, occurring sometimes locally (seemingly random), but always occurring on the 4th outbound fetch request in a Google Cloud Run First Generation service (reoccurs on 4th request when calling the endpoint again). Interestingly, in Cloud Run Second Generation the issue does not occur.
* Locally was not using Docker - running Deno directly on host

[GCP article about First Generation vs Second Generation](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/about-execution-environments). Maybe most interestingly, 'The second generation execution environment provides full Linux compatibility rather than system call emulation.'

Will try create a reproduction sometime this week.

author: bershanskiy

title: deno fmt: Markdown lists inside blockquotes handled incorrectly
body:
**Input**

```markdown
> 1. Proident ad ea dolore qui eiusmod culpa culpa ad aliqua tempor. Sint magna elit nulla laborum sunt ex culpa culpa minim. Sit ut in nostrud tempor exercitation ullamco. Laboris id eiusmod excepteur deserunt id ullamco duis ea cillum dolore.
>
>    Sit et aute consequat in duis consequat ipsum est velit occaecat ad officia ex magna. Cupidatat aliquip pariatur anim dolore aliqua Lorem. Sit nisi duis aute deserunt culpa in. Non nostrud ullamco deserunt nostrud aute esse reprehenderit quis aute eiusmod.
```

Semantically, this is:

```yaml
- blockquote
  - list
    - item
      - paragraph
        - text
      - paragraph
        - text
```

**Output**

```markdown
$ deno md --options-prose-wrap never bug.md
> 1. Proident ad ea dolore qui eiusmod culpa culpa ad aliqua tempor. Sint magna elit nulla laborum sunt ex culpa culpa minim. Sit ut in nostrud tempor exercitation ullamco. Laboris id eiusmod excepteur deserunt id ullamco duis ea cillum dolore.
   > Sit et aute consequat in duis consequat ipsum est velit occaecat ad officia ex magna. Cupidatat aliquip pariatur anim dolore aliqua Lorem. Sit nisi duis aute deserunt culpa in. Non nostrud ullamco deserunt nostrud aute esse reprehenderit quis aute eiusmod.
```

Semantically, this is:

```yaml
- blockquote
  - list
    - item
      - paragraph
        - text
        - softbreak
        - text
```

**Problem:**

1. The two input paragraphs are merged into one paragraph.
2. The `>` character is being indented. Semantics are preserved, but:
   1. It is confusing to the reader.
   2. It looks horrible syntactically.

**Expected output:**

The output should be identical to the input. When the list is not contained by a blockquote, the output _is_ identical to the input. I wouldn't expect the blockquote to change this.
author: denizdogan

title: Extra pong sent by Deno's websocket implementation
body:
Tugstenite already sends a pong for a recieved ping. This automatically happens when the socket read is being driven. From https://github.com/snapview/tokio-tungstenite/issues/88
> You need to read from the read-side of the socket so that it receives/handles pings, and on the next write it would then send the corresponding pong.

Furthermore, all the Autobahn tests currently failing show the extra pong.

eg: https://jubilant-eureka-q44yrye.pages.github.io/unknownserver_case_2_6.html (edit: the test result has been updated so the fail is not visible, see below)
```
Case Outcome

Actual events differ from any expected.

Expected:
{'OK': [('pong', '0xfefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefe ...')]}

Observed:
[('pong', '0xfefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefefe ...'), ('pong', u'')]
```

I think the fix is simple: just remove any explict pongs Deno is sending.

title: Error when a dynamic import contains npm specifiers
body:
For some reason I can only reproduce this with github actions.
You can find the repro here: https://github.com/jespertheend/gh-actions-deno-bug-repro/actions/runs/4169393892/jobs/7217303972

I recently moved one of my imports to a dynamic import in order to speed up script execution for cases where the import wasn't needed. But the dynamic import has an npm specifier ([which can be found here](https://github.com/jespertheend/dev/blob/06008344f6f4a5d7ad135bec05cdc2a368bb9bf1/src/plugins/esmify.js#L2)) to `npm:rollup@2.79.1`.
As a result an uncaught `ReferenceError` is thrown at `file:///home/runner/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/rollup/2.79.1/dist/es/shared/rollup.js:1:18` saying `__DENO_NODE_GLOBAL_THIS_1676335827__ is not defined`

I've made the workflow upload an artifact, which you can [download from its summary page](https://github.com/jespertheend/gh-actions-deno-bug-repro/actions/runs/4169393892), but there doesn't seem to be any reference to `__DENO_NODE_GLOBAL_THIS_1676335827__` in `rollup.js:1:18`

A workaround appears to be to import your dynamic import as a static import instead, i.e.
```js
import "https://deno.land/x/dev@v0.2.0/mod.js";
const specifier = "https://deno.land/x/dev@v0.2.0/mod.js";
const mod = await import(specifier);
```
does not trigger the issue. But that kind of defeats the purpose of having a dynamic import in the first place.

author: kt3k

title: Deno.makeTemp* do not appear to be checking for file existence
body:
I've run this on Deno 1.28.2, but it doesn't look like it's a recent regression from a cursory look at`git log` and `git blame`. Simple repro:

```
$ mkdir out
$ cat repro.ts
while (true) { console.log(Deno.makeTempFileSync({dir: "out"})); }
$ deno run --allow-all repro.ts # this will generate ~100k files, beware!
...
out/017f5cd8
out/b9d7898a
out/c2c8ca65
out/ea05084a
out/1b342639
out/671325db
out/9703a9d5
out/28d6be21
error: Uncaught AlreadyExists: File exists (os error 17)
while (true) { console.log(Deno.makeTempFileSync({dir: "out"})); }
                                ^
    at Object.makeTempFileSync (deno:runtime/js/30_fs.js:88:16)
    at file:///Users/cscheid/Desktop/daily-log/2023/02/14/deno-temp-file-stress/main.ts:1:33
$ cd out
$ ls | wc
   77678   77678  699102
```

So, although `make_temp` [has code that attempts to detect AlreadyExists](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blame/main/runtime/ops/fs.rs#L1778-L1782), that code does not appear to be executing.

We have seen this actually happen in the wild at least once. It's not as rare as one would naively expect because of the birthday paradox. We saw it when generating on the order of a few thousand files in the same directory. That was particularly unlucky but the point still stands.

title: [Feature Request] `--quiet` flag for deno bench

author: GJZwiers

title: Dynamic npm module imports should be able to fail individually #17770
body:
This is split away from #17770 

#17770 fixes crash on some module imports. Namely, after #17770 the following lines no longer crash deno:
```js
import("npm:ws:")
import("npm:wss:")
```
However, that PR did not fix module resolution. In particular:

Module resolutions are still bundled and failure of dynamic import will fail resolution of static modules too
```js
import a from "npm:ok-module";
try {
  await import("npm:bad-module");
} catch (e) {
  console.log("This should print");
}
console.log("This should print");
```

Failure of one dynamic import will fail import of other dynamic imports:
```
$ rm -rf ~/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/semver/
$ deno
Deno 1.30.3+0aeb8bc
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
REPL is running with all permissions allowed.
To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.
> const a = import("npm:" + "aefsfddsadv"); const b = await import("npm:" + "semver"); console.log("123")
Uncaught TypeError: Could not resolve 'npm:semver'.
  Caused by:
    could not find npm package directory for 'semver'
    at async <anonymous>:3:11
> const s = await import("npm:semver")
undefined
> s
Module {
  Comparator: [Class: Comparator],
  Range: [Class: Range],
  SEMVER_SPEC_VERSION: "2.0.0",
  SemVer: [Class: SemVer],
...
```

author: bartlomieju

title: The `--inspect-brk` flag of `deno run` is order-dependent, and fails silently with the wrong order
body:
When trying deno, at one point I tried to run the debugger with this command:

```sh
deno run my_script.ts --inspect-brk
```

That command didn't start a debugger session, which I found odd since I had managed to make it work earlier. It turns out that this flag is order-dependent, and must be specified *before* the source file:

```sh
deno run --inspect-brk my_script.ts    # Now it works!
```

I'm not sure if this is a bug or intended behavior. I suspect bug.

If it's intended, some problems:

- The `basics/debugging_your_code` tutorial doesn't mention that the argument is order-dependent.
- With a command like the above, where you end up issuing an "empty" `--inspect-brk`, the runtime could at least issue a warning that the user is likely passing the arguments incorrectly.
author: PoignardAzur

title: [Feature request] Record-replay debugging
body:
One of the biggest blockers I have when coding javascript is the debugging. When something goes wrong, I often have no idea why and I need to open a debugger to inspect values and see where the program deviates from my expectations.

Something that makes this process slow is having to restart the debugger over and over as I zero in on the bug. Often I'll get some information that tells me "Oh, the function I just passed over returned the wrong value", and then I need to start over and choose to go inside the function instead of skipping it. Having a "step one line back" button would be a lifesaver in this case.

**Therefore, it would be amazing if Deno could develop replay debugging similar to rr's.**

I don't really have much more to add: this is probably a very complex feature that would take a lot of man-hours to implement. But once implemented, it would make Deno incredibly more powerful as a tool.
author: PoignardAzur

author: jespertheend

title: allow snapshots to depend on `runtime_main`
body:
The current system only includes the `runtime_main` extension if `snapshot_from_snapshot` is not present as a feature. This creates a nightmare for anyone trying to snapshot the runtime as you have to specifically reference the 99_main.js file in order to achieve many important deno tasks (visible in [the cli's build script](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/a01af067d79e78ea8e2c21cf0ef92d86d425f8eb/cli/build.rs#L361)). My proposed solution to this is making `runtime_main` always dependable in runtime snapshots, and the `runtime` extension just doesn't depend on it if `snapshot_from_snapshot` is present. This would allow further snapshots of the runtime to have an extension that depends on `runtime_main` to make the process easier.

It is important to note that this could only be achieved with the completion and merge of #17795 (thank you @crowlKats).
author: orvitpng

title: `Deno.FsFile#readable` example code is broken
body:
https://deno.land/api@v1.30.3?s=Deno.FsFile#prop_readable:

A ReadableStream instance representing to the byte contents of the file. This makes it easy to interoperate with other web streams based APIs.

const file = await Deno.open("my_file.txt", { read: true });
const decoder = new TextDecoder();
for await (const chunk of file.readable) {
  console.log(decoder.decode(chunk));
}
file.close();
I have this shell script:
cd /tmp
echo -n 'test data' > my_file.txt
deno eval 'const file = await Deno.open("my_file.txt", { read: true });
const decoder = new TextDecoder();
for await (const chunk of file.readable) {
  console.log(decoder.decode(chunk));
}
file.close();'
I run it:
test data
error: Uncaught (in promise) BadResource: Bad resource ID
file.close();
     ^
    at Object.close (internal:core/01_core.js:387:25)
    at FsFile.close (internal:runtime/js/40_files.js:138:12)
    at file:///tmp/$deno$eval.js:6:6
It read data from my_file.txt correctly but errored closing file.
author: xBZZZZ

title: [BUG] lsp dts importmap
body:
### Brief
~~Within importmap context, dts should resolve to dts of target file, instead of dts of source file.~~

Edit: Not sure what happend, but I guess it's dts problem on lsp side.

### Reproduce
import_map.json

```json
{
  "imports": {
    "comment-json": "npm:comment-json@4.2.3",
    "fs/promises": "https://deno.land/std@0.177.0/node/fs/promises.ts",
    "node:fs/promises": "https://deno.land/std@0.177.0/node/fs/promises.ts"
  }
}
```

### Actual
Within vscode, below code type error on importId `comment-json`
```ts
import { parse, stringify } from 'comment-json'
console.log({ parse, stringify })
import { readFile, writeFile } from 'node:fs/promises'
console.log({ readFile, writeFile })
```

```txt
Resolved Dependency

Code: npm​:comment-json​@4.2.3

Uncached or missing npm package: "comment-json@4.2.3".deno(no-cache-npm)
```

`denoland.vscode-deno` deno language server log have many repeated same error, `@types/node` not found
```txt
Could not set npm package requirements.  \
Error getting response at https://registry.npmjs.org/@types/node: \
An npm specifier not found in cache: "@types/node", --cached-only is specified.
```

### Expected
Within vscode, below code types are OK
```ts
import { parse, stringify } from 'comment-json'
console.log({ parse, stringify })
import { readFile, writeFile } from 'node:fs/promises'
console.log({ readFile, writeFile })
```

### Workaround

Within vscode, below code types are OK
```ts
import { parse, stringify } from 'comment-json'
console.log({ parse, stringify })
import { readFile, writeFile } from 'fs/promises'
console.log({ readFile, writeFile })
```

### Edit
Above workaround, offen works, sometimes still got type error on `comment-json`
author: loynoir

title: Pressing CTRL+C once in Deno REPL must interrupt the running code, not exit the REPL
body:
It would be a nice feature if pressing CTRL+C once in Deno REPL would interrupt the running code, like in node.js and return to the REPL prompt.

For example,

```
> let a = []
undefined
> a[1<<30] = 100
100
> for (let i in a) console.log(i)
1073741824
undefined
> for (let i of a) console.log(i)
undefined
undefined
undefined
undefined
<...>
^C
```

Currently, pressing CTRL+C terminates the whole process.

If implemented, this behaviour should only affect REPL. Pressing CTRL+C while running code from the source file must be handled by the signal traps installed in the code itself, in the way its author intended, or if no traps has been installed, terminate the process.
author: milaz

title: REPL tab completion is enumerating all indices of an array
body:
```
> let a = new Array(1024).fill(1)
undefined
> a.<Tab>
Display all 1072 possibilities? (y or n)
0                     358                   716
1                     359                   717
<...>
--More--
```

I believe, there is no point in tab-completing any of the numerical indices in arrays.
Should they be excluded from the completion suggestions?
author: milaz

title: [Feature Request] Add support for Origin Private File System (OPFS)
body:
[OPFS](https://webkit.org/blog/12257/the-file-system-access-api-with-origin-private-file-system/) seems to be the standard way for Web Apps to write to temp storage, files and folders they'd need to work with. It would be cool and convinient if Deno supported OPFS out of the box like it currently does for the Cache API. IMO, that would allow web apps to have ultimate compat with Deno.

> It would also be pretty cool if you also supported the [File System Access API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/File_System_Access_API), to keep web compat.
author: okikio

title: importmap ignored by `deno install`
body:
When I use importMap, my code does not work as executable (`deno install`).

**Tested with Deno v1.30.3**.

## Minimal reproducible example:

**deno.json:**

```JSON
{
  ...
  "imports": {
    "std/": "https://deno.land/std@0.177.0/"
  },
  ...
}
```

**my-cli.ts**

```TypeScript
import * as log from 'std/log/mod.ts';

log.info('Hello world!')
```

**Install & run:**

```bash
deno install -A ./my-cli.ts

my-cli <--- This throws the error
```

**Error output:**
```bash
error: Relative import path "std/log/mod.ts" not prefixed with / or ./ or ../
    at file:///C:/temp/app.ts:1:22
```

The same problem when I use an import map file (import_map.json). Only if I use absolute paths, it also works with `deno install`.

title: `URLPattern` pattern matching regression
body:
Partial pathname pattern match does not seem to work as expected.

**Deno version**: 1.30.3

**Doc Reference**: https://deno.land/api@v1.30.3?s=URLPattern
**From the doc (expected behavior):**
```
// Specify the pattern as structured data.
const pattern = new URLPattern({ pathname: "/users/:user" });
const match = pattern.exec("/users/joe");
console.log(match.pathname.groups.user); // joe
```

**Actual behavior**:

Returns `null` on `pattern.exec("/users/joe");` call.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2852392/220326476-294f2119-7f7d-44ca-afc1-280ca022259a.png)
author: gothammm

title: `deno install` should warn when not ignoring auto-discovered configuration file
body:
`deno install` is a global action, so it ignores the cwd's auto-discovered config file. It seems people are often confused by this, so we should update `deno install` to warn that the configuration file is ignored and needs to be explicitly.

For local files, we might want to consider auto-discovering based on the specified path.
