author: dsherret

title: More descriptive error for "the trait `Deserialize<'_>` is not implemented for `&OpState`"
body:
Consider the following example that's mistakenly using `&OpState` instead of `&mut OpState`:

```rs
use deno_core::{anyhow::Result, op, OpState};

#[op]
pub fn op_hello(state: &OpState) -> Result<()> {
  Ok(())
}

```

The compiler prints an error that's not very helpful:

```
error[E0277]: the trait bound `&OpState: Deserialize<'_>` is not satisfied
  --> src/lib.rs:3:1
   |
3  | #[op]
   | ^^^^^ the trait `Deserialize<'_>` is not implemented for `&OpState`
   |
   = help: the following other types implement trait `Deserialize<'de>`:
             &'a Path
             &'a [u8]
             &'a str
             ()
             (T0, T1)
             (T0, T1, T2)
             (T0, T1, T2, T3)
             (T0, T1, T2, T3, T4)
           and 193 others
note: required by a bound in `from_v8`
  --> /Users/bajtos/.cargo/registry/src/github.com-1ecc6299db9ec823/serde_v8-0.82.0/de.rs:48:6
   |
48 |   T: Deserialize<'de>,
   |      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ required by this bound in `from_v8`
   = note: this error originates in the attribute macro `op` (in Nightly builds, run with -Z macro-backtrace for more info)
```

Can we improve the `op` macro to print a more helpful error message telling the developer to use a mutable reference?

I am guessing the problem is in the code parsing op fn arguments, where `&OptState` is not recognised as a special argument - it's treated as any other argument coming from JS and passed to `serde_v8`, which does not know what to do with it.

author: brianleroux

title: Deno 1.31.0 downloads Node modules without being asked to
body:
Consider this situation:

```shell
$ ls
node_modules
package-lock.json
package.json
update-list.ts
...

$ cat package.json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "typescript": "^4.9.5"
  }
}

$ cat update-list.ts
console.log("test");

$ deno run update-list.ts
# downloads typescript from package.json dependencies
```

Deno has no need to look into Node modules here, since `update-list.ts` is its own standalone Deno script. However in this case, it still installs `typescript` first. This is unnecessary. Is this intentional? Is there going to be no way to have Deno scripts in an otherwise Node project?

```shell
$ deno
Could not set npm package requirements. Error getting response at https://registry.npmjs.org/typescript for package "typescript": An npm specifier not found in cache: "typescript", --cached-only is specified.

exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()

To specify permissions, run `deno repl` with allow flags.

$ deno repl

Deno 1.31.0
exit using ctrl+d, ctrl+c, or close()
```

This doesn't happen if the `package.json` has no dependencies.

Related: #17928 

After running any script like mentioned in #17928, Deno downloads typescript, and then REPL no longer errors.

title: Lazily surface package.json dependency entry errors
body: Right now if we error parsing the package.json’s dependency entries then we surface that error eagerly. We should do it lazily on first package.json dependency bare specifier match in the resolver. This will be less annoying because it won’t get in the way if the user isn’t using the package.json with Deno.

author: comunidadio

title: tokio-util version-lock preventing integration along-side updated crates
body:
Deno 1.31.1 requires exactly tokio-util version "=0.7.4", but that crate is over 5 months old and several newer versions have been released.  Integrating the deno runtime along-side newer crates that use the latest tokio-util is not possible.

I believe a semver minimum bound would be adequate in this case as the tokio team is unlikely to break compatibility in a patch release.

I have submitted this PR, awaiting approval:  https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17863

Thank you.

title: Error loading package.json: Not a directory
body:
```ts
import "npm:@sdkgen/node-runtime";
```

This fails on Deno 1.31.1 with:

```
error: Uncaught Error: Error loading package.json at /mnt/node_modules/.deno/@sdkgen+node-runtime@2.2.0/node_modules/decimal.js/decimal.js/package.json. Not a directory (os error 20)
    at resolveExports (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:318:14)
    at Function.Module._findPath (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:357:31)
    at Function.Module._resolveFilename (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:618:27)
    at Function.Module._load (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:467:27)
    at Module.require (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:678:19)
    at require (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:818:16)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///mnt/node_modules/.deno/@sdkgen+node-runtime@2.2.0/node_modules/@sdkgen/node-runtime/dist/src/encode-decode.js:40:22)
    at Object.<anonymous> (file:///mnt/node_modules/.deno/@sdkgen+node-runtime@2.2.0/node_modules/@sdkgen/node-runtime/dist/src/encode-decode.js:374:4)
    at Module._compile (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:745:34)
    at Object.Module._extensions..js (internal:deno_node_loading/02_require.js:778:10)
```

The same import works on Deno 1.30.3, so this is a regression.

Importing `decimal.js` directly works, but not indirectly. Both modules are commonjs.

title: Deno touches deno.lock everytime script runs if using npm specifiers
body:
My application has a file watcher that watches for any changes in the application directory and if there are, it re-runs the build script and restarts the server. I have been using esm.sh for this. When it runs, usually it wouldn't detect changes in the deno.lock file unless there are changes made to the import_map.json.

You can see that it doesn't restart the server until I save a change to the index.tsx file.

```
kyle@kyle-XPS-15-9560:~/Projects/deno/react_app_example$ deno task dev
Task dev deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/dev.ts
Watching /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example
Live reload listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9002
Task build deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/build.ts
Building app
Build completed in 783 ms
Starting app
Listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9000
Server started
Detected change: /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example/routes/index.tsx
Task build deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/build.ts
Building app
Build completed in 1226 ms
Restarting app
Listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9000
Server restarted
```
If I change any of the imports from using esm.sh to using an npm specifier in the import map, it will get stuck in a loop where everytime the server is restarted, the deno.lock file ends up getting updated. I tried copying the file and re-running to see what the difference was, but there were no changes. So it looks like it's just touching the file on every run instead of only modifying the file if there is a change to to deno.lock file.

```
"npm/serialize-javascript": "https://esm.sh/serialize-javascript@6.0.0?target=deno&pin=v108"
```

```
"npm/serialize-javascript": "npm:serialize-javascript@6.0.0"
```

You can see below that the server keeps restarting because of changes detected to the deno.lock file. If I don't exit the program, it will repeatedly restart because of detected changes to the deno.lock file.

```
kyle@kyle-XPS-15-9560:~/Projects/deno/react_app_example$ deno task dev
Task dev deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/dev.ts
Watching /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example
Live reload listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9002
Task build deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/build.ts
Building app
Build completed in 879 ms
Starting app
Detected change: /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example/deno.lock
Task build deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/build.ts
Building app
Listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9000es/react@^18/jsx-dev-runtime~.d.ts
Server started
Build completed in 1509 ms
Restarting app
Detected change: /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example/deno.lock
Task build deno run -A --import-map=./import_map.json https://deno.land/x/udibo_react_app@0.5.1/build.ts
Listening on: http://0.0.0.0:9000
Server started
Building app
Build completed in 1307 ms
Restarting app
Detected change: /home/kyle/Projects/deno/react_app_example/deno.lock
```


Here is the repository used in the examples provided above. You can reproduce the issue by editing the import map to use npm specifiers for any of the npm dependencies or by just changing the same one I did in the example shown above.

https://github.com/udibo/react_app_example

Here is a link to the file watcher used. It's just using Deno.watchFs, watching for modify events to trigger generating new builds and restarting the server.

https://github.com/udibo/react_app/blob/0.5.1/dev.ts#L226

title: Implement node:path in Rust
body:
Currently the node:path module is implemented in JavaScript: https://github.com/denoland/deno/tree/main/ext/node/polyfills/path

We would like to implement this in Rust and reduce the amount of JavaScript. This should be done incrementally as much as possible, with benchmarks showing that the new Rust implementation out performs the previous JavaScript one. 

- [ ] join
- [ ] basename
- [ ] dirname
- [ ] extname
- [ ] delimiter
- [ ] format
- [ ] fromFileUrl
- [ ] isAbsolute
- [ ] normalize
- [ ] parse
- [ ] relative
- [ ] resolve
- [ ] sep
- [ ] toFileUrl
- [ ] toNamespacedPath
author: ry

title: `npm:wrangler` throws `Found argument '--no-warnings' which wasn't expected`
body:
The following error is thrown when running `deno run -A npm:wrangler publish worker.js`

```bash
error: Found argument '--no-warnings' which wasn't expected, or isn't valid in this context

        Did you mean '--no-config'?

        If you tried to supply `--no-warnings` as a value rather than a flag, use `-- --no-warnings`

USAGE:
    deno run --allow-all --unstable --no-config <SCRIPT_ARG>...

For more information try --help
```

Where `worker.js` can be just an empty file. 

`--no-warnings` is [a CLI flag for Node](https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#--no-warnings), but this error can be reproduced in Deno with:

```ts
import { spawn } from "node:child_process";
import process from "node:process";

spawn(
  process.execPath,
  [
    "--no-warnings",
  ],
  {
    stdio: ["inherit", "inherit", "inherit", "ipc"],
  },
);

```
It is a simplification of [the code used in the package](https://github.com/cloudflare/workers-sdk/blob/0ed6712c7a1b8f2017d1574594405c3c7cbda2da/packages/wrangler/bin/wrangler.js#L58). I am not sure how `process.execPath` is implemented for Deno, but the [Node docs](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#processexecpath) say: `The process.execPath property returns the absolute pathname of the executable that started the Node.js process.` Only in this case that would be the `deno` executable, which does not know a `--no-warnings` flag.

Source (Discord): https://discord.com/channels/684898665143206084/684898665151594506/1080156118208233522

```bash
deno --version

deno 1.31.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 11.0.226.13
typescript 4.9.4
```

title: ext/node: socket timeout cannot be cleared
body:
Copying this from https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/issues/3222 since node polyfills were moved to core recently.

**Describe the bug**

Calling `socket.setTimeout(0)` after previously calling `socket.setTimeout(anyPositiveInteger)` still times out after `anyPositiveInteger` miliseconds.

**Steps to Reproduce**

1. Run the following command to start a basic websocket server on port 8080:
```bash
> deno run --allow-net https://deno.land/x/websocket@v0.1.4/example/server.ts
```
2. Create a `socket.ts` file with the following code:
```typescript
import { connect } from 'node:net';

const x = connect(8080, '127.0.0.1');

x.setTimeout(1000);
x.setTimeout(0);

x.on('timeout', () => console.log('TIMEOUT'));
```
3. On another shell, execute the code with the following command:
```bash
> deno run --allow-net socket.ts
```
4. Wait a second (1000 ms) and verify that a `TIMEOUT` message is printed, indicating that the socket timed out even after we cleared it.

**Expected behavior**

Nothing. Given I cleared the socket's timeout, I would expect it to never timeout. Thats what happens when I run it on Node v19.7.0.

**Environment**

- OS: Windows 11 22H2 build 22621.1105
- deno version: 1.31.1
author: rmobis

title: 🐛 `Deno.consoleSize()` stabilization lost the ability to find console size when STDOUT/STDERR are redirected
body:
The stabilization of `Deno.consoleSize()` breaks the ability to determine the console size when STDOUT/STDERR are both redirected.

Minimal example (note: `find /v ""` == `cat` for WinOS)...

```shell
C> deno --version
deno 1.26.2 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 10.7.193.16
typescript 4.8.3

C> :: STDERR/STDOUT redirected
C> deno --unstable eval "const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(f.rid); console.log({size});" 2>&1 | find /v ""
{ size: { columns: 132, rows: 45 } }
```

This is no longer possible using Deno functions after the stabilization in v1.27.0+ as the rid is now ignored.

```shell
C> deno --version
deno 1.31.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 11.0.226.13
typescript 4.9.4

C> deno --unstable eval "const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(f.rid); console.log({size});"
{ size: { columns: 132, rows: 45 } }

C> :: STDERR/STDOUT redirected
C> deno --unstable eval "const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(f.rid); console.log({size});" 2>NUL | find /v ""

C> :: STDERR/STDOUT redirected (differently ~ another, different bug?!?)
C> deno --unstable eval "const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(f.rid); console.log({size});" 2>&1 | find /v ""
error: Uncaught Error: The handle is invalid. (os error 6)
const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(); console.log({size});
                                                      ^
    at Object.consoleSize (internal:runtime/js/40_tty.js:13:7)
    at file:///C:/Users/Roy/OneDrive/Projects/deno/dxx/repo.GH/$deno$eval.js:1:55
C> deno --unstable eval "const size = Deno.consoleSize(); console.log({size});" 2>&1 | find /v ""
error: Uncaught Error: The handle is invalid. (os error 6)
const f = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$'); const size = Deno.consoleSize(); console.log({size});
                                                      ^
    at Object.consoleSize (internal:runtime/js/40_tty.js:13:7)
    at file:///C:/Users/Roy/OneDrive/Projects/deno/dxx/repo.GH/$deno$eval.js:1:55
```

Can this be changed so that if an optional `rid` is supplied, it is used to determine the console size? Maybe in addition to the three rids now used?

Alternatively, the additional check could be added internally, opening a handle to 'CONOUT$' and testing that for size if the standard handles fail. Some initial testing with another implementation that I've created shows about a 20% increase in total time from 0.5ms to 0.6ms when adding the file open (sync) and testing the resultant ID. If it was tested only if the others failed, it wouldn't add any more time in the usual case but add robustness when needed.

> The equivalent Linux code uses '/dev/tty' in place of 'CONOUT$'.

I could offer a PR if desired.

author: snobu

title: Feature request: Option to prevent terminal from opening after compile (on windows)
body:
Windows applications differ from Linux and MacOS in that they can either be a console or GUI application.
Currently Deno is a console application by default, which carries over when using `deno compile`

However there are use cases for which you would not want a terminal window to open, see [this discussion](https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/11638). In the comments a user provides a script to change the relevant entry in the PE header to make any application a GUI application.

Another use case being possible webview support, for which it would not be a good user experience to see a terminal pop up.
[See latest comments in this discussion](https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/3234?sort=new#discussioncomment-5149598)

So I propose to add a flag, like `--no-terminal`, to `deno compile` which changes the header so it won't open the terminal.

title: docker arm64 builds
body:
Currently, official deno docker images at https://hub.docker.com/r/denoland/deno only provide amd64 builds.

It would be great to have official arm64 builds available as the architecture is getting more popular, both for development (recent Macbooks) and for servers (graviton etc).
author: comunidadio

title: Feature request: Add `--asset` flag to compile
body:
Currently only TS/JS files get added to the binary on `deno compile`, while other files might be needed as well.
This is related too #9574, but instead of having to manually change the binary I propose to add a flag, like `--asset <dir|file>`.
With the flag said directory or file gets included into the binary and is accessible in the runtime.

Making apps portable will be the main use case.
Even small `css` or `html` files now force you to either wrap it into a string export from a  JS/TS file or use the binary hack proposed in #9574.

This would also be useful for a possible webview API.
[See latest comments in this discussion](https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/3234?sort=new#discussioncomment-5149598)

As for the final API, I could see something like this as an option:
# Proposal
Given `deno compile --asset ./assets ./example.ts`
With `example.ts`:
```ts
// Returns path to a temp dir or temp file with the resources
// Or undefined if `--asset` was not used
const path = await Deno.asset()
```

So on `deno run` it needs to copy the contents of `./assets` to a temp dir.
And on running an executable from `deno compile` it needs to extract the resources into a temp dir.

One thing that I am not sure of with this approach is what happens when you write to the temp dir/file in `example.ts`.
I think there are two options to handle this:
- Somehow make the temp dir readonly (not sure how)
- Just allow it but overwrite all changes when rerunning

Option 1 is more restrictive but clearer
Option 2 might be confusing but simple to implement

title: Add support for Junctions
body:
Recently, I was working on a project that would have to run on windows, and create a link.
Unfortunately, on windows, symlinks require administrator privleges, so if you want to get around this, you need to use junctions.
Please add some sort of junction support to Deno, perhaps add a ```useJunctions``` flag to [SymlinkOptions](https://deno.land/api@v1.31.1?s=Deno.SymlinkOptions), that would create a junction instead of a link, if you are running on windows.
author: jessiejs

title: Add environment variable to change REPL history file path
body: A fairly common practice with REPL interfaces is to allow the location of the history file to be changed with an environment variable. This allows users to put their history in tmpfs to be cleared on shutdown or disable saving between sessions entirely by pointing towards `/dev/null` or an empty path. This kind of configuration is particularly useful when experimenting with API tokens or other secrets interactively, where ideally they should never touch the disk. I suggest adding an environment variable named `DENO_REPL_HISTORY` (based on Node's own feature) or similar to specify a custom history file path.

title: Third party `peerDependencies` aren't resolved to first party `dependencies`
body:
I want to use React libraries (e.g. MUI) in Preact but Deno doesn't seem to recognize aliased package.

```jsonc
// package.json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "preact": "10.13.0",
    "preact-render-to-string": "5.2.6",
    // alias React to Preact
    "react": "npm:@preact/compat@17.1.2",
    // Recoil has React in peerDependencies
    // https://github.com/facebookexperimental/Recoil/blob/0.7.6/packages/recoil/package-for-release.json#L17
    "recoil": "0.7.7"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/react": "npm:@preact/compat@17.1.2"
  }
}

```

```jsonc
// deno.json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "jsx": "react-jsx",
    "jsxImportSource": "preact",
    "lib": ["DOM", "ESNext"]
  }
}
```

```tsx
// main.tsx
import { RecoilRoot } from "recoil";
import { render } from "preact-render-to-string";

render(<RecoilRoot />);
```

`deno run main.tsx` shows warning messages by React.

```console
$ deno run --check main.tsx
Warning: Invalid hook call. Hooks can only be called inside of the body of a function component.
```

`deno info main.tsx` shows that `recoil` depends on `react@18.2.0`.

```console
$ deno info main.tsx
local: /home/user/path/to/main.tsx
emit: /home/user/.cache/deno/gen/file/home/user/path/to/main.tsx.js
type: TSX
dependencies: 8 unique
size: 4.04MB

file:///home/user/path/to/main.tsx (123B)
├── npm:preact@10.13.0/jsx-runtime (1.14MB)
├─┬ npm:recoil@0.7.7 (2.11MB)
│ ├── npm:hamt_plus@1.0.2 (94.32KB)
│ └─┬ npm:react@18.2.0 (308.7KB)
│   └─┬ npm:loose-envify@1.4.0 (5.68KB)
│     └── npm:js-tokens@4.0.0 (14.72KB)
└─┬ npm:preact-render-to-string@5.2.6 (371.39KB)
  ├── npm:preact@10.13.0 (1.14MB)
  └── npm:pretty-format@3.8.0 (17.72KB)
```

`npm install` creates `node_modules/react` directory and its content is `@preact/compat`.

Otherwise, `deno run main.ts` creates `node_modules/react` directory and its content is `react`.

```console
$ deno --version
deno 1.31.1 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 11.0.226.13
typescript 4.9.4
```
author: reosablo

title: npm start compatibility
body:
(Loving Deno so far.) I noticed a minor thing in the new `package.json` support (very cool!) I thought I should flag up if it's not already on the list: `deno task start` doesn't implement `npm start`'s / `npm run start`'s fallbacks. Currently it looks like it purely looks for a `start` task in `scripts` and says there's no task if it doesn't find one. In contrast, `npm start` has a [two-level fallback](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v6/commands/npm-start):

* If there's no `start` task in `scripts`, it looks for a `start` package property.
* If there's no `start` package property, it runs `node server.js`.

I suspect some number of folks don't even realize they're relying on those fallbacks (I didn't). It may be worth either:

1. Implementing the same fallbacks,
    or
3. Adding a specific message if the task requested is `start`, something like:
    > `deno task start` requires a `start` entry in `scripts`, it doesn't support the fallbacks `npm start` uses (https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v6/commands/npm-start). To use `deno task start`, add an explicit `start` task to `scripts`.

Again, great stuff on Deno and related, thanks.

title: Improve synchronization of multiple deno processes caching node_modules folder
body:
We should add some basic synchronization around multiple processes creating the node_modules folder at the same time. Basically do whatever `cargo build` does and have each process wait on the other.

This is necessary because it seems like multiple processes creating junctions at the same time causes some weird issues with the junctions becoming invalid. Also, it's duplicate work for the two processes to do this.

Edit: I have the code done for this... just need to write a test.
author: dsherret

title: Improved `deno info --json` output for `npm:` and `node:` modules
body:
Right now the representation of `npm:` specifiers in `deno info --json` are inconsistent. For example, `npm:` specifiers are currently added as an entry to the `"modules"` array if they are a root module, but are not added if they are a dependency of a "standard" (`https:` or `file:` module). There are entries in the `redirects` for NPM modules, but they point to specifiers that are only visible through the `deps` array on modules. Long story short, dealing with NPM specifiers in `deno info --json` is a royal PITA right now and requires many special cases. It also breaks backwards compat with the expectation that all RHS of the `redirects` map have entries in the `modules` array.

I propose we fix this as follows:
- each final resolution of a given NPM module + subpath is added as an entry in the `modules` array
- the pointer for npm: `module` -> package info in `npmPackages` is per module in the modules array, not per import in the `deps` array
- `deps` array is identical regardless of what kind of module the import points to
- each module can be either of kind `esm`, `npm` or `node`
   - all three kinds have the `specifier` field
   - all three kinds can optionally have the `error` field
   - npm packages have the `npmPackage` field, and additionally a _seperate_ field for subpath
   - esm kind stays as is
   - node kind has a field that stores the encoded path (eg `node:fs/promises` would have `fs/promises` in this field)
- redirects are used to map `npm:preact` to `npm:preact@10.13.0`
author: lucacasonato

title: Allow Comments in package.json
body: It's always struck me as a major flaw that you can't document your `package.json` file, but there's no technical reason it needs to be that way.  Deno should correct Node's mistake, and allow comments in that file.
author: machineghost

title: Content-Length header duplicated on Flash
body:
When sending a `Response` with the Flash server, and the user provides a `Content-Length` header, the Flash server will add an additional `Content-Length` header with the content length as it calculated it, where as if the `Content-Length` is provided to the "native" stable server, it does not duplicate the header (I am not sure if it is overriding it or if it is just letting it pass through).

It feels like the Flash server should follow the behaviour of the native stable server.

Ref: oakserver/oak#587 (I will fix it in oak by simply omitting the `content-length` in all cases as the servers are actually better at handling this header now)
author: kitsonk

title: Reevaluate compression algorithm for the runtime snapshot
body:
We are currently using `lzzzz::l4_hc` for compressing runtime and CLI snapshots. While the compression provides nice ~4:1 ratio (resulting in ~1.5Mb compressed snapshot) it is also quite expensive on startup to decompress the snapshot - it takes roughly 13% of the startup time which equates to something around 2.5ms. 

We should reevaluate the compression algorithm we use and maybe trade some of the snapshot size for faster decompression.

Ref https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/15945

title: Implement `mode` field of `Deno.FileInfo` on Windows
body:
Right now the `mode` field is completely un-populated on Windows.

On Node, it is always `0` ([libuv todo](https://github.com/libuv/libuv/blob/460accf9be077f376baebd93a39c214644442674/src/win/fs.c#L1769-L1788), hey @piscisaureus!)

On Go, it is reasonably populated. The mode very realistically emulates Unix. [Code here](https://cs.opensource.google/go/go/+/master:src/os/types_windows.go;l=144-166;drc=1951857ec07c1d491e1836770a647d3902934a67)

I think we should populate them like Go does.
author: lucacasonato

title: Double Pointer?
body:
I'm just wondering if there is a way to copy a TypeScript buffer to a C `char**`?
[Here](https://github.com/AlbertShown/webui/blob/a446188f17205c7cdca5e4b2edecaaa6b5a48615/examples/TypeScript/Deno/dynamic/webui.ts#L175), I tried to do it, but, I'm sure I missed something.

1. C call Deno callback with `char**` as parameter
2. Deno creates a buffer and updates it with some string
3. Deno set the buffer address to the `char**`
4. C reads back the Deno string

```
typescript 4.9.4
```

title: Promise from Deno.read() does not get resolved when reading from a COM port
body:
If you try to open a COM port under **Windows** like a file with the following code example and then try to read with Deno from this COM port with `Deno.read()` then the promise will never get resolved. I don't know if the problem also exists under **Linux**.
COM ports are (in my experience) treated like some kind of a "stream" and therefore there is no `EOF`.

```typescript
const buffer = new Uint8Array(1) // One character

const port = await Deno.open( '\\\.\\COM1', {read: true, write: true} );

while (1) {
    await port.read(buffer); // <- This point never gets resolved if there is nothing to read
    console.log(buffer); // <- And so this point never gets reached
}
```

I think the problem is related to the fact that `Deno.read()` never reaches `EOF` in this example and thus the promise never gets resolved.

> Resolves to either the number of bytes read during the operation or EOF (`null`) if there was nothing more to read.

There are a few workarounds like using a `setTimeout()` and `Promise.race()`, but this is not really a solution to the problem.

[Here is the article from Stackoverflow where I addressed the problem.](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/75400521/deno-read-abort-if-com-port-has-no-data)

I hope that the whole thing will be fixed somehow. Either that there is some kind of a timeout or that Deno recognizes when nothing more can be read. 👍

title: Snapshotted modules from Extensions should require one-byte names and specifiers
body: Currently we're spending around 1.5% of startup time on deserializing snapshotted modules. Most of that time is spent v8::String and Rust's string deserialization. We should enforce using one-byte strings for Extension's name and specifier to reduce this cost.
author: bartlomieju

title: deno check: Add a option to exclude specific files from type checking
body:
Now `deno check` command provides two options in terms of how many modules it's going to typecheck.

- no option: only local modules are typechecked
- `--all`: all modules including remote are typechecked

This suffices in almost all use cases, but it's going to be complicated if we want to perform typecheck within the project that vendors external dependencies using `deno vendor`. Vendored dependencies are considered "local" so `deno check` will check for vendored ones as well. This is sometimes troublesome if vendored dependencies have type errors that are known and acceptable.

So it would be great if we could have a new option, let's say `--ignore=<ignore>`, that make the type checker ignore type errors happening in the specified files. This way, specifying `--ignore=vendor/` will allow us to ignore vendored dependencies.

title: `deno vendor` panics when remote module contains import from `node:`
body:
When the target file of `deno vendor` contains an import of a remote file containing `node:` import, `deno vendor` panics.

### Reproduction

**main.js**
```js
import * as path from "https://gist.githubusercontent.com/magurotuna/3496e86270b410b89a09e8fc339c7f22/raw/4a5baf7b352f7d55326e612040614d4a9b13cda0/node_specifier_sync.js";

console.log(path.sep);
```

**node_specifier_sync.js** (note that this is remote)
```js
export * from "node:path";
```

`deno run` succeeds:

```sh
$ deno run main.js
/
```

However `deno vendor` panics:

```sh
$ RUST_BACKTRACE=1 deno vendor main.js

============================================================
Deno has panicked. This is a bug in Deno. Please report this
at https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/new.
If you can reliably reproduce this panic, include the
reproduction steps and re-run with the RUST_BACKTRACE=1 env
var set and include the backtrace in your report.

Platform: macos aarch64
Version: 1.31.1
Args: ["deno", "vendor", "main.js"]

thread 'main' panicked at 'Could not find local path for node:path', cli/tools/vendor/mappings.rs:142:28
stack backtrace:
   0: _rust_begin_unwind
   1: core::panicking::panic_fmt
   2: deno::tools::vendor::mappings::Mappings::local_path
   3: deno::tools::vendor::mappings::Mappings::local_uri
   4: deno::tools::vendor::mappings::Mappings::relative_specifier_text
   5: deno::tools::vendor::import_map::ImportsBuilder::add
   6: deno::tools::vendor::import_map::visit_resolution
   7: deno::tools::vendor::build::build
   8: deno::run_subcommand::{{closure}}
   9: deno::main::{{closure}}
  10: tokio::task::local::LocalSet::run_until::{{closure}}
  11: deno_runtime::tokio_util::run_local
  12: deno::main
note: Some details are omitted, run with `RUST_BACKTRACE=full` for a verbose backtrace.
```

---

If `main.js` imports _locally_, vendor succeeds:
**main.js** (local import version)
```js
import * as path from "./node_specifier_sync.js";

console.log(path.sep);
```

```sh
$ deno vendor main.js
Vendored 0 modules into vendor/ directory.
```
author: magurotuna

title: `package.json` support breaks on `git+<url>` in package.json in working tree
body:
Deno v1.31+ breaks our project's CI because a `package.json` file is present on the filesystem that includes a `git+<url>` dependency. That `package.json` file has nothing to do with the context in which Deno is being executed, and we can't currently work around this (other than to prevent v1.31 from being used in this project).

There are two separate things going on here:

### Blowing up on `git+<url>`

Deno currently blows up on a totally legal `package.json` file that contains a [`git+<url>` dependency](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v9/configuring-npm/package-json#git-urls-as-dependencies). This appears to be known behavior, but is not ideal, and doesn't appear to be able to be worked around. The error:

```
    Invalid npm specifier version requirement. Unexpected character.
      git+https://github.com/...
```

Ideally (to me) this would be a (suppressible) warning, not an error that exits the process.


### Unnecessarily(?) exploring the filesystem for `package.json`

The other issue here we encountered was that Deno was exploring the filesystem for a `package.json` file when that really wasn't necessary (from our side). Perhaps that is necessary for Deno, but we don't want or need that, and it cannot be worked around.

Project layout in question:
```
src/lambda/index.ts   # folder to run Deno within
package.json          # contains the offending git+<url> dep   
```

In the project above, we run Deno within `./src/lambda/`; everything related to Deno's execution context is contained within `./src/lambda/`, and Deno does not need to know anything about `./src/`, or `./`.


### Repro steps

Create a project like the above where the `package.json` contains a git url dep, run `deno eval "console.log('hi')"` from within `src/lambda/`, see error. `--node-modules-dir=false` has no impact.

Add an empty `package.json` to `src/lambda/`, and it will no longer fail.

title: Type checking fails because of .d.ts being included twice
body:
Since `1.31.2` it seems like files in `types/` are included twice when including using `compilerOptions.types` in `deno.json`. First they're checked with `.d.ts` ending, then with `.d.ts.d.ts` ending (which wierdly enough works, even though the files does not exist). Then type checker complains that everything in the files are already declared.

### Minimal steps to reproduce

Content of **deno.json**
```json
{
    "compilerOptions": {
        "types": [
            "./types/wat.d.ts"
        ]
    }
}
```

Content of **types/wat.d.ts**
```
declare class wat {
    static wat(): void;
}
```

Content of **main.ts**
```
console.log("Hello world!");
```

**Outcome**

Running `deno check main.ts`

```
TS2300 [ERROR]: Duplicate identifier 'wat'.
declare class wat {
              ~~~
    at /typestest/types/wat.d.ts.d.ts:1:15    

    'wat' was also declared here.
    declare class wat {
                  ~~~
        at /typestest/types/wat.d.ts:1:15
```

### Findings

Including types using the pattern `// @deno-types="../types/wat.d.ts"` instead of `deno.json: compilerOptions.types.wat` makes the problem go away.

Possibly related to PR #18026 

Everything is ok in previous version `1.31.1`

### Additional info

**Platform:** Windows 10 / Powershell / VS Code / Deno 1.31.2
author: Hexagon

title: fetch typings: support global agumentation
body:
Some types are currently defined using `class` but, from what I can tell, TypeScript classes cannot be globally augmented (see [How can I augment a globally declared class?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/75675508/how-can-i-augment-a-globally-declared-class)).

e.g.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/215528f4999ecfe931aa00b687095111cb0603d6/ext/fetch/lib.deno_fetch.d.ts#L413

I'd like to see `Headers`, `Request`, `Response` and any other applicable types follow the same pattern as many built-in type definitions:

- `interface {{Type}} { … }`
- `interface {{Type}}Constructor { … }`
- `var {{Type}}: {{Type}}Constructor;`

e.g.

- `interface Map { … }`
- `interface MapConstructor { … }`
- `var Map: MapConstructor;`

This will allow declaration merging for adding missing types or even defining custom ones.

author: dsherret

title: Get rid of the sysroot, but still support older glibc
body:
Deno links to glibc on linux. Deno wants to work on older versions of Linux. In order to produce backwards compatible binaries, we use [a sysroot hack](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/215528f4999ecfe931aa00b687095111cb0603d6/.github/workflows/ci.yml#L174-L234) to build against older an older glibc version.

glibc does version symbols - ideally we can just tell the rust build to use older symbols. in C there is an escape hatch through ASM .symver which people are using together with DBs like this https://github.com/wheybags/glibc_version_header.

There are not many call sites.... https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/13832

title: [bug] Executing `deno run` creates a node_modules directory when package.json is present
body:
When a package.json is present, even if it has no dependencies, a `node_modules/.deno/.deno.lock` file is created whenever executing `deno run` in that directory. Eg:

```sh
$ ls

$ echo '{"lock": false}' > deno.json # lock isn't super relevant here but I'm trying to emphasize how little I want to opt-in to NPM dependencies.

$ echo '{}' > package.json # Totally empty.

$ touch foo.ts # Empty script.

$ deno run foo.ts # Run the empty script.

$ ls # Unwanted node_modules/.deno/.deno.lock was created.
Permissions Size User    Group   Date Modified Date Changed Date Accessed Name
drwxr-xr-x     - stephen stephen 03-11 13:15   03-11 13:15  03-11 13:15   node_modules/ <--------- unwanted
.rw-r--r--    16 stephen stephen 03-11 13:15   03-11 13:15  03-11 13:15   deno.json
.rw-r--r--     0 stephen stephen 03-11 13:15   03-11 13:15  03-11 13:15   foo.ts
.rw-r--r--     3 stephen stephen 03-11 13:15   03-11 13:15  03-11 13:15   package.json

$ bat node_modules/.deno/.deno.lock # The lockfile is empty.
───────┬────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
       │ File: node_modules/.deno/.deno.lock   <EMPTY>
       │ Size: 0 B
───────┴────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

$ echo >| package.json # Overwrite package.json with bad JSON.

$ deno run foo.ts # The package.json is being read.
error: malformed package.json EOF while parsing a value at line 2 column 0
```

## Use case

I use a package.json as a sort-of NPM-ism for some of my build scripts to refer to a project `name` and `version` (only two fields). I don't use any NPM modules but now when I execute `deno run` a node_modules directory is created. I think this is a new issue in either v1.31.0 or v1.31.1 as these node_modules directories just started appearing in my `git status`.

## Workaround

Don't use package.json for project name / version. I can understand if you don't want to support this use case but it makes me a bit sad as I'm not using any NPM dependencies so I don't think you need the NPM lockfile and package.json is so familiar for `name` and `version`. I am thinking I will move these fields into my deno.json but I'm unsure if that's wise. It looks like package.json is being parsed so it seems straightforward enough to test if dependencies are present or not without performance implications.

I see that arweave uses `crypto` module to generate wallet but it's causing errors. I assume it's because `generateKeyPair` function is not implemented in Deno's `crypto` module yet?

author: dogukanakkaya

title: node: crypto hash functions leak cryptoDigest resources in tests
body:
Reproduction example:

Deno version:
```
deno 1.31.2 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 11.0.226.19
typescript 4.9.4
```

mod.ts:
```
import PG from "npm:pg@8.10.0";

export async function test() {
  const client = new PG.Client({connectionString: Deno.env.get("DATABASE_URL")});
  await client.connect();
  await client.end();
}
```

mod.test.ts:
```
import { test } from "./mod.ts";

Deno.test("Test", async () => {
  await test();
});
```

Run:
```
deno test --allow-net --allow-env
```

Expected output:
```
ok | 1 passed | 0 failed (53ms)
```

Actual Output:
```
error: AssertionError: Test case is leaking 4 resources:

 - A "cryptoDigest" resource (rid 8196) was created during the test, but not cleaned up during the test. Close the resource before the end of the test.
 - A "cryptoDigest" resource (rid 8200) was created during the test, but not cleaned up during the test. Close the resource before the end of the test.
 - A "cryptoDigest" resource (rid 8203) was created during the test, but not cleaned up during the test. Close the resource before the end of the test.
 - A "cryptoDigest" resource (rid 8206) was created during the test, but not cleaned up during the test. Close the resource before the end of the test.

    at assert (ext:deno_web/00_infra.js:353:11)
    at resourceSanitizer (ext:cli/40_testing.js:417:5)
    at async Object.exitSanitizer [as fn] (ext:cli/40_testing.js:435:7)
    at async runTest (ext:cli/40_testing.js:840:5)
    at async runTests (ext:cli/40_testing.js:1098:20)

FAILED | 0 passed | 1 failed (258ms)
```

The cause of the issue appears to be the use of the node crypto library for hashing in the pg library, see for example https://github.com/brianc/node-postgres/blob/master/packages/pg/lib/sasl.js#L194 If any of the code paths that use these hash functions are called this error occurs.

title: feat: view test coverage in editor
body:
Deno
- can run tests.
- can generate test coverage reports.
- has an an editor integration (LSP).

It would be amazing if VSCode would **display the test coverage right inside the editor**.

The line coverage results are very useful when writing tests because they let me easily see which case are covered by tests and which ones are not. However, every time I add a test to cover some more code, I need to run `deno test` and `deno coverage` and then reload my browser window.

I am aware that there are third-party tools that should be able to consume the LCOV output and do this, but I would prefer if it would “just work” without any additional configuration.

title: (deno_node/http.ts) `ClientRequest::_createUrlStrFromOptions` does not match the logic in nodejs
body:
```
import { request } from 'node:http';

const options = {
    port: "8080",
    host: "192.168.0.2:8080",
    hostname: "192.168.0.2",
    method: "GET",
    headers: {},
    path: "/test"
};

const req = request(options, () => { console.log('success') });
req.on('error', console.error);
req.end();
```
It can be executed normally in nodejs, But such an error will be thrown in deno:
```
TypeError: Invalid URL: 'http://192.168.0.2:8080:8080/test'
    at getSerialization (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:74:11)
    at opUrlParse (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:65:10)
    at new URL (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:363:27)
    at new Request (ext:deno_fetch/23_request.js:323:25)
    at ext:deno_fetch/26_fetch.js:418:27
    at new Promise (<anonymous>)
    at fetch (ext:deno_fetch/26_fetch.js:414:18)
    at ClientRequest._final (ext:deno_node/http.ts:162:29)
```

I found that `http://192.168.0.2:8080:8080/test` was created by [ClientRequest._createUrlStrFromOptions](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/main/ext/node/polyfills/http.ts#:~:text=%60%24%7Bprotocol,path%7D%60)

title: 🐛 (WinOS) `Deno.isatty()` sometimes returns incorrect `false` result for 'CONOUT$'
body:
```shell
>deno --version
deno 1.31.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
v8 11.0.226.13
typescript 4.9.4
```

As seen here, `Deno.isatty()` incorrectly identifies an open 'CONOUT$' as *not* a TTY unless the file is opened with the `{ read: true }` option...

```shell
>deno eval "const f_w = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$', {write: true}); const f_rw = Deno.openSync('CONOUT$', {read: true, write: true}); Deno.writeAllSync(f_w, (new TextEncoder).encode('f_w writes to CONSOLE\n')); console.log({w_isTTY: Deno.isatty(f_w.rid), rw_isTTY: Deno.isatty(f_rw.rid)});"
f_w writes to CONSOLE
{ w_isTTY: false, rw_isTTY: true }
```

Notably, as seen above, use of the opened 'CONOUT$' pseudo-file only requires 'write' permission, and most users would not, in general, add the otherwise unneeded `read` permission.

In code review for the bug, I see that the [ops code](https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/f2e5e01832a40eb71be82f1b46304c56aa2e8dba/runtime/ops/tty.rs#L181-L196) uses [GetConsoleMode](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/console/getconsolemode) which does state that "The handle must have the `GENERIC_READ` access right.".

I didn't delve into the code for `Deno.open()` to determine what permissions the resultant file handle has when opened with `{ write: true }`, but I'd bet that `GENERIC_READ` is missing from the `f_w`-associated windows file handle and causes the issue.

If that's the case, then I'm not sure how this would be fixed. Maybe just a caveat to the documentation of `Deno.isatty()` that, for WinOS, 'CONIN$' and 'CONOUT$' must be opened with the `{read: true}` permission in order to be recognized as TTYs?
author: rivy

title: Avoid internally getting diagnostics for remote files with `--check` (not `--check=all`)
author: dsherret

title: 🐛(WinOS) `Deno.setRaw()` when changed for stabilization is no longer able to effect 'CONIN$'
body:
`Deno.setRaw(rid: number)`, [when changed to only be applicable to `Deno.stdin`](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/15797) and [stabilized](https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16399), is no longer able to be used for 'CONIN$' (and also, I expect, '/dev/tty').

Previously, when stdin could be closed or redirected and 'CONIN$' could still be used as console input and used in 'raw' mode. 'CONIN$' can still now be used for console input, but it's impossible to configure it to use 'raw' mode without using FFI (which is still unstable, as of 2023-03).

There was some discussion about applicability for PTYs in #15797, but no mentioned process for actual functionality beyond `Deno.stdin`.

If, as @lucacasonato said, `rid`'s are undesirable for stabilized functions, and that abstraction for file references is lost, it seems that this functionality would need to be added to the `Deno.Reader` or the `Deno.FsFile` interface. As a counter example, `Deno.isatty(rid: number)` is already stable and uses the `rid` abstraction. So, maybe a return of `Deno.setRaw(rid: number)` would be ok?

Thoughts?

author: nayeemrmn

title: Error formatting helpers for deno_core embedders
body:
We are building a JavaScript/WASM runtime using `deno_core` and few other extensions. Thank you for providing these awesome building block to the community! ❤️ 

I would like to provide similar error reporting for our users as Deno CLI does.

I found a helpful function `format_js_error`, but it's implemented in `deno_runtime` crate and unavailable to `deno_core` consumers. It depends on `fmt_errors` module that's in `deno_runtime` again.

What I would love to have: a crate that provides the same error-reporting helpers as used by `deno_runtime` & Deno CLI and which can be used in projects embedding only `deno_core`.

I am happy to contribute such a change myself if you could give me guidance on what approach would be acceptable to you.

Questions coming to my mind:
- First of all, what do you think about this idea? Is it in line with your project vision and architecture?
- Should we move the code to `deno_core` or a new crate? I feel that error formatting with colours is beyond the scope of deno core and would add extra dependencies that many consumers may not want or like. On the other hand, maybe we can use Rust features to allow people to opt into these new error-formatting helpers.
- If it should go to a new crate - what name to give it and where to put it in the workspace?

title: `node:readline` doesn't work with `Deno.stdin`: "Uncaught TypeError: input.on is not a function"
body:
```ts
// readline.ts

import * as readline from 'node:readline'

const rl = readline.createInterface({
	input: Deno.stdin,
	output: Deno.stdout,
})

rl.question(">", line => {
	console.log('Got line', line)
})
```

```bash
$ deno run readline.ts

error: Uncaught TypeError: input.on is not a function
```

title: `Deno.serve` with `--watch` throws `Address already in use (os error 98)`
body:
This was reported in https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/16699 but while the error is fixed on the canary when using `std/http/server.ts` it remains when using `Deno.serve`. Therefore, I am making this issue to separate concerns.

Repro:

```ts
await Deno.serve(() => new Response("Hello World!"));
```

```
deno run --allow-net --unstable --watch server.ts
```

After making file changes to `server.ts` and saving it shows:

```
Watcher File change detected! Restarting!
error: Uncaught AddrInUse: Address already in use (os error 98)
Watcher Process finished. Restarting on file change...
```

title: Supporting transitive mappings for npm: specifiers
author: guybedford

title: `workspace:` specifier not supported for `package.json` dependency
body:
I'm using Deno in a monorepo where I use `pnpm` as the package manager (through [rush](https://rushjs.io/)).

To refer to other packages in the same monorepo, I use the version specifier `"workspace:*"`, which is not supported by Deno's `package.json` functionality.

My [`package.json`](https://github.com/dossierhq/dossierhq/blob/main/libraries/deno-postgres/package.json) file looks like this:

```json
{
  "devDependencies": {
    "@dossierhq/core": "workspace:*"
  }
}
```

I get this warning when `deno task test`:

```
Warning Ignoring dependency '@dossierhq/core' in package.json because its version requirement failed to parse: Not implemented scheme: @dossierhq/core
```

It would be great if Deno could support `"workspace:*"` in `package.json` so I don't need to use import maps to support monorepo setups.

title: `deno run -A npm:aws-cdk destroy` throws `process is not defined`
body:
Hi. For the following AWS CDK app, I get an error when running `deno run -A npm:aws-cdk destroy --app "deno run -A app.ts"`:

`app.ts:`
```ts
import cdk from 'npm:aws-cdk-lib';

const app = new cdk.App();

new cdk.Stack(app, 'my-stack');
```

**Here's the output:**

```sh
$ deno run -A npm:aws-cdk destroy --app "deno run -A app.ts"
not implemented: ClientRequest.setTimeout

process is not defined
<hangs here indefinitely>
```

**Versions:**
```
$ deno --version
deno 1.31.2 (release, x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu)
v8 11.0.226.19
typescript 4.9.4

$ deno run -A npm:aws-cdk --version
2.69.0 (build 60a5b2a)
```

**Notes:**
- Broadly speaking, the synth and deploy commands work.
- The cdk deploy command is very noisy about `ClientRequest.setTimeout` not being implemented, but it deploys the CloudFormation stack nonetheless.
- This "process is not defined" error only happens during the cdk destroy command.

title: `alert`, `prompt`, `confirm` keyboard interaction inconsistent vs browsers
body:
The behavior of pressing Enter and Esc immediately after calling `alert`, `prompt`, and `confirm` is inconsistent between the browser and Deno. The inconsistent behavior is as follows: Environment→
Function↓ | Browser (Chrome/FF) | Deno -|-|- `alert(message?)` | • Enter: return `undefined`
• Esc: return `undefined` | • Enter: return `undefined`
• Esc: input the ESCAPE character [U+001B](https://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/001b/index.htm) ⚠️ `prompt(message?)` | • Enter: return `""`
• Esc: return `null` | • Enter: return `null` ⚠️
• Esc: input the ESCAPE character [U+001B](https://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/001b/index.htm) ⚠️ `prompt(message, defaultValue)` | • Enter: return `defaultValue`
• Delete all text, then Enter: return `""`
• Esc: return `null` | • Enter: return `defaultValue`
• Delete all text, then Enter: return `defaultValue` ⚠️
• Esc: input the ESCAPE character [U+001B](https://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/001b/index.htm) ⚠️ `confirm(message?)` | • Enter: return `true`
• Esc: return `false` | • Enter: return `false` (because result is `[y/N]`) ⚠️
• Esc: input the ESCAPE character [U+001B](https://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/001b/index.htm) ⚠️ It seems like it'd make more sense if the interaction mirrored the browser as closely as possible within a CLI. Enter meaning confirm/continue and Esc meaning reject/cancel is a well-established UX pattern in other contexts, too. It's true that `[y/N]` is also common in CLI contexts, but it doesn't seem obviously more common than `[Y/n]` (might even be less common).

title: Strange benchmark results
body:

title: `stdin.read` (and `stdin.readSync`) corrupt non-ASCII input on Windows
body:
`stdin.read` (and `stdin.readSync`) corrupt non-ASCII input on Windows.

To reproduce:

```js
const c = new Uint8Array(6);
Deno.stdin.read(c).then(() => console.log(c));
```

Then, enter a non-ASCII character. The resulting bytes will be corrupted on Windows.

Examples, with trailing LF/CRLF/null bytes truncated:

Input | Expected | Actual | Decoded as UTF-8
-|-|-|-
ÿ | `[195, 191]` | `[152]` | "�" (Invalid char)
Ā | `[196, 128]` | `[65]` | "A"
ā | `[196, 129]` | `[97]` | "a"
啊 | `[229, 149, 138]` | `[63]` | "?"
🦄 | `[240, 159, 166, 132]` | `[63, 63]` | "??"

Expected results are the UTF-8 bytes. Results on Linux are as expected.

author: rgripper

title: Deno needs a flag for Import not to error-check modules
body:
JavaScript in the browser does not check imports for environmental (ex: dom use in workers) errors. Deno, however, by default does. This creates problems for browser based code running in deno. We need a flag to skip this checking so that deno behaves like the browser.

Example: I have a large utilities file which includes both vanilla JS exports and dom exports. Many of the"apps" using this run in both workers and in the main thread even tho the utilities library uses the dom. This is possible because imports are not error-checked at load time, but naturally fail at run-time if a worker asks for a dom utility. 

Load-time checking would require duplicating the apps, one for workers, another for main thread browser apps.

I believe run-time failure is the standard, thus making this worker/main-thread sharing possible.

So when I run an app in deno that uses no dom utility, I still get:

```
error: Uncaught ReferenceError: document is not defined
       const can = document.createElement('canvas')
```
.. i.e. is  a load-time error rather than a run-time error.

I believe this may be common for devs wishing to run their modern JS apps in places other than the browser such as node, deno, bun and others.

Thus the need for an option for run-time errors rather than load-time pre-checking.
author: backspaces

title: Make additive lock file optional
body:
Deno 1.27.1 introduced additive lock files:
- Release notes: https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.27.1
- Pull Request: https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/16500

Before version 1.27.1, I could use the `--cached-only` functionality to ensure that, whenever I'm executing `deno run`, **only** the dependencies specified in the lock file are executed, giving me more control over when or how the lock file is updated, improving reproducibility.

I could limit lock file modifications to a single `deno cache --lock-write` and nothing else. Adding dependencies without "locking" them purposefully and then executing the project would result in a clear error.

I would love to have this functionality back. After version 1.27.1, I have no way to control this, and as a result:
- Reproducibility and security worsened, allowing the code on the repository to "work", but behave differently depending on when it was executed. Today I could commit an addition of a new dependency without locking it in, and the code would execute without issues, introducing the possibility of it behaving differently in the future by downloading a different version of the dependency.
- An automated build that checks that all the dependencies are present in the lock file becomes more complicated, needing to:
  - a) Check with git if lock file changed, and fail if so.
  - b) Have a separate DENO_DIR folder for each run, effectively disabling any possible caching.

It should be able to disable additive lock files. IMHO, it should actually be disabled by default or not even implemented in the toolchain, but the possibility to disable them by a flag would be an improvement.

PS: A change on how lock files are managed isn't something I would expect from a "patch" version. I can't find Deno's versioning specification, but many people would assume it's SEMVER because "it's three numbers separated by dots". Seeing Deno's versioning history, "minor" number bumps can mean breaking changes, but until today, didn't knew "patch" number bumps could mean breaking changes, too. Maybe some clarification over the versioning is worth the effort.

title: `deno compile` output can't dynamically import files that were not present at compilation time
body:
_(Creating this issue as a continuation of https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655)_

As @dsherret commented in #8655, Deno 1.31 added support for [statically analyzable dynamic imports](https://deno.com/blog/v1.31#deno-compile-works-with-statically-analyzable-dynamic-imports), and Deno 1.32 will add support for web workers (https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/090169cfbc6699486765b729d532b5b837210b12) and the ability to specify non-analyzable modules to include in the binary (https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/b64ec7926831896f4e43b685891111409de45e85).

These are good improvements to `deno compile`, but they don't cover many use-cases described in #8655.

Here's an incomplete list of things that `deno compile`'s output can't do:

* [Config files written in javascript/typescript](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655#issuecomment-1437444576) 
* [User-defined plugins](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655#issuecomment-1247057354)
* [Inspect user supplied values (`graphgen inspect path/to/your/custom/setup.ts`)](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655#issuecomment-1247057354)
* [Writing interpreters of other languages](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655#issuecomment-1173616850)
* [Running user javascript files created by a deno-based compiler](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655)
* [Filesystem-based routing](https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8655#issuecomment-833422361)

title: Refactor LSP to be less error prone around url mapping
body:
The lsp maps directory specifier like `file:///folder` to the proper `file:///folder/` specifier that works with the `Url` struct and it also has this concept of a `LspUrlMap` that I don't fully understand. This means there are "internal" representations of urls and "external" representations used with the client. The problem is it's very easy to use one where you should be using the other because they're the same type. I started on a refactor to make the internal representation a `LspInternalUrl(Url)`, but it became too big of a change and most of our code expects `Url`s. Ideally, the external specifiers would be a separate type then those would need to be passed through something else to make them `Url`s.

See https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18373 for an example of error prone usage (which is also error prone because the code was using a tuple).

title: Feature Request: Importing a `js` file could fall back to `ts` file in a `ts` file
body:
### Discussed in https://github.com/denoland/deno/discussions/18293
Originally posted by **Conaclos** March 20, 2023 Hi! One thing that prevents me from using _Deno_ for my TypeScript projects is the incompatibility of Deno with other tools. in particular _ESbuild_ and _TSC_. I use _ESbuild_ and _TSC_ to transpile my projects for npm and the browser. ESbuild and TSC do not change the extension of import paths. That's mean we have to use js extension to import a TypeScript file. It could be nice to implement a very simple resolution strategy for _Deno_: in a TypeScript file (file ending with ts extension): an import path ending with a _js_ extension is resolved to the same file, but ending with a ts extension if the _js_ file does not exist. ```ts // a.ts import * as b from "./b.js" // resolves to "./b.js" or "./b.ts" if "./b.js" does not exist ... ```

author: Cre3per

title: Add support for "Deno.upgradeHttpRaw" in "Deno.serveHttp" API
body:
Currently the unstable `Deno.upgradeHttpRaw` only works with unstable `Deno.serve` API. Because of that, our polyfill for `node:http` relies on the unstable `Deno.serve` API, which has many stability issues related to it.

If we were able to add support for `Deno.upgradeHttpRaw` with `Deno.serveHttp` API, we could replace implementation of `node:http` to use more stable `Deno.serveHttp` API - and in the future we might even remove Flash server completely and make `Deno.serve` a convenient wrapper around `Deno.serveHttp`.

The problem is that `hyper` that we use for `Deno.serveHttp` API, doesn't have a public API to do "raw upgrades" - ie. Hyper only has [`OnUpgrade`](https://docs.rs/hyper/latest/hyper/upgrade/struct.OnUpgrade.html), which returns a future that resolves after we return a proper response. 

However, in case of `node:http` API, we need a way to let the user code construct the response and return it at some point in the future:

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/09ddb35265f67a89383a8bd614224adb455d1e0c/ext/node/polyfills/http.ts#L755-L763

Docs for `upgrade` event:
https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#event-upgrade
author: bartlomieju

title: TypeScript: Set `useUnknownInCatchVariables` to `true` by default (like in vanilla TS)
body:
[According to the manual](https://deno.land/manual@v1.32.0/advanced/typescript/configuration#how-deno-uses-a-configuration-file) — by default — the compiler option [`strict`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#strict) is [set to `true`](https://deno.land/manual@v1.32.0/advanced/typescript/configuration#how-deno-uses-a-configuration-file:~:text=strict,-true), and the option [`useUnknownInCatchVariables`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#useUnknownInCatchVariables) is currently [set to `false`](https://deno.land/manual@v1.32.0/advanced/typescript/configuration#how-deno-uses-a-configuration-file:~:text=useUnknownInCatchVariables,-false).

That manual page also shows an example of [what an implied `tsconfig.json` looks like](https://deno.land/manual@v1.32.0/advanced/typescript/configuration#what-an-implied-tsconfigjson-looks-like) — which doesn't currently depict the state of this relationship, but that's not the focus of this issue...

The TSConfig reference includes this documentation:

> ## [`useUnknownInCatchVariables`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#useUnknownInCatchVariables)
> 
> In TypeScript 4.0, support was added to allow changing the type of the variable in a catch clause from `any` to `unknown`. Allowing for code like:
> 
> ```ts
> try {
>   // ...
> } catch (err) {
>   // We have to verify err is an
>   // error before using it as one.
>   if (err instanceof Error) {
>     console.log(err.message);
>   }
> }
> ```
>
> This pattern ensures that error handling code becomes more comprehensive because you cannot guarantee that the object being thrown is a Error subclass ahead of time. With the flag `useUnknownInCatchVariables` enabled, then you do not need the additional syntax (`: unknown`) nor a linter rule to try enforce this behavior.
> 
> ---
>
> **Default:**
> `true` if [`strict`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#strict), `false` otherwise.
> 
> Related:
> [`strict`](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig#strict)
> 
> Released:
> [4.4](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/release-notes/typescript-4-4.html)

Here are two links to the TS playground comparing the two behaviors (based on the code above):

- [enabled (`true`)](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play?useUnknownInCatchVariables=true#-jFTD8ZQNMjcTBgKgALgct3gr32hwmx1OjQudEYlCcEDyABUAMqhcLcOgQHjNCDUZrABimCheGCuGBAA)
- [disabled (`false`)](https://www.typescriptlang.org/play?useUnknownInCatchVariables=false#-jFTD8ZQNMjcTBgKgALgct3gr32hwmx1OjQudEYlCcEDyABUAMoQAAmSDoKF0mBi4UiPGaEGozWADFMFGhAAK2gpuKoVAAPFYAB2A5lQCxafgE7R5BgAhF4YK4YEA)

As the documentation describes: this feature is enabled by default when `strict` is enabled, but **that's not currently the case in Deno** — and, as the code in the playground links demonstrate, this feature is useful in providing better type safety in `catch` blocks — helping prevent further potential exceptions.

I would like to see Deno set this compiler option to `true` by default, just like vanilla TS does — and have better type safety in `catch` blocks.

title: `deno upgrade` permission check should check cliOptionOutput
body:
### Actual
```sh
$ deno upgrade --version 1.30.3 --output ./deno_1_30_3
error: You don't have write permission to /usr/bin/deno because it's owned by root.
Consider updating deno through your package manager if its installed from it.
Otherwise run `deno upgrade` as root.
```

Workaround
```shj
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.30.3 --output ./deno_1_30_3
Downloading https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v1.30.3/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
...
```

### Expected
deno upgrade should permission check `./deno_1_30_3` instead of `/usr/bin/deno`

### Related
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18405
https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/18406

title: Using Deno.core in embedded Deno
body:
Patch abd9610530 removed `Deno.core` and if I try to use `Deno.core.opAsync("whatever")` in my embedded Deno runtime now, I get the error that `Deno.core` is undefined.

I've tried to use `Deno[Deno.internal].core.opAsync` instead, since I saw that used in some places in Deno's codebase. While this is not undefined, it returns the following runtime error:

```
Uncaught TypeError: ops[name] is not a function
```

For an embedded Deno runtime, how is one supposed to use the `Deno.core` functionality?  My main goal is just the `opAsync` calls in this specific scenario.

A bit of additional context: The runtime constructed is through `MainWorker::bootstrap_from_options`. There is no startup snapshot (thought that it might be necessary?). The code is executed through `execute_main_module` and then `run_event_loop`. I can provide the full code if necessary.

title: fetch failure in a dynamically moduled module
body:
Background: I have a models/ dir in my repo's top level containing 23 models/modules. 
I want to import them into deno to run 500 steps then report the model state. 
This would replace the current test as a far simpler one, a Good Thing!
The test program first gets the names of all the modules then dynamically imports them, one at a time, for testing.

This works brilliantly, except when one of the models uses fetch for data that it needs.
Here is a very simplified test of this approach, run in the repo top level, after a deno upgrade

```
// deno run -A models/denotest.js
const modelsDir = 'models/'

console.log('cwd', Deno.cwd())
Deno.chdir(modelsDir)
console.log('cwd', Deno.cwd())

// const model = await import('./AntsModel.js') // works, no use of fetch
const model = await import('./CountiesModel.js') // fails in fetch
console.log(model)
```

It produces:
```
// deno run -A models/denotest.js
cwd /Users/owen/Dropbox/src/agentscript
cwd /Users/owen/Dropbox/src/agentscript/models
error: Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Invalid URL: './data/nmcounties.json'
const counties = await fetch('./data/nmcounties.json').then(resp => resp.json())
                       ^
    at getSerialization (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:74:11)
    at opUrlParse (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:65:10)
    at new URL (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:368:27)
    at new Request (ext:deno_fetch/23_request.js:323:25)
    at ext:deno_fetch/26_fetch.js:418:27
    at new Promise (<anonymous>)
    at fetch (ext:deno_fetch/26_fetch.js:414:18)
    at file:///Users/owen/Dropbox/src/agentscript
```

.. the ./data/nmcounties.json file is in the models/ dir and is correctly used elsewhere.

This also gets the same error: simply importing the model within the models/ dir:
```
// deno run -A models/denotest.js
// import model from './AntsModel.js' // works
import model from './CountiesModel.js' // fails
console.log(model)
error: Uncaught TypeError: Invalid URL: './data/nmcounties.json'
const counties = await fetch('./data/nmcounties.json').then(resp => resp.json())
                       ^
    at getSerialization (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:74:11)

    at new URL (ext:deno_url/00_url.js:368:27)

    at new Promise (<anonymous>)
    at fetch (ext:deno_fetch/26_fetch.js:414:18)
    at file:///Users/owen/Dropbox/src/agentscript/models/CountiesModel.js:5:24

```

title: Errors or OOM when dealing with big files
body:
When dealing with multigigs files Deno fail to garbage collect some memory (this is pure speculation from my side) and fail both writing and reading till EOF.

I've setup'ed a repro repo [here](https://github.com/nitroin/deno-oom-repro).

## Steps to repro

### Write a dump file

Create a dummy large dump file (usually problems arise when dealing with multigigs files).

```bash
> deno task write-dump
```

And let Deno create a dummy file under `./output` folder. Stop with `CTRL+C | CMD+C` when the file is 3 GB or more.

### Try to read the created file

Lunch the reader task to repro OOM.

```bash
> deno task read-dump
```

It usually take some minutes depending on the hardware/configuration used, but the process should exit with an error:

```
error: Uncaught (in promise) RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded
      yield decoder.decode(concat(...chunks));
```

## Notes

Also `Deno.writeText` can leak some memory, so an OOM can occur also when writing the dump file if you're like enough. You can monitor the memory usage with top or htop an see the memory never being released.

title: [dts] node builtin should have correct type for deno version > 1.30.3
body:
### Background
According to release log
- deno version <= 1.30.3 use `https://deno.land/std@XXX/node/foobar.ts` XXX <= 0.177.0, which have correct type
- deno version > 1.30.3 use `node:foobar`, which have wrong type

### Effected Range
1.30.3 < deno version <= 1.32.1

### Before migration

```ts
import { readFile } from "https://deno.land/std@0.177.0/node/fs/promises.ts";

console.log(readFile);
```

Hover on `readFile` got correct type indication
```
(alias) const readFile: ((path: string | URL, opt: TextOptionsArgument) => Promise<string>) & ((path: string | URL, opt?: BinaryOptionsArgument | undefined) => Promise<...>) & ((path: string | URL, opt?: FileOptionsArgument | undefined) => Promise<...>)
import readFile
```

### After migration: Actual
```ts
import { readFile } from "node:fs/promises";

console.log(readFile);
```

Hover on `readFile` got **WRONG** type indication
```
import readFile
```

### After migration: Expected
```ts
import { readFile } from "node:fs/promises";

console.log(readFile);
```

Hover on `readFile` got correct type indication
```
(alias) const readFile: ((path: string | URL, opt: TextOptionsArgument) => Promise<string>) & ((path: string | URL, opt?: BinaryOptionsArgument | undefined) => Promise<...>) & ((path: string | URL, opt?: FileOptionsArgument | undefined) => Promise<...>)
import readFile
```

### Links
https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/releases/tag/0.177.0
https://github.com/denoland/deno_std/releases/tag/0.178.0
https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.30.3
https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.31.0

title: [feat] `deno upgrade` should have download cache
body:
### Actual
`deno upgrade` download tarball everytime version change.

```
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.32.1
...Downloading...
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.30.3
...Downloading...
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.32.1
...Downloading...
```

### Expected
`deno upgrade` download tarball, use cache when possible.

```
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.32.1
...Downloading to cache...
...Copy to dest...
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.30.3
...Downloading to cache...
...Copy to dest...
$ sudo deno upgrade --version 1.32.1
```

author: loynoir

title: startTls on server-side
body:
I'm trying to make an ESTMP server, that supports the `STARTTLS` command. However, this requires me to upgrade the connection, both on the client and the server to `TLS`. On the client, this is done without too much trouble, via the `Deno.startTls` function. However, calling this upon a server to client connection causes this error in the client, when the client tries to perform a TLS handshake:

```
Received a ClientHello handshake message while expecting [ServerHello, HelloRetryRequest]
Sending fatal alert UnexpectedMessage
```

This leads me to believe that `Deno.startTls` only performs a midway client-side TLS handshake. I would love it if an equivalent server-side `startTls` function was implemented, because right now I'm forced to reimplement the whole TLS protocol, or not include STARTTLS as a command in my SMTP server, none of which are real options.

title: `deno coverage` default exclude pattern should be better
body:
### Brief
By default, `deno coverage` contains `XXX.test.mts` and `npm:XXX`.


### Reproduce
```sh
$ deno test --coverage=cov_profile path/to/source/
```

test files are in pattern `XXX.test.mts`

### Actual
- `deno coverage` default exclude pattern is not superset of `deno test` default autoscan pattern.
- `deno coverage` contains `npm:XXX`

```sh
$ deno coverage build/cov_profile --lcov | grep SF:
SF:/home/vscode/.cache/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/XXX
SF:/path/to/source/foo.mts
SF:/path/to/source/foo.test.mts
SF:/path/to/source/bar.mts
SF:/path/to/source/bar.test.mts
```

### Expected
- `deno coverage` default exclude pattern is superset of `deno test` default autoscan pattern
- `deno coverage` not contains `npm:XXX`

```sh
$ deno coverage build/cov_profile --lcov --exclude='\\.test\\.'  --exclude=/npm/| grep SF:
SF:/path/to/source/foo.mts
SF:/path/to/source/bar.mts
```

### Additional
`deno coverage` may have broken pipe.

```sh
$ deno coverage build/cov_profile --lcov | grep -qF /npm/
error: Broken pipe (os error 32)
```

title: `deno upgrade --output`: No such file or directory (os error 2)
body:
deno 1.32.1+101abf3:
```
cd /tmp
mkdir foo
deno upgrade --version 1.11.5 --output foo/deno
error: No such file or directory (os error 2)
```

deno 1.32.1:
```
deno upgrade --version 1.11.5 --output foo/deno
Downloading https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/download/v1.11.5/deno-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.zip
Deno is upgrading to version 1.11.5
Archive:  /tmp/.tmp2QmbJs/deno.zip
  inflating: deno                    
Upgraded successfully
Release notes: https://github.com/denoland/deno/releases/tag/v1.11.5
Blog post: https://deno.com/blog/v1.11
```
Issue seems to be that Deno is calling `std::fs::metadata` on the output exe instead of the current exe since https://github.com/denoland/deno/commit/0742ea1170239b7d010e219b3349b2f9edaaefe5, but that file has not been made yet at that point.

https://github.com/denoland/deno/blob/8c051dbd1a075ad3c228f78b29b13f0e455972a7/cli/tools/upgrade.rs#L270

title: `Deno.consoleSize` + full-width text/ansi escape codes is bug-prone with no official way of mitigating
body:
Even in "monospace" text, many characters take up double the space, and many take up no space at all. Comparing `Deno.consoleSize().columns` to string length, or even using more accurate Unicode-aware forms of measuring, such as `[...str].length` or `[...new Intl.Segmenter('en', { granularity: 'grapheme' }).segment(str)].length`, is highly bug-prone:

```js
'人'.length // 1, but takes up 2 character width
'\x1b[36mcyan\x1b[1m'.length // 13, but only 4 printable characters
'á'.normalize('NFD').length // 2, but only 1 grapheme
'🦄'.length // 2, which is "correct" but for the wrong reason (due to UTF-16 code units)
'\u200c'.length // 1, should be 0 because it's a zero-width character
```

In order for `Deno.consoleSize` to be reliable* for general-purpose use, some method of getting the width of a string also needs to be exposed. Possible candidates:

1. `ext/console/02_console.js > getStringWidth`
2. `ext/node/polyfills/internal/util/inspect.mjs > getStringWidth`, which is a thin wrapper around 1
3. The https://github.com/unicode-rs/unicode-width crate (which is a dependency of `rustyline`)

Of these, `unicode-width` looks to be by far the most comprehensive.

\* Even once this is implemented there will be edge cases, such as differences in fonts, codepoints that don't have a fixed width, etc. but it should at least be reliable for the majority of real-world use cases.

author: ry

title: Disable package.json in deno.json
body:
To put it simply, it is problematic to maintain projects with both native Deno and native Node support, when Deno is constantly referencing `package.json`, which is entirely unnecessary.

I suggest adding an option in `deno.json` to disable `package.json`, or even better, any Node compatibility, like:
```json
{
	"disableNode": true
}
```

Or maybe disable `package.json` or Node support when `deno.json` is discovered, and only enabled when an option is present in `deno.json`, like:
```json
{
	"enableNode": true
}
```

Personally, the latter "enable on demand" approach makes much more sense. What's the point of supporting Node when Deno is supported **natively** already?

Related:
* #17916
* #17930
