author: alexgleason

title: Embedder's guide to snapshotting
body:
_Continuing the discussion from https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18271#discussion_r1150218083._

It would be great to have a guide for embedders showing how to set up their build to create a snapshot of all JS files bundled in their custom runtime to get faster startup times.

I can probably figure this out by reading the source code of Deno core & cli, but it would be much nicer if there were a guide. Having said that, both the guide and snapshotting are pretty much "nice to have" for us right now.

Another possible solution is to add an example to `deno_core` to show this in actions.

Quoting from https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/18271#discussion_r1150259267:
> there's no official guide - but long story short - you need to define `extension!` in your `build.rs` script when creating a snapshot and include all the files you want. Looking at `runtime/build.rs` and `cli/build.rs` (the latter creates a snapshot from existing snapshot) is the fastest way to see it in action. If you could open an issue about it we can look into adding some examples to `deno_core`.

author: rotu

title: Feature: Give `deno install` parity with `deno compile`'s new dynamic import handling.
body:
Deno 1.31 and 1.32 add support for `deno compile` to analyze dynamic imports and include them in the built binary. Those imports do not require the `--allow-net` or `--allow-read` permission when running the binary.
<https://deno.com/blog/v1.32#deno-compile-support-for-web-workers-and-dynamic-import>

But no such support was added to `install`, so if I try to `deno install` the same codebase, I have quite different functionality.

My preference would be for `deno compile`, `deno install`, and `deno run` to operate similarly w.r.t. statically-analyzable dynamic imports. 

I believe #17697 will will likely bring `deno run` into alignment, but I didn't see a similar ticket for `deno install`, so this is that ticket.  

My preference would be for `deno install` to do the same kind of bundling and/or caching that `deno compile` is doing.  i.e.:

 * `deno install` should analyze dynamic imports, and install them into the(/some) cache.
 * Running a `deno install`ed program shouldn't need extra disk permissions to load those cached imports.
 * It **definitely** shouldn't reach out to the network and re-load those resources every time an application runs. 

---

## Related Issues ##

 * My use case for wanting this: https://github.com/NfNitLoop/deno-embedder/issues/6
 * https://github.com/denoland/deno/issues/8109 -- I believe that is a different request than this one. I'm not sure permissions requirements should change between application executions depending on the state of the cache. I'd rather `deno install` just "do the thing I mean" and remove permissions from the equation.
author: NfNitLoop

title: `deno check` cache causes wrong results
body:
Summary
----------

The cache used by `deno check` can lead to incorrect results reported.  Running `deno check app.ts` a second time will just return the previous result, even though the types of local modules that `app.ts` depends on have been corrected.

Reproduction
---------------

Given the files:

```ts
// File: app.ts
import { greet } from "./_greet.ts"

greet("world")
```

and:

```ts
// File: _greet.ts
export function greet(name: number) {
    console.log(`Hello, ${name}!`)
}
```

`deno check` gives the expected error:

```
Check file:///C:/Users/Cody/code/deno/check-cache-bug/app.ts
error: TS2345 [ERROR]: Argument of type 'string' is not assignable to parameter of type 'number'.
greet("world")
      ~~~~~~~
    at file:///C:/Users/Cody/code/deno/check-cache-bug/app.ts:4:7
```

If we fix `_greet.ts` to take a `name: string`, `deno check` should now pass, but we still get the same error. 

You can work around this by running `deno check --reload app.ts`, but there are two big problems with this:

First: For large projects, this can result in re-downloading quite a large dependency tree with every run of `deno check --reload`.

But more importantly, you have to first *know* that you're getting the wrong results to know to go look for `--reload`.  If you run into the caching problem from the other direction (i.e.: you cached a "pass", which should now be failing), `deno check` is giving you a dangerous result. You think your build is good and you push it out, only to run into runtime errors that TypeScript should've saved you from.

Possible fixes?
---------------

Maybe when `deno check` caches its results, it can save the graph of local dependencies checked. Then, the next `deno check` will *only* use the cache if *none* of those local dependencies have changed.
author: NfNitLoop

title: `deno fmt` does not format extension-less deno scripts
body:
Now that the `--ext` flag has been added to `deno run` in https://github.com/denoland/deno/pull/17172, `deno fmt` should be able to  format files with (or without) any extension.

```
% deno --version
deno 1.32.1 (release, aarch64-apple-darwin)
v8 11.2.214.9
typescript 5.0.2

% cat <<'END' | tee script script.foo script.ts >/dev/null
#!/usr/bin/env -S deno run --ext=ts

console.log(    "hello"    );
END

% deno fmt script.ts
/Users/uasi/tmp/script.ts
Checked 1 file

# 😞 
% deno fmt script script.foo
error: No target files found.

# 😞 
% deno fmt --ext=ts script script.foo
error: No target files found.
```

title: Running 'deno install' command as sub process only pipes though stderr
body:
Hello!!

### **Description Of Issue**
When creating a **_piped_** sub-process in deno and the commands to run is the command `deno uninstall <name>`, the output of the command is only piped through the `stderr` reader and writer instead of the `stdout` reader and writer.

Everything works and the process runs as expected, it just pipes to the wrong reader and writer.

> **Note** I also tried running the script from the **windows terminal** and I get the same behavior and output as if I run it in debug mode in **VSCode**.

### **Steps To Reproduce**

1. Create a simple deno app to run the attached script.
2. Create a simple hello world script to use for running the `deno install` command
    > **Note** I just create a separate demo hello world script to point to using a file path in the command for testing purposes.
    > This is to make sure that you are running the command with no errors and to observe that the wrong routing is being used anyway.
3. Add the `RunnerService` class/code to the project.
    > **Note** Sample code 👉🏻 [RunnerService.zip](https://github.com/denoland/deno/files/11122894/RunnerService.zip)
4. Create an instance of type `RunnerService`.
5. Execute the `RunnerService.run()` function with the command array input of `["deno", "install",  "-A", "-f", "-n", "<file path to hello world main>"]`.
6. Observe that the `RunnerService.pipeThroughStdErr()` function is being invoked instead of the `RunnerService.pipeThroughStdOut()` function, even though the command runs successfully with no errors.

When I run the command in the CLI, I do not have any issues at all with this same command running. 
I run the same command in the CLI and it outputs with no issues and outputs the same output as when I am running the command as a sub process.  This has what has brought me to believe that the sub process is not behaving correctly.

### **System Info:**
Deno Version:
- deno 1.32.1 (release, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc)
- v8 11.2.214.9
- typescript 5.0.2
OS: Windows (Version 22H2 - OS Build 22621.1413)

If you have anymore questions or need anymore info from me, let me know!!

Cheers!!

title: `deno repl` hangs with excessive memory usage running from certain CWDs
body:
OS: Windows 10 Version 10.0.19045.2728

From `deno 1.32.1+7722014` and up, running `deno repl` or just `deno` from certain directories causes the deno process to hang with no repl prompt and with excessive memory usage.

The bug is reproducible if running deno from `C:\`, `C:\Windows`, `C:\Users`, `C:\Users\<current_user>`, `C:\Windows\System32`, `C:\Windows\SysWow64`. At the same time runinng `deno repl` from `C:\Users\<current_user>\Desktop` or `C:\Windows\System32\wbem` proceeds as normal. These are the directories that I've tested which reproduced the bug 100% of the times. Other directories and drives seems unaffected.

The last unaffected canary version with available binaries to download is `deno 1.32.1+e888c3f`

Edit:
Just checked with the 1.32.2 release. It's still affected.

Edit2:
Checked 1.32.2 Linux under Windows WSL, it hangs when executing with the root dir (`/`) as the current directory.

title: node: missing `node_modules/.bin` directory causes npm scripts to fail
body:
During our installation process we don't create a `node_modules/.bin` folder. That causes existing scripts in `package.json` to fail when referencing other package managers (`npm`, `yarn`, `pnpm`,...). Typically the `node_modules/.bin` folder contains symlinks to the binary path of packages which have the `"bin"` field in their `package.json` file.

Steps to reproduce:

1. `mkdir deno-bin && cd deno-bin`
2. `echo '{"name":"foo","scripts":{"test": "npm run other", "other": "mh-yes"},"dependencies": {"@marvinh/yes": "1.0.1"}}' >> package.json`
3. `deno task test`

Error:

```sh
Warning Currently only basic package.json `scripts` are supported. Programs like `rimraf` or `cross-env` will not work correctly. This will be fixed in the upcoming release.
Task test npm run other

> other
> mh-yes

sh: mh-yes: command not found
```

title: `deno install` fails to run program
body:
I used this command to generate a bin script:

```
deno install -A --root /usr/local --name my-program --force $PWD/cli/main.ts
✅ Successfully installed my-program
/usr/local/bin/my-program
```

If I then run it, I get this error:

```
error: Uncaught ReferenceError: __DENO_NODE_GLOBAL_THIS_1680362762__ is not defined
    at file:///Users/me/Library/Caches/deno/npm/registry.npmjs.org/ts-pattern/4.2.2/dist/index.module.js:1:18
```

After looking into this... the install command adds `--no-config`.

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --allow-all --no-config 'file:///path/cli/main.ts' "$@"
```

If I remove `--no-config` and run `my-program` (from any directory) it works. Example:

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --allow-all  'file:///path/cli/main.ts' "$@"
```

I tried this and it fails:

```
#!/bin/sh
# generated by deno install
exec deno run --allow-all --no-lock 'file:///path/cli/main.ts' "$@"
```

I don't understand how the auto-discovery stuff works. As I mentioned, after removing `--no-config` I can run this program from any directory (not just the directory where the `deno.jsonc` and `deno.lock` related to the cli/main.ts script are found).
